refined_solution:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following special-case theorem. Let \(E\) be a finite set, let \(M\) be a loopless simple matroid on \(E\) of rank \(r\le 3\), and assume that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, meaning that for all flats \(A,B\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(A)+\operatorname{rk}(B)
=
\operatorname{rk}(A\cap B)+\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup B)).
\]
Assume also that \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), i.e. an inclusion-reversing bijection with \((F^\perp)^\perp=F\). Let \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) denote the projective tropical linear space of \(M\), and let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) denote the poset, ordered by inclusion, of projective tropical linear spaces \(L(N)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) arising from valuated matroid quotients \(N\) of \(M\). For a flat \(F\), put
\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
where \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero matroid of loops on \(F\), so that \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\). Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\).

This is a relaxation of the original question: the original question asks about an arbitrary matroid with a flat-lattice polarity, while the theorem proved here assumes \(M\) is finite, loopless, simple, modular, and of rank at most \(3\).

## Proof

Let \(r=\operatorname{rk}(M)\). Since \(M\) is simple, we identify atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) with elements of \(E\). Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite graded lattice of rank \(r\), an order-reversing involution sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats. Thus, when \(r\ge 2\), it bijects hyperplanes with atoms.

We use three standard facts.

1. **Quotients and inclusion.** If \(v,w\) are valuated matroids on \(E\), then
   \[
   L(v)\subseteq L(w)
   \]
   precisely when \(v\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(w\). In particular, elements of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) are exactly valuated quotients of \(M\), ordered by inclusion of their tropical linear spaces.

2. **Uniqueness of Plücker vectors.** A projective tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to adding a global scalar.

3. **Codimension-one identification.** For a simple modular matroid \(M\), codimension-one valuated quotients of \(M\) are encoded by weights \(w_H\) on hyperplanes \(H\in\mathcal H(M)\). Explicitly, the associated rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid has Plücker coordinates
   \[
   \nu(I)=
   \begin{cases}
   w_{\operatorname{cl}(I)}, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
   \infty, & I\text{ dependent}.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   Under the polarity \(H\mapsto H^\perp\), this codimension-one relative Dressian is identified with \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

Thus, for \(q=(q_e)_{e\in E}\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define a codimension-one tropical subspace \(\alpha(q)\) by hyperplane weights
\[
\boxed{\alpha(q)_H:=q_{H^\perp}}.
\]
Equivalently, its rank \(r-1\) Plücker vector is
\[
\nu_q(I)=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & I\text{ dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
The inverse map sends a codimension-one subspace with hyperplane weights \(w_H\) to the point \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) with
\[
q_e=w_{e^\perp}.
\]
This is well-defined because tropical linear spaces determine their valuated Plücker vectors up to global scalar.

We next prove the key incidence symmetry. Assume \(2\le r\le 3\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be a basis of \(M\). Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Then \(B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again a basis, and
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})=b_i^\perp.
\]
For \(r=2\), this is immediate: if \(B=\{a,b\}\), then \(a^*=b^\perp\), \(b^*=a^\perp\), and these are distinct atoms.

For \(r=3\), write \(B=\{a,b,c\}\). Then
\[
a^*=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)^\perp,\quad
b^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,c)^\perp,\quad
c^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,b)^\perp.
\]
Since \(b^*,c^*\le a^\perp\) and \(b^*\ne c^*\), their closure is \(a^\perp\). Similarly,
\[
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,c^*)=b^\perp,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,b^*)=c^\perp.
\]
Also \(a^*\not\le a^\perp\), because otherwise applying \(\perp\) would give \(a\le \operatorname{cl}(b,c)\), contradicting that \(a,b,c\) is a basis. Hence \(a^*,b^*,c^*\) is a basis.

Now let \(p,q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). We claim
\[
\boxed{p\in \alpha(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad q\in \alpha(p).}
\]
By the tropical linear space equations for the rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid \(\alpha(q)\), the condition \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is that for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+\nu_q(S\setminus\{i\})\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(S=B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) is a basis, this becomes
\[
\boxed{
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ is attained at least twice.}
}
\]
If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)=r-1\), then \(S\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The only non-automatically-\(\infty\) terms occur for \(i\in C\), and they all have the same added quantity \(q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\). Hence the condition reduces to the circuit condition for \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\), so the condition is automatic.

Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the displayed basis equations for all bases \(B\). But the basis equation for \(B\),
\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}),
\]
is exactly the basis equation for \(B^*\) with \(p\) and \(q\) interchanged, because \((b_i^*)^*=b_i\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on bases, the equations for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) and \(q\in\alpha(p)\) are identical. Thus incidence is symmetric.

We now define \(\Phi\). If \(r=0\), the Dressian has only one element and we take \(\Phi=\mathrm{id}\). If \(r=1\), then \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) consists only of the bottom and top elements, and we swap them.

Assume now \(2\le r\le 3\). Let \(\hat 0\) be the rank-zero tropical linear space and \(\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Define
\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1,\qquad \Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0.
\]
For a point \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), viewed as a rank-one element of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), set
\[
\Phi(p)=\alpha(p).
\]
For a codimension-one tropical subspace \(L\), set
\[
\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L).
\]
When \(r=2\), the rank-one and codimension-one cases coincide, and the two definitions agree because \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\).

The map \(\Phi\) is involutive by construction: \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\), \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\), and \(\hat 0,\hat 1\) are swapped.

It remains to prove that \(\Phi\) reverses inclusion. Let \(X\subseteq Y\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). Equal-rank inclusions are equal, because inclusion corresponds to valuated quotient and a same-rank quotient has the same Plücker vector up to scalar. The bottom/top cases are automatic. In rank \(3\), the only nontrivial strict inclusion is a point \(p\) contained in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then
\[
p\subseteq L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\in\alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in\alpha(p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]
where the middle equivalence is the incidence symmetry proved above. Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally we verify that \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The flats \(\varnothing\) and \(E\) map to \(\hat 1\) and \(\hat 0\), and \(\Phi\) swaps them, matching \(\varnothing^\perp=E\) and \(E^\perp=\varnothing\).

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane. Then \(X_H\) is a point whose rank-one coordinates may be normalized as
\[
q^H_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in H,\\
0, & e\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
For any hyperplane \(K\),
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=q^H_{K^\perp}.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(q^H)_K=\infty\) exactly when \(K^\perp\le H\), equivalently when \(H^\perp\le K\). These are precisely the hyperplane weights of the codimension-one flat tropical subspace \(X_{H^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

Similarly, let \(a\) be an atom. The codimension-one subspace \(X_a\) has hyperplane weights
\[
w^a_K=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & a\le K,\\
0, & a\nleq K.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore \(\alpha^{-1}(X_a)\) has point coordinates
\[
q_e=w^a_{e^\perp}.
\]
So \(q_e=\infty\) exactly when \(a\le e^\perp\), equivalently when \(e\le a^\perp\). This is exactly the point \(X_{a^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]
Since, for \(r\le 3\), every proper nonempty flat is either an atom or a hyperplane, this proves
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\). The theorem follows.

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\), assume that its lattice of flats \(L(M)\) is modular, and assume that \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
satisfying \((F^\perp)^\perp=F\). Let \(0\le k\le r\). In this write-up, a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) means a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), considered up to adding one global real constant to all finite values, satisfying the tropical Plücker relations and the following circuit-incidence relations: for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), with the convention that \(p_T=\infty\) unless \(T\) is a \(k\)-subset, the minimum of
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

The theorem proved here is the following partial result. First, every such \(p\) satisfies \(p_I=\infty\) for every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset \(I\), and \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) for every \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subset \(I\). Write \(\bar p(F)\) for this common value when \(F\) is a rank-\(k\) flat, and set \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) for flats of rank other than \(k\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids, and
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
projectively, in fact exactly for any fixed representative.

Moreover, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of the matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\), i.e. the rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) valuated matroid with value \(0\) on bases of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) and value \(\infty\) elsewhere. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
projectively, indeed exactly with the above \(0/\infty\) representatives.

This is a relaxation of the original problem. It proves a set-level rank-reversing involution on valuated quotients of \(M\), and it proves compatibility with the embedded flat subposet. It does not prove that this involution reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear subspaces, and therefore it does not prove the existence of an order-reversing involution on the whole relative Dressian poset.

## Proof

### 1. Conventions and elementary modular facts

All minima below are interpreted in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A finite family has its minimum “attained at least twice” if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs for at least two indices. Thus a singleton family satisfies this condition exactly when its only entry is \(\infty\).

For a function \(p\) defined on \(k\)-subsets, we extend notation by setting \(p_T=\infty\) for every subset \(T\subseteq E\) with \(|T|\ne k\).

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution of the finite ranked lattice \(L(M)\), it satisfies
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
Indeed, a maximal chain from \(\hat 0\) to \(F\) is carried to a maximal chain from \(F^\perp\) to \(\hat 1\).

We shall use the following elementary consequence of modularity.

**Lemma 1.1: line-closed sets are flats.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(S\) is a set of atoms of \(L(N)\) with the property that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the rank-two flat \(a\vee b\) also lies in \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat.

**Proof.** Let \(x\) be an atom in the closure of \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimal such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Suppose \(m\ge2\), and put
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad L=a_m\vee x.
\]
By minimality, \(x\not\le P\) and \(a_m\not\le P\). Also \(x\le P\vee a_m\), so \(P\vee L=P\vee a_m\). Since \(L\) has rank \(2\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
\operatorname{rk}P+\operatorname{rk}L-\operatorname{rk}(P\vee L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(P\wedge L\) is an atom \(b\). By induction on \(m\), \(b\in S\). Since \(b,a_m\in S\) and \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is closed. \(\square\)

We shall also use the standard Bergman criterion for rank-one quotients.

**Lemma 1.2: tropical point criterion.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite matroid on a set of atoms \(A(N)\), and let \(w:A(N)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real number \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N):w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** If some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat, then there is \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(A_\tau)\). Hence there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). Then \(w_e<\tau\le w_x\) for all \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\), so \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\).

Conversely, if some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\), choose \(\tau\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), but \(e\notin A_\tau\) and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. \(\square\)

### 2. Valuated quotients and flat-constancy

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \((k-2)\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\left(
p_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), these relations are vacuous.

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfying the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

We first prove the essential flat-constancy property.

**Lemma 2.1: flat-constancy.**  
Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Then:

1. If \(I\subseteq E\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, then \(p_I=\infty\).
2. If \(I,I'\subseteq E\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I'),
   \]
   then \(p_I=p_{I'}\).

**Proof.** The case \(k=0\) is trivial, so assume \(k\ge1\).

First let \(I\) be an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then every element of \(C\setminus\{c\}\) already lies in \(A\), so all terms in
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{x\}}:x\in C\}
\]
are \(\infty\) except possibly the term \(p_{A\cup\{c\}}=p_I\). The circuit-incidence relation therefore forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same \(M\)-closure. Since \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\), because otherwise \(C\subseteq A\cup\{a\}=I\), contradicting independence of \(I\). Therefore the only possibly finite terms in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) are
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},\qquad p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
The condition that the minimum have no unique finite minimizer implies
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}}=p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]

Finally, any two bases of the restriction \(M|F\) of a matroid to a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) are connected by a sequence of single basis exchanges. Applying the adjacent-basis argument along such a sequence shows that \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\). \(\square\)

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\). For flats \(F\) with \(\operatorname{rk}F\ne k\), set
\[
\bar p(F):=\infty.
\]

### 3. The definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Define \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), then \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and the expression is meaningful.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, since \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), there is a \(k\)-subset \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By Lemma 2.1, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose a basis \(J\) of the rank-\((r-k)\) flat \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)=\bar p(F)=p_I<\infty.
\]

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

### 4. A rank-three edge lemma

We first prove the rank-three local fact needed later.

**Lemma 4.1: rank-three edge lemma.**  
Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), meaning that the minimum of \(w\) on every circuit of \(P\) has no unique finite minimizer. Extend \(w\) to non-atoms by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) if \(F\) is not an atom. For any four hyperplanes, i.e. rank-two flats,
\[
L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4
\]
of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two of the \(L_i\) are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so \(w(L_1\wedge L_2)=\infty\), while the other two sums are equal:
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4)
=
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3).
\]
Thus the assertion is immediate. Hence assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If all values of \(w\) are \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise add a global constant so that the minimum value of \(w\) is \(0\). By Lemma 1.2, the positive upper level sets of \(w\) are flats. Since \(P\) has rank \(3\), the nonempty proper flats in a chain have the form
\[
a\le B
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane. Hence, after choosing such a flag, there exist \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{a\}}(x)+\mu\mathbf 1_B(x).
\]
Thus \(w(a)=\lambda+\mu\), \(w(x)=\mu\) for \(x\in B\setminus\{a\}\), and \(w(x)=0\) for \(x\notin B\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and in that case
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=y_i.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i\) and \(j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), is \(\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j\ne a\), and is \(\lambda+\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j=a\).

We are therefore looking at the three perfect matchings of four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where a matched pair contributes a nonnegative cost only when its two colors agree. If all four colors are distinct, all three matching costs are \(0\). If exactly one pair of colors agrees, the two matchings avoiding that pair tie for the minimum. If the colors have multiplicities \(2+2\), the two cross-matchings tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(3+1\), all three matchings have the same cost. If all four colors agree, all three matchings again have the same cost. Hence no unique finite minimum occurs.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\lambda+\mu,& y=a,\\
\mu,& y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching sums are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of them are not \(a\), so at least two of the three sums equal \(\mu\), the minimum. If exactly two of \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors have the same cost and are minimal. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

### 5. The local dual Plücker lemma

We now prove the key local relation.

**Lemma 5.1: local dual Plücker lemma.**  
Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), not necessarily distinct. Set
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Then, with the convention \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}F=k\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\). The other two sums are equal:
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24})
=
\bar p(K_{23})+\bar p(K_{14}).
\]
Thus the result follows. Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

Since \(L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Therefore every \(K_{ij}\) has rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}Z\ge k-2.
\]
Because \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) are distinct, \(H_1\wedge H_2\) has rank \(k\), so \(\operatorname{rk}Z\le k\). Thus there are three cases.

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\)

Every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), hence \(K_{ij}=Z\) for all \(i,j\). All three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
We claim \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Since \(T_i\) is the intersection of three hyperplanes in \(Y\), its rank is at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Hence \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\).

Let \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j=K_{ij}\). The flats \(T_m\) and \(T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats over \(Z\), so their join has rank \(k\). Therefore
\[
T_m\vee T_n=K_{ij}.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and for each \(i\) choose an element \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Then
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(K_{ij}\). By flat-constancy,
\[
p_{B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}=\bar p(K_{ij}).
\]
Apply the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for the valuated matroid \(p\) to the \((k-2)\)-set \(B\) and the four elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\). The three Plücker sums become exactly
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),\quad
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),\quad
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23}).
\]
Thus the desired minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\)

The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three modular geometric lattice. Let \(P\) be the simple rank-three matroid whose lattice of flats is \([Z,Y]\). The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become hyperplanes \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(K_{ij}\) become points
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=K_{ij}/Z.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(x\) of \(P\), define
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x).
\]
If all these values are \(\infty\), then the desired relation is trivial. Otherwise \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\). Indeed, let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\), and choose representatives \(e_x\in E\) for the atoms \(x\in D\). The set
\[
B\cup\{e_x:x\in D\}
\]
is dependent, whereas replacing \(D\) by any proper subset gives an independent set. Hence it contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{e_x:x\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), we get that
\[
\min_{x\in D} p_{B\cup\{e_x\}}
=
\min_{x\in D}\bar p(Z\vee x)
=
\min_{x\in D}w(x)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\) is a tropical point.

Now Lemma 4.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) gives exactly the desired relation for the six flats \(K_{ij}\). \(\square\)

### 6. The tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Let
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the rank-\(\ell\) tropical Plücker relations.

If \(\ell<2\), there are no Plücker relations to check. Assume \(\ell\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every set \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all six \(p^\dagger\)-terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every \(\ell\)-subset appearing in the Plücker relation that contains \(a\) is \(M\)-dependent. Each of the three Plücker sums contains exactly one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Hence we may assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-2=r-k-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp=Y\wedge x^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), \(X\vee x\) has rank \(\ell-1\), so \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For any pair \(x,y\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]
If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(X\vee x=X\vee y\), so \(H_x=H_y\), and \(H_x\wedge H_y\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), hence has rank \(k+1\). Therefore
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
So in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is therefore exactly the local dual Plücker relation of Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma for quotient incidence

We next prove the tool needed for the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

**Lemma 7.1.**  
Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\). Suppose
\[
\delta:L(N')\to L(N)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), write \(\bar q(H)\) for the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define a function \(u\) on atoms \(a\) of \(N'\) by
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a)).
\]
Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.2, it is enough to show that for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a\in A(N'):\ u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to show that \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, \(a,b,c\) are distinct atoms on a rank-two flat of \(N'\). Applying \(\delta\), the flats
\[
\delta(a),\quad \delta(b),\quad \delta(c)
\]
are distinct hyperplanes of \(N\), all containing
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b)=\delta(a)\wedge\delta(b),
\]
which has rank \(n-2\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) of \(N\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_a\})=\delta(a),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_b\})=\delta(b),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_c\})=\delta(c).
\]
In the rank-two contraction \(N/Z\), the three corresponding atoms form a circuit. Equivalently, there is an \(N\)-circuit \(C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}.
\]
Apply the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). The minimum of
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_b\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. By flat-constancy,
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}}=\bar q(\delta(a))=u_a,
\]
and similarly for \(b\) and \(c\).

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge\tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimum among the three displayed values. This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge\tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Therefore \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Thus \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). \(\square\)

### 8. The quotient circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\subseteq E,\qquad |A|=\ell-1=r-k-1.
\]
We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has the wrong size, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-1=r-k-1\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+1\).

For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{c\})=X\vee c,
\]
and therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

Consider the restriction \(M|Y\), which has rank \(k+1\). Define \(q\) on \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\) by restriction:
\[
q_I=p_I,\qquad I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\) is identically \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(H)=\infty\) for every hyperplane \(H\) of \(Y\), and all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\); the desired relation is trivial.

Assume \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the Plücker relations and circuit-incidence relations are inherited from \(p\).

The interval \([X,E]\), which is the lattice of flats of the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\), is order-reversingly isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by
\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]
Applying Lemma 7.1 to \(N=M|Y\), to the simplified contraction \(N'=M/X\), and to \(q\), we obtain a tropical point \(u\) on the atoms of \(M/X\), given by
\[
u_{X\vee c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
for \(c\notin X\).

Now return to the original circuit \(C\). The terms with \(c\in C\cap X\) are \(\infty\), so only \(C\setminus X\) matters. In the contraction \(M/X\), the set \(C\setminus X\) is dependent unless it is empty. Moreover, if \(c_0\in C\setminus X\), then because \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit,
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus in \(M/X\), the image of \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of the other elements of \(C\setminus X\). Consequently, every element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in some circuit of the contraction contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the values
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
had a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\), then the corresponding contraction circuit containing \(c_0\) would have a unique finite minimum for the tropical point \(u\). This contradicts the circuit condition for \(u\). Therefore the quotient circuit-incidence relation holds for \(p^\dagger\).

Combining Sections 6 and 8, \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

### 9. Involutivity

We now prove that
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\subseteq E\) be a \(k\)-subset. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 2.1, and the definition of \((p^\dagger)^\dagger\) also gives
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F\) has rank \(k\), so \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). Let \(J\) be any basis of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\) exactly.

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a global constant \(c\), then \(\bar p\) and \(p^\dagger\) are also shifted by \(c\). Hence \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids and is an involution there.

### 10. Compatibility with flat points

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\). Its rank is
\[
r-\operatorname{rk}F.
\]
For an independent set \(I\) of this rank, \(p^F_I=0\) exactly when \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). Equivalently, if
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
then \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, \(I\) is a basis of the contraction \(M/F\) exactly when \(F\vee G=\hat 1\), and modularity then gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}G+\operatorname{rk}F-r
=
0.
\]

Thus
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Now let \(J\) be an \(M\)-independent subset of size \(\operatorname{rk}F\), and put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J
=
\overline{p^F}(G^\perp).
\]
This is finite exactly when \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e. exactly when
\[
G^\perp\vee F=\hat 1,\qquad G^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0.
\]
Applying \(\perp\), these two conditions are equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F^\perp=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F^\perp=\hat 1.
\]
That is, \(G\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
0,& \operatorname{cl}_M(J)\text{ is a complement of }F^\perp,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is precisely the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid \(p^{F^\perp}\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

### 11. Scope of the result

The construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is therefore a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the set of projective valuated matroid quotients of \(M\), and it agrees with the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat points.

No order-reversal statement for arbitrary inclusions has been proved here. In particular, this proof does not show that if one tropical linear subspace is contained in another, then their \(\dagger\)-images are contained in the opposite order. Thus the result is only a set-level involution plus compatibility with the flat subposet, not a full solution of the original Dressian-poset question.

## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a finite loopless simple modular matroid of rank \(r\le 3\) on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\Dr(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces \(L(v)\subseteq \Trop(M)\) arising from valuated matroid quotients \(v\) of the ordinary valuated matroid of \(M\), ordered by inclusion. For a flat \(F\), set
\[
X_F:=\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
viewed as a tropical linear space on the original ground set \(E\).

Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\qquad\text{for every flat }F\in\mathcal L(M).
\]

Here modular means
\[
\rk(A)+\rk(B)=\rk(A\cap B)+\rk(\cl(A\cup B))
\]
for all flats \(A,B\).

---

### 1. Standard facts and notation

Write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with the min-plus convention. A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(v\) on \(E\) is represented by Plücker coordinates \(v_I\in\mathbb T\) for \(I\in\binom Ek\), not all \(\infty\), modulo adding a global scalar.

For the ordinary matroid \(M\), let \(\mu_M\) be the valuated matroid with
\[
(\mu_M)_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard projective facts. First, projective tropical linear spaces are cut out by valuated circuit equations. Second, for valuated matroids \(v,w\) on the same ground set,
\[
L(v)\subseteq L(w)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
v\text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of }w.
\]
Equivalently, if \(\rk(v)=k\le \ell=\rk(w)\), then \(v\) is a quotient of \(w\) precisely when, for every \(I\in\binom E{k-1}\) and \(J\in\binom E{\ell+1}\), the minimum
\[
\min_{j\in J\setminus I}\bigl(v_{I\cup\{j\}}+w_{J\setminus\{j\}}\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice. These are the projective tropical linear space and quotient-inclusion theorems of Brandt–Eur–Zhang. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))

---

### 2. Same-rank inclusions are equal

We first record a useful lemma.

**Lemma 2.1.** Let \(v,w\) be valuated matroids of the same rank \(k\) on \(E\). If
\[
L(v)\subseteq L(w),
\]
then \(L(v)=L(w)\).

**Proof.** By the quotient-inclusion theorem, \(v\) is a valuated quotient of \(w\). The underlying ordinary matroids are therefore a same-rank matroid quotient. Same-rank ordinary quotients have the same rank function, hence the same bases.

Let \(B=I\cup\{a\}\) and \(B'=I\cup\{b\}\) be adjacent bases, with \(|I|=k-1\). Apply the valuated quotient incidence relation to \(I\) and \(J=I\cup\{a,b\}\). The only two terms are
\[
v_B+w_{B'}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
v_{B'}+w_B,
\]
and the minimum of these two terms must be attained twice. Hence
\[
v_B+w_{B'}=v_{B'}+w_B,
\]
or equivalently
\[
v_B-w_B=v_{B'}-w_{B'}.
\]
The basis graph of a matroid is connected, so \(v_B-w_B\) is constant on all bases. Thus \(v\) and \(w\) differ by a global tropical scalar, and therefore define the same projective tropical linear space. \(\square\)

---

### 3. Codimension-one quotients

Because \(M\) is simple, we identify rank-one flats with elements of \(E\). Since \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism of the graded lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\), it sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats.

For \(2\le r\le 3\), define a map from points of \(\Trop(M)\) to codimension-one valuated quotients as follows. If \(q=(q_e)_{e\in E}\in\Trop(M)\), define hyperplane weights
\[
w_H(q):=q_{H^\perp},
\qquad H\text{ a hyperplane of }M.
\]
Equivalently, define a rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid \(\nu_q\) by
\[
(\nu_q)_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\cl(I)^\perp},& |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty,& |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ dependent in }M.
\end{cases}
\]
Let
\[
\alpha(q):=L(\nu_q).
\]

We prove that \(\alpha\) identifies \(\Trop(M)\) with the rank \(r-1\) part of \(\Dr(M)\).

---

#### Rank \(2\)

If \(r=2\), then \(M=U_{2,E}\), because \(M\) is simple. Hyperplanes are atoms. Rank-one quotients of \(M\) are exactly points of \(\Trop(M)\), and the map
\[
q\longmapsto \bigl(q_{e^\perp}\bigr)_{e\in E}
\]
is just a coordinate permutation induced by the polarity. Hence \(\alpha\) is a bijection from \(\Trop(M)\) to the rank-one part of \(\Dr(M)\). In this case \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\).

---

#### Rank \(3\)

Assume now \(r=3\). Hyperplanes are lines. In a rank-three modular simple matroid, any two distinct lines meet in exactly one point: if \(L_1,L_2\) are distinct lines, then \(\cl(L_1\cup L_2)=E\), so modularity gives
\[
2+2=\rk(L_1\cap L_2)+3,
\]
hence \(\rk(L_1\cap L_2)=1\).

Let \(\nu\) be a rank-two valuated quotient of \(M\). We first show that \(\nu_{ab}\) depends only on the line \(\cl(a,b)\). Suppose \(a,b,c\) are distinct points on a line \(H\), and choose \(d\notin H\). Apply the quotient incidence relation to \(I=\{a\}\) and \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\). The term with \(j=d\) is infinite because \(\{a,b,c\}\) is dependent in \(M\), while the terms with \(j=b,c\) are
\[
\nu_{ab},\qquad \nu_{ac}.
\]
Thus \(\nu_{ab}=\nu_{ac}\). Varying \(a,b,c\) shows that there is a well-defined line weight
\[
w_H:=\nu_{ab}
\qquad\text{whenever }H=\cl(a,b).
\]

Define
\[
q_e:=w_{e^\perp}.
\]
We show \(q\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(\nu\) has at least one finite basis coordinate, \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\).

The circuits of a simple rank-three matroid are exactly:

1. three-point subsets contained in a line;
2. four-point subsets with no three collinear.

First consider a line circuit \(\{a,b,c\}\subseteq H\). Let \(h=H^\perp\). Choose a line \(D\) not containing \(h\): if \(h\notin H\), take \(D=H\); if \(h\in H\), choose \(e\in H\setminus\{h\}\) and \(d\notin H\), and take \(D=\cl(d,e)\).

For each \(x\in\{a,b,c\}\), the line \(x^\perp\) contains \(h\). Since \(D\) does not contain \(h\), the two lines \(D\) and \(x^\perp\) meet in a point \(u_x\neq h\). The points \(u_a,u_b,u_c\) are distinct. Apply the quotient incidence relation to
\[
I=\{h\},\qquad J=\{h,u_a,u_b,u_c\}.
\]
All three sets \(J\setminus\{u_x\}\) are bases of \(M\), so the relation says that the minimum of
\[
\nu_{h u_a},\quad \nu_{h u_b},\quad \nu_{h u_c}
\]
is attained at least twice. But \(\cl(h,u_x)=x^\perp\), so
\[
\nu_{h u_x}=w_{x^\perp}=q_x.
\]
Therefore \(\min(q_a,q_b,q_c)\) is attained at least twice.

Now consider a four-point circuit \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) with no three collinear. Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(q_a\) is the unique minimum. Let
\[
x=\cl(b,c)\cap \cl(a,d),
\]
which exists and is distinct from \(a,b,c,d\). The line circuit \(\{a,d,x\}\) forces
\[
q_x=q_a,
\]
because \(q_a<q_d\). But then the line circuit \(\{b,c,x\}\) has unique minimum \(q_x\), since \(q_b,q_c>q_a=q_x\), a contradiction. Hence every circuit of \(M\) satisfies the tropical circuit condition, so \(q\in\Trop(M)\).

Conversely, let \(q\in\Trop(M)\). Define
\[
(\nu_q)_{ab}:=q_{\cl(a,b)^\perp}
\qquad(a\neq b).
\]
We must show that \(\nu_q\) is a rank-two valuated quotient of \(M\).

First suppose \(q\) has only finite coordinates. Then \(q\) lies in the ordinary Bergman fan. We use the following elementary flag description. After adding a scalar, \(q\) can be written as a nonnegative linear combination of characteristic vectors of a chain of proper flats. Indeed, if the finite values of \(q\) are
\[
c_0<c_1<\cdots<c_m,
\]
then the sets
\[
F_t=\{e:q_e\ge c_t\},\qquad t=1,\dots,m,
\]
are flats exactly because no circuit has a unique minimum, and
\[
q\equiv \sum_{t=1}^m (c_t-c_{t-1})\mathbf 1_{F_t}
\pmod{\mathbb R\mathbf 1}.
\]
In rank three, such a chain has length at most two, so \(q\) has the form
\[
q\equiv \lambda\,\mathbf 1_p+\mu\,\mathbf 1_L
\]
with \(\lambda,\mu\ge 0\), where \(p\) is a point, \(L\) is a line, and if both coefficients are nonzero then \(p\le L\).

We verify the rank-two tropical Plücker relations for \(\nu_q\). For a four-subset \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\), the relation is that the minimum of
\[
\nu_{ab}+\nu_{cd},\qquad
\nu_{ac}+\nu_{bd},\qquad
\nu_{ad}+\nu_{bc}
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(a,b,c,d\) lie on one line, all three sums are equal. If \(a,b,c\) lie on a line \(H\) and \(d\notin H\), then the three sums have the common summand \(q_{H^\perp}\), and the remaining three terms are
\[
q_{\cl(a,d)^\perp},\quad
q_{\cl(b,d)^\perp},\quad
q_{\cl(c,d)^\perp}.
\]
These three points lie on the line \(d^\perp\), so their minimum is attained at least twice by the line-circuit condition for \(q\).

It remains to treat the case where \(a,b,c,d\) have no three collinear. Let
\[
s_1=\cl(a,b)^\perp,\quad s_1'=\cl(c,d)^\perp,
\]
\[
s_2=\cl(a,c)^\perp,\quad s_2'=\cl(b,d)^\perp,
\]
\[
s_3=\cl(a,d)^\perp,\quad s_3'=\cl(b,c)^\perp.
\]
The six points \(s_i,s_i'\) are distinct. For a flat \(F\), define
\[
m_F=\bigl(|F\cap\{s_1,s_1'\}|,\ |F\cap\{s_2,s_2'\}|,\ |F\cap\{s_3,s_3'\}|\bigr).
\]
The Plücker triple for \(q=\lambda\mathbf 1_p+\mu\mathbf 1_L\) is
\[
\lambda m_p+\mu m_L,
\]
up to adding the same scalar to all three coordinates.

For a point \(p\), \(m_p\) is either \(0\) or a standard basis vector. For a line \(L\), let \(\ell=L^\perp\). The condition \(s_i\in L\) is equivalent to \(\ell\in s_i^\perp\), i.e. to \(\ell\) lying on the corresponding side line of the quadrangle. Thus \(m_L\) is one of
\[
0,\quad (1,0,0)\text{ up to permutation},\quad (2,0,0)\text{ up to permutation},\quad (1,1,1).
\]
If \(p\le L\), then every nonzero coordinate of \(m_p\) occurs in a coordinate where \(m_L\) is already positive. It follows immediately in all cases that the minimum of the three coordinates of
\[
\lambda m_p+\mu m_L
\]
is attained at least twice. Hence the Plücker relation holds.

Now we check the quotient incidence relations between \(\nu_q\) and \(\mu_M\). Let \(x\in E\) and \(J\in\binom E4\). We must show that the minimum of
\[
\nu_{xj}+(\mu_M)_{J\setminus\{j\}},
\qquad j\in J\setminus\{x\},
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(J\) lies on a line, all terms are \(\infty\). If \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\) with \(a,b,c\) collinear and \(d\notin\cl(a,b,c)\), then only the terms with \(j\in\{a,b,c\}\) can be finite. If \(x\in\{a,b,c\}\), the two remaining finite terms are equal because they come from the same line. If \(x\notin\cl(a,b,c)\), the relevant poles lie as distinct points on the line \(x^\perp\), so the line-circuit condition for \(q\) applies. If \(x\in\cl(a,b,c)\setminus\{a,b,c\}\), all relevant terms are equal.

Finally, if \(J\) has no three collinear, then all \((\mu_M)_{J\setminus\{j\}}\) are finite. The points \(\cl(x,j)^\perp\), for \(j\in J\setminus\{x\}\), lie on the line \(x^\perp\). If a minimizing pole occurs more than once, the minimum is already repeated. Otherwise, a unique minimum would give a three-point line circuit on \(x^\perp\) with a unique minimum, impossible. Thus the incidence relation holds.

So \(\nu_q\) is a rank-two valuated quotient of \(M\) whenever \(q\) is finite. For an arbitrary projective \(q\in\Trop(M)\), choose finite \(q^{(n)}\in\Trop(M)\) converging to \(q\). The coordinates of \(\nu_{q^{(n)}}\) converge to those of \(\nu_q\), and the tropical Plücker and incidence relations are closed conditions. Hence \(\nu_q\) is also a rank-two valuated quotient for arbitrary \(q\in\Trop(M)\).

The two constructions are inverse: starting from \(\nu\), the line weights \(w_H\) give \(q_e=w_{e^\perp}\), and then
\[
(\nu_q)_{ab}=q_{\cl(a,b)^\perp}=w_{\cl(a,b)}=\nu_{ab}.
\]
Starting from \(q\), one recovers \(q_e\) from the line \(e^\perp\). Thus \(\alpha\) is a bijection between \(\Trop(M)\) and the rank-two part of \(\Dr(M)\).

---

### 4. Incidence symmetry

Assume \(2\le r\le 3\). We prove that for all \(p,q\in\Trop(M)\),
\[
p\in \alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in \alpha(p).
\]

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be a basis of \(M\). Define
\[
b_i^*:=\cl(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
We first note that
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again a basis, and
\[
\cl(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})=b_i^\perp.
\]

For \(r=2\), this is immediate. If \(B=\{a,b\}\), then
\[
a^*=b^\perp,\qquad b^*=a^\perp,
\]
which are distinct atoms, hence a basis.

For \(r=3\), write \(B=\{a,b,c\}\). Then
\[
a^*=\cl(b,c)^\perp,\qquad
b^*=\cl(a,c)^\perp,\qquad
c^*=\cl(a,b)^\perp.
\]
The points \(b^*,c^*\) both lie on the line \(a^\perp\), and they are distinct, so
\[
\cl(b^*,c^*)=a^\perp.
\]
Similarly,
\[
\cl(a^*,c^*)=b^\perp,\qquad
\cl(a^*,b^*)=c^\perp.
\]
Moreover \(a^*\not\le a^\perp\), because otherwise applying \(\perp\) would give
\[
a\le \cl(b,c),
\]
contradicting that \(a,b,c\) is a basis. Thus \(a^*,b^*,c^*\) is a basis.

Now \(p\in\alpha(q)\) means that \(p\) satisfies the circuit equations of \(\nu_q\). For each \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\), the relevant condition is
\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+(\nu_q)_{S\setminus\{i\}}\bigr)
\]
attained at least twice.

If \(S=B\) is a basis of \(M\), this condition is
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
attained at least twice.

If \(S\) is not a basis, then for \(r\le 3\) the condition imposes no additional restriction beyond \(p\in\Trop(M)\). Indeed, in rank \(3\), such an \(S\) is a three-point line circuit, and the three Plücker coefficients \((\nu_q)_{S\setminus\{i\}}\) are all equal, possibly all \(\infty\). Thus the condition is either automatic or exactly the usual circuit condition for \(p\).

Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the collection of basis equations
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\quad\text{attained at least twice}
\]
for all bases \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\).

But the basis equation for \(B\) with \(p,q\) is exactly the basis equation for \(B^*\) with \(q,p\), because applying the construction again gives
\[
(b_i^*)^*=b_i.
\]
Hence
\[
p\in\alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in\alpha(p).
\]

---

### 5. Definition of \(\Phi\)

If \(r=0\), then \(E=\varnothing\), and \(\Dr(M)\) has one element. Define \(\Phi=\mathrm{id}\).

If \(r=1\), then \(M\) has one nonloop element, and \(\Dr(M)\) has only the bottom element \(X_E\) and the top element \(X_\varnothing=\Trop(M)\). Define \(\Phi\) by swapping them.

Now assume \(2\le r\le 3\). Let
\[
\hat 0:=X_E,\qquad \hat 1:=X_\varnothing=\Trop(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1,\qquad \Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0.
\]
For a rank-one element \(p\in\Dr(M)\), equivalently a point \(p\in\Trop(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(p):=\alpha(p).
\]
For a rank \(r-1\) element \(L\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(L):=\alpha^{-1}(L).
\]
In rank \(2\), the rank-one and rank-\((r-1)\) parts coincide and \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\), so this is consistent. In rank \(3\), it exchanges points and tropical hyperplanes inside \(\Trop(M)\).

By construction, \(\Phi\) is an involution.

---

### 6. \(\Phi\) reverses inclusion

Let \(X\subseteq Y\) in \(\Dr(M)\). If \(X\) and \(Y\) have the same rank, then \(X=Y\) by Lemma 2.1, so there is nothing to prove.

The cases involving \(\hat 0\) or \(\hat 1\) are automatic because \(\Phi\) swaps them. Thus the only nontrivial strict inclusion occurs in rank \(3\), where \(X\) is a point \(p\) and \(Y\) is a rank-two tropical linear space \(L\). Write
\[
L=\alpha(q)
\]
for a unique \(q\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
p\subseteq L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\in\alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in\alpha(p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]
using the incidence symmetry proved above. Hence \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

---

### 7. Extension of the flat-lattice polarity

It remains to verify that \(\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}\) for every flat \(F\).

First,
\[
X_\varnothing=\Trop(M)=\hat 1,\qquad X_E=\hat 0,
\]
and \(\Phi\) swaps them, matching
\[
\varnothing^\perp=E,\qquad E^\perp=\varnothing.
\]

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane. Then \(X_H\) is a rank-one tropical linear space, i.e. a point, with coordinates
\[
q^H_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in H,\\
0,& e\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
For any hyperplane \(K\),
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=q^H_{K^\perp}.
\]
Thus
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=\infty
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
K^\perp\le H
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
H^\perp\le K.
\]
These are exactly the hyperplane weights of
\[
X_{H^\perp}=\Trop(M/H^\perp\oplus U_{0,H^\perp}).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi(X_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

Now let \(a\) be an atom. The codimension-one tropical linear space \(X_a\) has hyperplane weights
\[
w^a_K=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& a\le K,\\
0,& a\nleq K.
\end{cases}
\]
Applying \(\alpha^{-1}\), we get the point \(q\) with
\[
q_e=w^a_{e^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
q_e=\infty
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\le e^\perp
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
e\le a^\perp.
\]
Thus \(q\) is exactly the point \(X_{a^\perp}\). Therefore
\[
\Phi(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

For \(r\le 3\), every proper nonempty flat is either an atom or a hyperplane. Hence
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\). This completes the proof.

### Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\). Assume that its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Fix \(0\le k\le r\). Let
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), meaning that \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, and satisfies the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\). We use the convention \(p_T=\infty\) whenever \(|T|\ne k\). For \(k=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations.

Then:

1. If \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). If \(I,I'\in\binom{E}{k}\) are \(M\)-independent and \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I')\), then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). Thus \(p\) has a well-defined value \(\bar p(F)\) on every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), namely the common value on the bases of \(F\).

2. Define \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

3. The operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids, is involutive, and satisfies
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
for every representative \(p\).

4. For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let \(p^F\) be the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on \(E\). Then \(p^F\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), and
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

No assertion is made here that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

---

### Proof

Write \(\overline{\mathbb R}=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A finite family in \(\overline{\mathbb R}\) has no unique finite minimizer if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs at least twice.

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution,
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp.
\]
Also
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
Indeed, the anti-isomorphism sends maximal chains in \([\widehat 0,F]\) to maximal chains in \([F^\perp,\widehat 1]\).

---

#### 1. Two elementary facts about modular matroids

We identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\), since \(M\) is simple.

**Lemma 1.1.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(S\) is a set of atoms such that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the rank-two flat \(a\vee b\) also belongs to \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Let \(F=\bigvee_{a\in S} a\). It suffices to show that every atom \(x\le F\) lies in \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimally such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
By minimality, no \(a_i\) lies in the join of the others; hence \(a_1,\dots,a_m\) are independent atoms, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m)=m.
\]

If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Assume \(m\ge 2\). If \(x=a_m\), then \(x\in S\), so assume \(x\ne a_m\). Put
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad L=a_m\vee x.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}P=m-1\), \(\operatorname{rk}L=2\), and
\[
P\vee L=P\vee a_m.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
\operatorname{rk}P+\operatorname{rk}L-\operatorname{rk}(P\vee L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(b:=P\wedge L\) is an atom. Since \(b\le P\), induction on \(m\) gives \(b\in S\). Also \(a_m\in S\). Because \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), the line-closedness assumption gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is closed. ∎

**Lemma 1.2.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple matroid with atom set \(A(N)\). Let
\[
w:A(N)\to \overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies the tropical circuit condition
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N): w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** Suppose some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. Then there exists \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A_\tau)\). Hence some circuit \(C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\). Since \(w_e<\tau\le w_x\) for all \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer.

Conversely, suppose a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Choose a real number \(\tau\) with
\[
w_e<\tau\le w_x
\]
for every \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\); this is possible even if all other values are \(\infty\). Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), but \(e\notin A_\tau\) and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. ∎

---

#### 2. Flat-constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p:\binom{E}{k}\to\overline{\mathbb R}\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

If \(k=0\), then the only \(0\)-subset is \(\varnothing\), which is independent, so the claims below are immediate. Assume \(k\ge 1\).

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). For every \(x\in C\setminus\{c\}\), the set \(A\cup\{x\}=A\) has size \(k-1\), so by convention
\[
p_{A\cup\{x\}}=\infty.
\]
The only possibly finite term in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) is \(p_I=p_{A\cup\{c\}}\). Therefore the relation forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\), because otherwise it would be contained in the independent set \(A\cup\{a\}\). Applying the circuit-incidence relation to \(C\) and \(A\), all terms are \(\infty\) except possibly
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},\qquad p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
Thus those two values are equal.

Finally, any two bases of a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) are connected by a sequence of single-element basis exchanges inside \(M|F\). The preceding adjacent-basis argument shows that \(p\) has the same value on all bases of \(F\).

Therefore \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). For flats of rank different from \(k\), set
\[
\bar p(F):=\infty.
\]

---

#### 3. Definition and nontriviality of \(p^\dagger\)

Let
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
Define \(p^\dagger\) on \(\ell\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This function is not identically \(\infty\). Since \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), there exists \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By the previous section, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(\ell\). Choose a basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I
<
\infty.
\]

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations.

---

#### 4. A rank-three matching lemma

**Lemma 4.1.** Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\overline{\mathbb R}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), i.e. \(w\) is not identically \(\infty\) and satisfies the tropical circuit condition on every circuit of \(P\). Extend \(w\) to all flats of \(P\) by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) whenever \(F\) is not an atom.

For any four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)=\infty.
\]
The other two sums are equal:
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4)
=
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3).
\]
Thus the claim holds. Hence assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

Normalize \(w\) by subtracting its finite minimum, so \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 1.2, every positive upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x:w(x)\ge \tau\},\qquad \tau>0,
\]
is a proper flat of \(P\). These flats are nested. In a rank-three simple matroid, every nonempty proper flat is either an atom or a hyperplane. Therefore the positive upper level sets form a chain consisting of at most one hyperplane \(B\) and at most one atom \(a\le B\). Allowing degenerate cases, we may choose a flag \(a\le B\) and numbers
\[
0\le \beta\le \alpha\le \infty
\]
such that
\[
w(x)=
\begin{cases}
0,& x\notin B,\\
\beta,& x\in B\setminus\{a\},\\
\alpha,& x=a.
\end{cases}
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). For each \(i\), set
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
This is an atom. For \(i\ne j\), the atom \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies in \(B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\), in which case
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=y_i.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i\) and \(j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), and is \(w(y_i)\) if \(y_i=y_j\).

The three displayed sums are therefore the three perfect-matching costs on four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where a matched pair contributes only when the two colors agree. If all four colors are distinct, all three costs are \(0\). If exactly one pair agrees, the two matchings avoiding that pair tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(2+2\), the two cross-matchings tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(3+1\), all three matchings have the same cost. If all four colors are equal, all three matchings again have the same cost. In all cases there is no unique finite minimum.

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). Since the \(L_i\) are distinct, \(L_2,L_3,L_4\ne B\). Put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4,
\]
and write \(c(y)=w(y)\) for atoms \(y\le B\). For atoms \(y,z\le B\), define
\[
g(y,z)=
\begin{cases}
c(y),& y=z,\\
0,& y\ne z.
\end{cases}
\]
The three sums are
\[
c(y_2)+g(y_3,y_4),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+g(y_2,y_4),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+g(y_2,y_3).
\]

If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of them are different from \(a\), so at least two of the three sums are equal to \(\beta\), and the third is at least \(\beta\). If exactly two of the \(y_j\) are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two equal atoms have the same cost and are minimal. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Again, no unique finite minimum occurs. ∎

---

#### 5. A local dual Plücker relation

**Lemma 5.1.** Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Put
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
With the convention \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}(F)=k\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\). The other two sums are equal. Hence the result follows.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Thus every \(K_{ij}\) has rank \(k\).

Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}Z\ge k-2.
\]
Since \(H_1\wedge H_2\) has rank \(k\), we also have \(\operatorname{rk}Z\le k\). There are three cases.

**Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\).**  
Then every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), so every \(K_{ij}=Z\). All three sums are equal.

**Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\).**  
For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
We claim \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is obtained by intersecting two distinct hyperplanes, giving rank \(k\), and then intersecting with a third hyperplane, which lowers rank by at most one. Thus \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k-1\). On the other hand \(T_i\le H_j\wedge H_m\) for distinct \(j,m\ne i\), so \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\le k\). If \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Hence \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\).

Let \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq K_{ij}\). They are distinct: if \(T_m=T_n\), then this common flat would be contained in all four \(H_i\), hence in \(Z\), impossible because \(\operatorname{rk}T_m=k-1>\operatorname{rk}Z\). Thus \(T_m\) and \(T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats containing \(Z\) inside the rank-\(k\) flat \(K_{ij}\), so
\[
T_m\vee T_n=K_{ij}.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each \(i\), choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Since \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\) for \(i\ne j\), the elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) are distinct. Moreover, for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the set
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is a basis of \(K_{ij}\). Therefore
\[
p_{B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}=\bar p(K_{ij}).
\]

Apply the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) to the \((k-2)\)-set \(B\) and the four elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\). The three resulting sums are precisely
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23}).
\]
Thus the desired relation holds.

**Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\).**  
The interval \([Z,Y]\) is the lattice of flats of a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(P\), namely the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\). Its atoms are the rank-\(k\) flats of \(M\) between \(Z\) and \(Y\), and its hyperplanes are the rank-\((k+1)\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\).

Define
\[
w(X)=\bar p(X)
\]
for every atom \(X\) of \(P\). If all values \(w(X)\) are \(\infty\), then all six \(\bar p(K_{ij})\) are \(\infty\), and the desired relation is trivial.

Assume \(w\) is not identically \(\infty\). We show that \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(X\in D\), choose an element
\[
e_X\in X\setminus Z.
\]
Because \(D\) is dependent in the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\), the set
\[
B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}
\]
is dependent in \(M\). Since every proper subset of \(D\) is independent in \(P\), every proper subset of the representatives is independent over \(Z\). Therefore any \(M\)-circuit contained in \(B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}\) must contain all representatives \(e_X\).

Choose such an \(M\)-circuit \(C\). Applying the circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), the terms coming from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup\{e_X\}}=\bar p(X)=w(X).
\]
Hence
\[
\min_{X\in D} w(X)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\).

Now apply Lemma 4.1 to the four hyperplanes \(H_1,\dots,H_4\) of \(P\). Their pairwise meets are precisely the atoms \(K_{ij}\), so Lemma 4.1 gives exactly the claimed relation. ∎

---

#### 6. Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the rank-\(\ell\) tropical Plücker relations, where \(\ell=r-k\).

If \(\ell<2\), there are no Plücker relations. Assume \(\ell\ge 2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every set \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all six terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every set \(S\cup\{a,x\}\) is \(M\)-dependent. Each Plücker sum contains exactly one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Hence we may assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-2=r-k-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), the flat \(X\vee x\) has rank \(\ell-1\), so \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For any pair \(x,y\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then since \(x,y\notin X\), we have
\[
X\vee x=X\vee y.
\]
Therefore \(H_x=H_y\), so \(H_x\wedge H_y=H_x\) has rank \(k+1\), and hence
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
Consequently, in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is therefore precisely Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

---

#### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma

**Lemma 7.1.** Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\ge 1\). Suppose
\[
\delta:\mathcal L(N')\to\mathcal L(N)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), let \(\bar q(H)\) be the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a))
\]
for every atom \(a\) of \(N'\). Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** Since \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\), there is an \((n-1)\)-subset \(I\) with \(q_I<\infty\). By flat-constancy, \(I\) is independent in \(N\), and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_N(I)\) is a hyperplane with \(\bar q(H)<\infty\). Since \(\delta\) maps atoms of \(N'\) bijectively to hyperplanes of \(N\), \(u\) is not identically \(\infty\).

By Lemma 1.2, it suffices to prove that every upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a\in A(N'):u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to prove that \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove, so assume \(a,b,c\) are distinct. Then
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b)
\]
has rank \(n-2\), and the three hyperplanes \(\delta(a),\delta(b),\delta(c)\) of \(N\) all contain \(Z\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For \(s\in\{a,b,c\}\), choose
\[
x_s\in \delta(s)\setminus Z
\]
so that \(B\cup\{x_s\}\) is a basis of \(\delta(s)\). In the rank-two contraction \(N/Z\), the elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) lie in three distinct rank-one flats, hence form a circuit. Equivalently, \(B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}\) is dependent in \(N\), while \(B\) together with any two of the \(x_s\) is independent. Thus there is an \(N\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}
\]
containing all three elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\).

Apply the circuit-incidence relation for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). The terms coming from elements of \(C\cap B\) are \(\infty\), and
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_s\}}=\bar q(\delta(s))=u_s
\]
for \(s=a,b,c\). Therefore
\[
\min(u_a,u_b,u_c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge \tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimum among \(u_a,u_b,u_c\). This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge \tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). ∎

---

#### 8. Circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the circuit-incidence relations for a rank-\(\ell\) quotient, where \(\ell=r-k\).

If \(\ell=0\), there are no circuit-incidence relations. Assume \(\ell\ge 1\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is either dependent or has the wrong size, so all terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+1\).

For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is independent, and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]

Let \(q\) be the restriction of \(p\) to \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\):
\[
q_I=p_I,\qquad I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\) is identically \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(H)=\infty\) for every rank-\(k\) flat \(H\subseteq Y\). Hence all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\), and the desired relation holds.

Assume \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\), because the Plücker and circuit-incidence relations are inherited from \(p\).

Let \(N'\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its lattice of flats is the interval \([X,E]\). The lattice of flats of \(M|Y\) is the interval \([\widehat 0,Y]\). The map
\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing isomorphism
\[
[X,E]\longrightarrow [\widehat 0,Y].
\]
By Lemma 7.1, the function \(u\) on atoms of \(N'\) defined by
\[
u_{X\vee c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
is a tropical point of \(N'\). For \(c\notin X\), let \(\alpha(c)\) denote the atom of \(N'\) represented by \(c\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=u_{\alpha(c)}.
\]

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that the values
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
have a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\in C\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). Since \(C\) is a circuit of \(M\),
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus, in the contraction \(M/X\), the element \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of
\[
R=C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\}.
\]

If some element \(d\in R\) is parallel to \(c_0\) in \(M/X\), then \(\alpha(d)=\alpha(c_0)\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}=u_{\alpha(d)}=u_{\alpha(c_0)}=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}},
\]
contradicting uniqueness. Hence no element of \(R\) represents \(\alpha(c_0)\).

Passing to the simplification \(N'\), we then have
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in \operatorname{cl}_{N'}(\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}).
\]
Therefore there is a circuit \(D\) of \(N'\) such that
\[
\alpha(c_0)\in D\subseteq \{\alpha(c_0)\}\cup\{\alpha(d):d\in R\}.
\]
For every \(\beta\in D\setminus\{\alpha(c_0)\}\), choose \(d\in R\) with \(\beta=\alpha(d)\). By uniqueness of the finite minimum at \(c_0\),
\[
u_\beta=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{d\}}>p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c_0\}}=u_{\alpha(c_0)}
\]
or \(u_\beta=\infty\). Thus \(u\) has a unique finite minimum on the circuit \(D\), contradicting that \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

Therefore the circuit-incidence relation holds for \(p^\dagger\). Combining this with the Plücker relations proved above, \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

#### 9. Involutivity

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is any basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Hence
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
exactly.

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a global real constant \(c\), then \(\bar p\) and \(p^\dagger\) are also shifted by \(c\). Hence the construction descends to projective valuated matroids.

---

#### 10. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Put
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]
viewed as a matroid on \(E\), where the elements of \(F\) are loops. Its rank is
\[
n=r-\operatorname{rk}F.
\]
Define
\[
p^F_I=
\begin{cases}
0,& I\text{ is a basis of }N_F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

The flats of \(N_F\), viewed as subsets of \(E\), are exactly the flats of \(M\) containing \(F\). Hence \(N_F\) is a matroid quotient of \(M\). The \(0/\infty\)-valuation of the bases of a matroid satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

We verify the circuit-incidence relations. If \(n=0\), there are none. Suppose \(n>0\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). Suppose there were a unique \(c_0\in C\) such that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is an \(N_F\)-basis. Then \(A\) is independent in \(N_F\). Let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}_{N_F}(A).
\]
For every \(c\in C\setminus\{c_0\}\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is not an \(N_F\)-basis, so \(c\in H\). Since \(H\) is a flat of \(N_F\), it is also a flat of \(M\). But
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})\subseteq H,
\]
contradicting that \(A\cup\{c_0\}\) is independent in \(N_F\). Therefore \(p^F\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

Now compute its image under \(\dagger\). Let \(G\) be a rank-\(n\) flat of \(M\). For any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(G\),
\[
p^F_I=0
\]
if and only if \(I\) is a basis of \(N_F\), which is equivalent to
\[
F\vee G=\widehat 1.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular and \(\operatorname{rk}F+\operatorname{rk}G=r\), this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\widehat 0.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(\operatorname{rk}F\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \((p^F)^\dagger_J=\infty\), and \(J\) cannot be a basis of \(N_{F^\perp}\), so \(p^{F^\perp}_J=\infty\).

Assume \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
