S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Consequently
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]

So it is enough to prove the theorem for the simplification \(S\), equipped with the induced involution \(\bar\alpha\). At the end we transport the construction back to \(M\) using \(\Delta\).

---

## 2. The simplified matroid is modular

We now show that \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is modular.

Because \(S\) is simple, \(L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is finite, atomic, ranked, and semimodular. The involution
\[
\bar\alpha:L(S)\to L(S)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism, so \(L(S)\) is isomorphic to its dual lattice \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\). Hence \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also geometric and therefore semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function of \(L(S)\), and let \(r=\rho(\bar E)\). Semimodularity of \(L(S)\) gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Semimodularity of the dual lattice gives the reverse inequality. Indeed, the dual rank is \(\rho^\vee(X)=r-\rho(X)\), so semimodularity in the dual says
\[
\rho^\vee(X)+\rho^\vee(Y)
\ge
\rho^\vee(X\vee Y)+\rho^\vee(X\wedge Y),
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)= \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore \(L(S)\) is modular. Equivalently, \(S\) is a simple modular matroid.

We now prove the theorem in the simple modular case.

---

## 3. Flat-constancy for valuated quotients of a simple modular matroid

Let \(M\) now be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
The bottom flat is \(\varnothing\), and the top flat is \(E\). Because the polarity reverses maximal chains,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), i.e.
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]

### Circuit-incidence form of the quotient condition

Since \(p\) is a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\), the quotient incidence relations say that for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\) and every \((r+1)\)-subset \(T\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+M_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), then every \(M_{T\setminus i}\) is \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C_T\), and \(M_{T\setminus i}\) is finite exactly for \(i\in C_T\). Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit appears as such a \(C_T\) by extending \(C\setminus c\) to a basis and adding \(c\).

Therefore the quotient condition is equivalent to:

\[
\tag{CI}
\text{For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\), }
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer.}
\]

### Dependent sets have infinite value

Let \(I\subseteq E\) have \(|I|=k\). If \(I\) is dependent in \(M\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]

### Constancy on bases of a flat

Suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases \(I\) of any fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

We denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]
Thus
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of the flat \(F\).

For \(k=0\), this means \(\bar p(\varnothing)=p_\varnothing\). For \(k=r\), it says all \(M\)-bases of \(E\) have the same \(p\)-value.

---

## 4. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank-\((r-k)\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\text{ is \(M\)-independent and }|J|=r-k,\\[4pt]
\infty,&
J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent and }|J|=r-k.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl}_M(J))=r-k\), its polar has rank \(k\), so \(\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) is defined.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), and let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

We prove next that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

## 5. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\)

### Boundary cases

If \(k=0\), then \(p\) is rank zero and \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to adding the scalar \(p_\varnothing\). Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

If \(k=r\), then flat-constancy says that \(p\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to scalar, and \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Again \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Assume from now on that
\[
1\le k\le r-1,
\qquad
n=r-k.
\]

### 5.1 Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every term is \(\infty\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\]
then every Plücker sum contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(S\) is independent and
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]
Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2=r-k-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent, and \(p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

So the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23],
\]
so the claim is immediate.

Assume therefore that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting four hyperplanes can lower rank by at most four, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\)

Then every pairwise intersection \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is equal to \(Z\). Hence all three sums are equal.

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\)

Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). Define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[12]=p_{A\cup t_3\cup t_4},
\qquad
[34]=p_{A\cup t_1\cup t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other pairings. Therefore the desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\)

The interval \([Z,Y]\) has rank three. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\), and its hyperplanes are the \(H_i\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show that \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\), where \([Z,Y]\) is viewed as the simple rank-three matroid whose lattice of flats is the interval \([Z,Y]\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit in this interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For every point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_P)=P.
\]
By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge fact.

### Rank-three edge fact

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\Trop(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

For a vector \(w\) on the points of a matroid \(N\), the condition \(w\in\Trop(N)\) is equivalent to saying that every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. Indeed, a non-flat superlevel set produces a circuit with a unique minimum, and a circuit with a unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set.

If all values of \(w\) are \(\infty\), the edge claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract the minimum value of \(w\). In rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, allowing degenerate cases. Thus for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\),
\[
w(x)=\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j=a\). Hence a matching receives positive cost only from pairs with equal colors \(y_i\). For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: according to the multiplicities of the colors, either all three matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color.

If one line, say \(L_1\), equals \(B\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\) for \(j=2,3,4\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the edge fact.

Applying the rank-three edge fact to the interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

### 5.2 The quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It remains to show that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, we must prove that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1=r-k-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp),
\]
while for \(c\in X\), the term is \(\infty\).

We need the following codimension-one polarity lemma.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((r-k-1)\) flat, and let \(Y=X^\perp\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\Trop(M/X).
\]

Proof. We use the superlevel-flat criterion. It is enough to show that for every \(\lambda\), the set of points
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda
=
\{P\text{ a point of }M/X: u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\).

Because \(M/X\) is modular, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is closed under lines. Indeed, if \(Q\) is a point in the join of a line-closed set, one reaches \(Q\) inductively by repeatedly using modularity to place \(Q\) on a line spanned by two already obtained points.

So take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and because \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3\ge H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), and we are done. Otherwise choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two contraction over \(H_1\wedge H_2\), the three points \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit. Applying (CI) to \(B\) and this circuit gives
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). ∎

Now return to the circuit \(C\) of \(M\). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). More precisely, for every \(c\in C\setminus X\),
\[
c\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}((C\setminus X)\setminus c),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus c)\) and \(X\) is a flat. Hence each element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\).

Since \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. The elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\) to the circuit-incidence relation for \(p^\dagger\). Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Involutivity and flat quotients

### Involutivity

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\subseteq E\) have size \(k\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### Flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). If \(G\) is a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat, then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Polarity exchanges complements:
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,\ G\vee F=E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
G^\perp\vee F^\perp=E,\ G^\perp\wedge F^\perp=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
=
p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 7. Order reversal via the point-annihilator theorem

We now prove that the dagger operation reverses inclusion of tropical linear spaces.

### Boolean apartment duality

Let \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a point of \(M\). For every \(S\subseteq \{1,\ldots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),
\]
and applying polarity converts the meet into the join of the points \(b_i^*\). Hence \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\ldots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis. Also \((B^*)^*=B\), so \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases.

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on the set \(\{1,\ldots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
This is a valuated matroid. Indeed, the Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\), and \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\): every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. To see this, start with an \(M\)-basis containing a finite \(p\)-basis, and move to the desired basis through the connected basis graph of \(M\). If an exchange removes an element of the finite \(p\)-basis, the circuit-incidence relation (CI) supplies a replacement inside the new basis.

The dagger operation restricts on polar apartments to ordinary valuated duality:
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, if \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\ldots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

### Polar hyperplanes

Let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), we may apply the dagger construction to \(x\). Define
\[
\alpha(x):=x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Explicitly, for an \(M\)-independent \((r-1)\)-set \(I\),
\[
\alpha(x)_I
=
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},
\]
where \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp\) is a point of \(M\); and \(\alpha(x)_I=\infty\) if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent.

We call \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) the polar hyperplane of \(x\).

### Point-annihilator theorem

For every rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in\Trop(M)\),
\[
\tag{\(*\)}
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

#### Forward implication

Assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\ldots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
By the ordinary annihilator theorem,
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Now let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Its restriction \(y|_B\) lies in \(\Trop(\mu_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_i (y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
\alpha(x)_{B\setminus b_i}=x_{b_i^*},
\]
so this is exactly the tropical circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\).

It remains to explain why dependent \(r\)-subsets impose no extra conditions. Let \(K\subseteq E\) have size \(r\) and be \(M\)-dependent. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2\), then every \((r-1)\)-subset of \(K\) is dependent, so all terms in the \(\alpha(x)\)-equation are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1\), then \(K\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in
\[
\min_{i\in K}(y_i+\alpha(x)_{K\setminus i})
\]
occur exactly for \(i\in D\), and for those \(i\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(K\setminus i)\) is constant. Hence the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(M)\) on \(D\), which holds because
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M).
\]
Thus \(y\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Since \(y\in\Trop(p)\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

#### Reverse implication

Assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\ldots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\). By the ordinary annihilator theorem, it is enough to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\).

Let \(S\subseteq\{1,\ldots,r\}\) have size \(k-1\), and set
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If \(C_A\) is nonzero, then \(C_A\in\Trop(p)\), hence by assumption
\[
C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Testing the tropical equation for \(\alpha(x)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\). If \(C_A\) is zero, there is nothing to prove.

Therefore
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)
=
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the \(M\)-bases, this shows that \(x\) restricts to \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment.

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). We must prove
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). The equation holds because
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\), the flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)\) are equal. Hence the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\in\Trop(M)\) on \(D\). Thus it holds.

Therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves the point-annihilator theorem \((*)\).

### Order reversal

For a tropical linear space \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then the point-annihilator theorem gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\) is a tropical linear space contained in \(\Trop(M)\), and the definition depends only on \(X\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\),
\[
\Phi^2=\mathrm{id}.
\]
If
\[
X=\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)=Y,
\]
and \(x\in\Phi(Y)=\Trop(q^\dagger)\), then by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
and applying the point-annihilator theorem again gives
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Phi(X).
\]
Therefore
\[
X\subseteq Y\implies \Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
So \(\Phi\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

By the flat-quotient computation from Section 6,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). Thus the theorem is proved in the simple modular case.

---

## 8. Transport back from \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) to \(M\)

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\), possibly with loops and parallel elements. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M),
\]
and let
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
be the poset isomorphism from Section 1. The flat-lattice involution on \(M\) induces an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar\alpha:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
By Sections 2 through 7, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending \(\bar\alpha\).

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is also an order-reversing involution.

Finally, let \(F\in L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S)\). Section 1 showed that
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Since
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\bar F),
\]
we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Trop(S/\bar\alpha(\bar F)\oplus U_{0,\bar\alpha(\bar F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the theorem for the original matroid \(M\). ∎

## Claim

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid, and use the projective convention
\[
\mathbb{TP}^{E}:=\bigl((\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E\setminus\{(\infty,\dots,\infty)\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1).
\]
Let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion. If the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(\Dr(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\) extending it, in the sense that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\) of \(M\).

We prove this claim.

---

### 1. Valuated-matroid preliminaries

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on a finite set \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We use the convention that a minimum is “attained at least twice” if there is no unique finite minimum.

The tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\Trop(p)=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb{TP}^{E}:
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimum for every }J\in\binom E{k+1}
\right\}.
\]
For \(k=0\), this space is empty under the projective convention.

For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
The following standard facts are used repeatedly.

1. **Quotients and inclusion.** If \(p,q\) have ranks \(k\le \ell\), then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) iff
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q).
   \]
   In coordinates this quotient relation is expressed by the tropical incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}\bigr)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimum for all \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\) and \(B\in\binom E{\ell+1}\). Equivalently, all cocircuits \(C_A^p\) satisfy the tropical equations defining \(\Trop(q)\).

2. **Circuit form for quotients of an ordinary matroid.** If \(M\) is an ordinary matroid, viewed as a constant valuated matroid, then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) iff for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C}\, C_A^p(c)
   =
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum.

3. **Boolean annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), with dual \(\mu^*\), and if
   \[
   H_u=\left\{
   v\in\mathbb{TP}^{\Omega}:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\},
   \]
   then
   \[
   u\in \Trop(\mu^*)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]
   Indeed, the defining equations for \(u\in\Trop(\mu^*)\) are precisely tropical orthogonality to every cocircuit \(C_A^\mu\), and \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of its valuated cocircuits.

Thus \(\Dr(M)\) may equivalently be regarded as the set of tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\), where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroid quotients of \(M\).

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the map sending a nonloop to its parallel class.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes, so
\[
F\longmapsto \bar F:=\pi(F\setminus L_0)
\]
is a lattice isomorphism
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
Hence the given polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces one on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb{TP}^{\bar E}\to \mathbb{TP}^{E}
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
The loop circuits \(\{e\}\) force \(x_e=\infty\), and the parallel-pair circuits \(\{e,f\}\) force \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(e\in L_0\), the circuit \(\{e\}\) gives \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\) for every \(A\), so \(e\) is a loop of \(p\). If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops in \(M\), then the circuit \(\{e,f\}\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}
\]
whenever \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\); if \(A\) contains one of \(e,f\), the corresponding \(k\)-set is \(M\)-dependent and has value \(\infty\). Thus each parallel class of \(M\) either becomes entirely looped in \(p\), or remains a zero-offset parallel class.

Consequently \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\), and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Conversely, every valuated quotient of \(S\) pulls back to a valuated quotient of \(M\) by adding \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Moreover, if \(F=L_0\cup\pi^{-1}(\bar F)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence it is enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume from now on that \(M\) is simple, of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats is a finite geometric lattice. Let \(\rho\) denote rank.

A geometric lattice is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution, it reverses rank:
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the opposite inequality. Therefore equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(M\) is modular.

So it remains to construct the involution when \(M\) is simple modular.

---

### 4. Flat-constant coordinates

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of the simple modular matroid \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the quotient circuit relation forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Next suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same closure. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The quotient circuit relation gives
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
with no unique finite minimum, hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of \(M|X\) is connected for every flat \(X\), \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Thus, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

### 5. Two local lemmas

#### Lemma 5.1: Rank-two testing in modular matroids

Let \(N\) be a modular matroid. Let \(z\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) be constant on parallel classes. Then \(z\in\Trop(N)\) iff its restriction to every rank-two flat of \(N\) satisfies the rank-two Bergman fan circuit conditions.

Proof. The forward direction is immediate. Conversely, suppose every rank-two flat satisfies the circuit conditions. For \(t\in\mathbb R\), let
\[
U_t=\{e:z_e\ge t\}.
\]
Because \(z\) is constant on parallel classes and satisfies the rank-two conditions, \(U_t\) is closed under lines: if two atoms of a rank-two flat lie in \(U_t\), then every atom on that line lies in \(U_t\).

In a modular geometric lattice, every line-closed set of atoms is the set of atoms of a flat. Indeed, let \(F\) be the join of a line-closed set \(U\), and let \(e\le F\) be an atom. Choose a minimal set of atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in U\) with
\[
e\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
If \(m\le 2\), line-closure gives \(e\in U\). If \(m>2\), put
\[
A=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad b=a_m.
\]
By minimality, \(e\not\le A\) and \(b\not\le A\). Modularity gives
\[
\rho\bigl(A\wedge(e\vee b)\bigr)
=
\rho(A)+\rho(e\vee b)-\rho(A\vee b)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(A\wedge(e\vee b)\) is an atom \(y\le A\). By induction \(y\in U\), and since \(e\le y\vee b\), line-closure gives \(e\in U\).

Therefore every \(U_t\) is a flat. This is equivalent to saying that no circuit of \(N\) has a unique finite \(z\)-minimum. Hence \(z\in\Trop(N)\). \(\square\)

#### Lemma 5.2: A rank-three matching lemma

Let \(P\) be a simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\Trop(P)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be rank-two flats of \(P\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

Proof. If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, two of the three displayed sums are equal and the remaining one contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(P\) is disconnected, then either \(P\cong U_{1,1}^{\oplus 3}\), in which case there are only three rank-two flats, contradicting distinctness of the four \(L_i\), or
\[
P\cong U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}.
\]
Let \(a\) be the rank-one component and let \(Q\) be the rank-two component. The rank-two flats are \(Q\) and the flats \(\{a,x\}\) for \(x\in Q\). If none of the \(L_i\) is \(Q\), all pairwise meets are \(a\), so all three sums are equal. If one of them is \(Q\), say \(L_1=Q\), and the others are \(\{a,x_2\},\{a,x_3\},\{a,x_4\}\), then the three sums are
\[
w(x_2)+w(a),\quad w(x_3)+w(a),\quad w(x_4)+w(a).
\]
Since \(x_2,x_3,x_4\) form a circuit in \(U_{2,n}\), their \(w\)-minimum is not unique. Hence the matching minimum is not unique.

Now assume \(P\) is connected. Then \(P\) is a projective plane: any two distinct lines meet in a point. If \(w\) is identically \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise, subtract a scalar so that \(\min w=0\). The Bergman fan condition says that all upper level sets \(\{x:w_x\ge t\}\) are flats; in a rank-three projective plane, this means
\[
w=\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
\]
for some point-line flag \(a\le B\), with \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), set
\[
\gamma_i=L_i\cap B.
\]
Then
\[
w(L_i\cap L_j)
=
\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j}
+
\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j=a}.
\]
Thus the three sums are weighted same-color matching costs on four colored objects. Checking the color multiplicities \(4\), \(3+1\), \(2+2\), \(2+1+1\), and \(1+1+1+1\), the minimum is never unique.

If one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), write \(\gamma_i=L_i\cap B\) for \(i=2,3,4\). Each matching contains exactly one pair involving \(B\), contributing a common \(\mu\). After subtracting this common term, a direct check of the three possibilities for \(\gamma_2,\gamma_3,\gamma_4\) shows that the minimum is again not unique: if all three are equal, all sums are equal; if exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two tie for the minimum; if all are distinct, at least two sums are \(0\). \(\square\)

---

### 6. The polar Plücker vector

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}\operatorname{cl}(J)=r-k\), its polar has rank \(k\), so \(\bar p\) is defined there.

#### Lemma 6.1

The function \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Moreover,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
up to global scalar, and for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^F\) denotes the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

Proof. Boundary ranks are immediate: a rank-zero quotient is the all-loop matroid, while a rank-\(r\) quotient of \(M\) is \(M\) itself up to scalar by the flat-constancy proved above. Assume \(1\le k\le r-1\), and set \(m=r-k\).

We first prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{m-2},\qquad a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S
\]
be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), all three Plücker sums contain an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation holds.

Assume none of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=(X\vee i)^\perp=Y\wedge i^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\). If \(H_i\ne H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}=\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the following local statement. Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Set
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23])
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two hyperplanes coincide, the claim is immediate. Assume they are distinct, and put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Because \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\rho(Z)=k-2\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is exactly the desired relation.

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Contract \(Z\) inside \(Y\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a simple modular rank-three matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become rank-two flats of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) become points. Define
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x)
\]
for points \(x\) of \(P\). The quotient circuit relations for \(p\) imply \(w\in\Trop(P)\): choosing a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\), every circuit of \(P\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit contained in \(Z\) together with representatives of that circuit, and the relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\) gives the Bergman circuit condition for \(w\). Lemma 5.2 therefore gives the required matching relation.

Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the Plücker relations.

We now show that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). It is enough to verify the circuit-incidence relations. Let
\[
A\in\binom E{m-1}.
\]
If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), define
\[
f(c)=p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]
We claim that \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\).

By Lemma 5.1, it is enough to check rank-two flats of \(M/X\). Let \(W/X\) be such a flat, and put
\[
Z=W^\perp.
\]
Then \(Z\) has rank \(k-1\). If two elements are parallel in \(W/X\), they have the same \(X\vee c\), hence the same \(f\)-value. Now take three distinct atoms \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) of \(W/X\). Their polars
\[
R_i^\perp
\]
are rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and elements
\[
y_i\in R_i^\perp\setminus Z.
\]
In the rank-two matroid \((M|Y)/Z\), the \(y_i\) form a circuit. Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}
\]
whose elements outside \(Z\) are exactly \(y_1,y_2,y_3\). Applying the quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\) gives
\[
\min_i p_{A_0\cup y_i}
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Since
\[
p_{A_0\cup y_i}=\bar p(R_i^\perp),
\]
this is precisely the rank-two Bergman condition for \(f\) on \(W/X\). Therefore \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\).

Now let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\). Otherwise, \(C\setminus X\) is a dependent set in \(M/X\) with no coloops in its restriction; hence every element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in an \(M/X\)-circuit contained in \(C\setminus X\). Since \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\), the minimum of \(f\) on \(C\setminus X\) is not unique. This is exactly the quotient circuit relation for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Involutivity follows directly. If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}\bigl(\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}(I))
=
p_I.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to global scalar.

Finally let \(F\) be a flat and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). If \(G\) is a rank-\((r-\rho(F))\) flat, then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\vee F=E,\qquad G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Because polarity swaps joins and meets, \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) iff \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
\(\square\)

---

### 7. Polar bases

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\).

For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\), write \(B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}\). The flats \(\operatorname{cl}(B_S)\) form a Boolean sublattice:
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B_S)\wedge \operatorname{cl}(B_T)
=
\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cap T}),
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(B_S)\vee \operatorname{cl}(B_T)
=
\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cup T}).
\]
Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}(B_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S})^\perp.
\]
In particular this flat has rank \(|S|\), so \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis. The same identity shows that the polar-basis construction is involutive: \((B^*)^*=B\).

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) quotient of \(M\). If \(N\) is the underlying matroid of \(p\), then \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), so every flat of \(N\) is a flat of \(M\). Therefore an \(M\)-basis \(B\) cannot lie in a proper \(N\)-flat, and hence \(B\) spans \(N\). Thus the restriction \(p|_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid.

For \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
p_{B_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S}}.
\]
Hence
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

### 8. The point-annihilator theorem

For \(x\in\Trop(M)\), view \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient \(p_x\) of \(M\), with \((p_x)_e=x_e\). Define
\[
\alpha(x):=p_x^\dagger.
\]
Explicitly, \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient of \(M\) with
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp},& I\in\binom E{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

#### Theorem 8.1

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Proof. The case \(k=0\) says
\[
x\in\Trop(M)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\varnothing\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
which is true. The case \(k=r\) follows from the same argument below, with \(p^\dagger\) rank \(0\), so \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\varnothing\). Assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

First suppose \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, with polar basis \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Because \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Fix \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). The formal cocircuit \(C_A^p\) restricts to a vector in \(\Trop(p|_B)\), so
\[
\min_{i=1}^r \bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum. But
\[
\alpha(x)_{B\setminus b_i}
=
x_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}
=
x_{b_i^*},
\]
so this proves the defining equation for \(C_A^p\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on every \(M\)-basis \(B\).

It remains to check dependent \(r\)-subsets \(J\). If \(\rho(J)<r-1\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, so all corresponding \(\alpha(x)\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-1\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite terms in the equation for \(J\) occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\alpha(x)_{J\setminus j}
\]
is constant. Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), the cocircuit \(C_A^p\) satisfies the \(M\)-circuit equation on \(C\). Hence the minimum over \(j\in C\) is not unique. Therefore \(C_A^p\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\) for every \(A\), and by the quotient-inclusion criterion,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, with polar basis \(B^*\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We claim
\[
u_B\in \Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it is enough to check tropical orthogonality to every cocircuit of \(p|_B\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(b_i)=p_{S\cup b_i}
\]
for some \(S\subseteq B\), \(|S|=k-1\). It is the restriction of the corresponding cocircuit \(C_S^p\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\), the vector \(C_S^p\) satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Applying the equation indexed by the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\), proving the claim.

Using
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})=(p|_B)^*,
\]
we get
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
\]
for every polar basis \(B^*\).

Now let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). Since every \(M\)-basis is the polar basis of another, the previous paragraph applies, and the defining equation for \(J\) in \(\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho(J)<r-k\), then all \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\) the values \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) on \(C\) is not unique. Therefore the defining equation holds for every \(J\), so
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
\(\square\)

---

### 9. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then Theorem 8.1 gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\Dr(M)\).

Because \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), we have
\[
\Phi^2(X)=X.
\]
Moreover, if \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

Finally, for a flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^F))
=
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p^{F^\perp})
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
So \(\Phi\) extends the given polarity on the embedded flat subposet.

---

### 10. Return to the original matroid

For a general finite matroid \(M\), let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We proved that
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat subposets and with the induced polarities on \(\mathcal L(S)\) and \(\mathcal L(M)\).

Construct the order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) on \(\Dr(S)\) by the simple modular argument. Define
\[
\Phi_M:=\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\). For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), with image \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the desired involution exists. \(\square\)

## Claim

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid with ground set \(E\), and suppose its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\longrightarrow \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We prove this from first principles, using the standard equivalence between valuated matroids and tropical Plücker vectors.

---

## 1. Conventions

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus conventions.

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), whose finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and which satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations:
for every \(S\in \binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\left(
p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\,
p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\,
p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. We set \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\neq d\).

The tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\Trop(p)=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}:
\text{ for every }J\in\binom E{d+1},
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

For an ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\), we identify \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) iff
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]
Equivalently, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. We will call this the circuit-incidence condition.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Thus the elements of \(S\) are the nonloop parallel classes of \(M\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes. Hence there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
The involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) therefore induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the projection to nonloop parallel classes. Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\Delta\) descends to tropical projective space.

If \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), then \(\Trop(q)\subseteq \Trop(M)\). The equations defining \(\Trop(M)\) force:

1. every loop of \(M\) to be a loop of \(q\);
2. elements in the same nonloop parallel class of \(M\) to be either all loops of \(q\), or valuated-parallel with zero offset.

Indeed, if a loop \(e\) of \(M\) were nonloop in \(q\), some valuated cocircuit of \(q\) would have finite \(e\)-coordinate, giving a point of \(\Trop(q)\) with \(x_e<\infty\), contradicting \(\Trop(q)\subseteq\{x_e=\infty\}\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then \(\Trop(M)\) imposes \(x_e=x_f\). If \(e,f\) were separated in \(q\), a cocircuit of \(q\) would separate their coordinates; if they were valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, their two-element circuit would impose \(x_e-x_f\neq 0\). Both contradict \(x_e=x_f\) on \(\Trop(q)\).

Thus every quotient of \(M\) descends uniquely to a quotient of \(S\), and conversely every quotient of \(S\) has a zero-parallel extension to \(M\). Therefore
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism.

Moreover, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem for simple matroids.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice. Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution, it is isomorphic to its dual lattice. Hence both \(\mathcal L(M)\) and its dual are semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Semimodularity of the dual gives the reverse inequality. Therefore
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(M\) is modular.

From now on, \(M\) is simple modular of rank \(r\), equipped with a polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

---

## 4. Flat-constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

### Lemma 4.1

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\).

**Proof.** Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), and pick \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\). The circuit-incidence condition says that
\[
\min_{x\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup x}=p_I
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p_I=\infty\). ∎

### Lemma 4.2

If \(I,I'\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]

**Proof.** It is enough to consider the case
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying the circuit-incidence condition to \(A\) and \(C\), we obtain that
\[
\min(p_{Aa},p_{Ab})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p_{Aa}=p_{Ab}\), allowing the value \(\infty\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected, so the conclusion follows. ∎

Thus for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

## 5. The formal dagger operation

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank \(r-k\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent and }|J|=r-k,\\[3pt]
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent and }|J|=r-k.
\end{cases}
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N\) be the ordinary support matroid of \(p\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\]
for all \(A\subseteq E\).

Set \(n=r-k\).

---

## 6. The support of \(p^\dagger\) is a matroid

For a flat \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp).
\]
Extend this to arbitrary subsets by
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We check that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
=
0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)
=
0.
\]
Also
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)
=
r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)
=
r-k=n.
\]

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats, then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Therefore
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)-\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0.
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For submodularity, let \(G,H\) be flats. Since \(M\) is modular,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Also, using submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) and the quotient property,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp),
\]
and
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
while
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is submodular. Since \(\rho^\dagger(A)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(A)\le |A|\) and adjoining one element increases \(\rho^\dagger\) by at most one, \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

Let \(N^\dagger\) be the resulting rank \(n\) matroid.

Now let \(J\in\binom En\). Then \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n.
\]
This forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent, and then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)
=
n-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\) iff
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k.
\]
Equivalently, \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat on which \(\bar p\) is finite. Hence
\[
p^\dagger_J<\infty
\]
exactly for the bases \(J\) of \(N^\dagger\). Therefore the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is the set of bases of a matroid.

---

## 7. The tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the three-term tropical Plücker relations.

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\,
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all three terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every displayed summand contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all three terms are \(\infty\).

Assume therefore that \(S\) is independent and that \(a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2.
\]
Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

Thus the desired Plücker relation follows from the following local statement.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23])
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23].
\]
So the claim is immediate.

Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), one has
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then all pairwise intersections \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). Work inside the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For \(i=1,2,3,4\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\). Indeed, in the rank-four interval, the intersection of three hyperplanes has rank at least one, and if it had rank at least two then intersecting with the fourth hyperplane could not give the bottom element \(Z\), by modularity.

For \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[12]=p_{A t_3 t_4},\qquad [34]=p_{A t_1 t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other two pairings. Therefore the required relation is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to consider \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) has rank three. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\), where \([Z,Y]\) is regarded as the rank-three simple matroid whose flats are the interval flats.

Let \(D\) be a circuit in this interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

Now use the following rank-three fact.

If \(N\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(N)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are distinct lines of \(N\), then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, for a vector \(w\) on the points of a matroid, \(w\in\Trop(N)\) iff every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. In rank three, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line. After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\lambda=0\) or \(\mu=0\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put \(y_i=L_i\wedge B\). Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a matching receives positive cost only from pairs of equal colors \(y_i\). Among four colored objects, the minimum total matching cost is never unique. If one line, say \(L_1\), equals \(B\), then the three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). A direct check according to the multiplicities of \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) shows that the minimum is again attained at least twice.

Applying this rank-three fact to the interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the final case, and hence the local lemma. ∎

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. Since its finite support is the set of bases of the matroid \(N^\dagger\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

## 8. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

We must prove the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We need
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For every point \(P\) of the contraction \(M/X\), define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Here \(P\) is a rank \(n\) flat containing \(X\), and \(P^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat contained in \(Y\).

We claim that
\[
u\in\Trop(M/X).
\]

It is enough to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\). Since \(M/X\) is modular, it is enough to show that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Take \(P_1,P_2\in \mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\).

Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\). Their pairwise intersections have rank \(k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval over \(H_1\wedge H_2\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(B\), we get that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, and therefore a flat.

Thus \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\).

Now return to the circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms in the desired minimum are \(\infty\). Otherwise, for every \(c\in C\setminus X\), the image of \(c\) in \(M/X\) lies in a circuit contained in the image of \(C\setminus X\). Since \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\). Hence
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]

So \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 9. Involutivity of dagger

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

---

## 10. Flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), equivalently iff
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

Polarity exchanges complements. Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 11. The annihilator theorem

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Thus \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

For a rank \(k\) quotient \(p\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis contains a basis of the support matroid of \(p\), so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\).

The restriction of dagger to the polar apartment is ordinary valuated duality:
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

Now let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), define its polar hyperplane to be the rank \(r-1\) quotient
\[
x^\perp:=x^\dagger.
\]

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
\boxed{
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp).
}
\]

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(B\), the standard valuated annihilator theorem says
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
\]
where
\[
H_u=\{y:\min_i(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
This follows because the valuated circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex span of its valuated cocircuits.

### Forward implication

Assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*\), we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\Trop(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
x^\perp_{B-b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Thus the tropical hyperplane equation for \(x^\perp\) holds on the basis \(B\).

If \(K\in\binom Er\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2\), in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1\), in which case \(K\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(x^\perp\) occur exactly for \(i\in D\), and for those \(i\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(K-i)\) is constant. The equation therefore reduces to the circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(M)\), which holds.

Hence \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\). Since \(y\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]

### Reverse implication

Assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\).

By the ordinary annihilator theorem, it is enough to test orthogonality against the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu_B\). Let \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(|S|=k-1\), and put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
i\longmapsto p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
e\longmapsto p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If it is nonzero, it lies in \(\Trop(p)\), hence in \(\Trop(x^\perp)\). Testing the equation for \(x^\perp\) on the basis \(B\), we get
\[
\min_i(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\).

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the \(M\)-bases, this means that \(x\) restricts to \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment.

Now let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\). We verify the equation for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation. If \(J\) is dependent with \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le r-k-1\), all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), and the finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in D\). For those \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is constant, so the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Hence
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This proves the annihilator equivalence.

---

## 12. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M):X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then by the annihilator theorem,
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Hence \(\Phi(X)\in\Dr(M)\), and the definition depends only on \(X\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]

If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)
\implies
X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by the flat quotient computation,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

This proves the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

## 13. Transport back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Section 2,
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a poset isomorphism, and the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced polarity.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence \(\Phi_M\) is the desired order-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Work in the projective tropical torus with boundary
\[
\mathbb{TP}^{E}
=\bigl((\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{E}\setminus\{(\infty,\ldots,\infty)\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\ldots,1).
\]
Let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion. Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then \(\Dr(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\) such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(U_{0,F}\) denotes the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is viewed as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\).

---

## 1. Valuated-matroid facts used throughout

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We use the convention that a minimum is “attained at least twice” if there is no unique finite minimizing term. We also allow the formal all-\(\infty\) vector in intermediate affine calculations; it satisfies every “no unique finite minimum” condition, although it is not a point of tropical projective space.

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), its tropical linear space is
\[
\Trop(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb{TP}^{E}:
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimum for every }J\in\binom E{k+1}
\right\}.
\]
For \(k=0\), this space is empty in the above projective convention.

For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the formal valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

### Lemma 1.1: Quotients, inclusion, and annihilators

Let \(p,q\) be valuated matroids on the same finite set \(E\), of ranks \(k\le \ell\).

1. \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q).
   \]
   Equivalently, the tropical incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}\bigr)
   \]
   have no unique finite minimum for every
   \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\) and \(B\in\binom E{\ell+1}\).

2. If \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid, viewed as the constant valuated matroid with values \(0,\infty\), then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C} C_A^p(c)
   =
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum.

3. If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), with dual \(\mu^*\), and
   \[
   H_u=
   \left\{
   v\in\mathbb{TP}^{\Omega}:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\},
   \]
   then
   \[
   u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]

4. If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\), then every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset \(I\) satisfies \(p_I=\infty\). Moreover, if \(N\) is the underlying matroid of \(p\), then every flat of \(N\) is a flat of \(M\).

5. If \(R\subseteq E\) spans the underlying matroid of \(p\), so that \(p|_R\) has rank \(k\), then for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), the restriction \(C_A^p|_R\) satisfies the tropical linear-space equations of \(p|_R\).

Sketch of proof. The cocircuit-generation theorem for valuated matroids says that \(\Trop(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\). Thus \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q)\) is equivalent to every \(C_A^p\) satisfying the equations of \(\Trop(q)\), which are exactly the displayed incidence relations. For ordinary \(M\), the equations of \(\Trop(M)\) are precisely the Bergman circuit equations, giving (2). For (3), \(u\in\Trop(\mu^*)\) means that \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\); by cocircuit generation, this is equivalent to \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u\). For (4), if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\), the circuit relation for \(C\) has only the term \(p_I\), so \(p_I=\infty\). If \(F\) is a flat of the underlying matroid \(N\) of \(p\), \(e\notin F\), and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(F)\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq F\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). Let \(I\) be an \(N\)-basis of \(F\); since \(F\) is \(N\)-flat, \(I\cup\{e\}\) is \(N\)-independent, and it extends to an \(N\)-basis \(B\). Put \(A=B\setminus\{e\}\). In the circuit relation for \(C\), the term indexed by \(e\) is finite, while every term indexed by \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq F\) is infinite, contradiction. Thus \(F\) is \(M\)-closed. Finally, (5) is the Plücker incidence relation of \(p\) with itself, restricted to subsets of \(R\). \(\square\)

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the map sending a nonloop to its parallel class.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(M)=0\), then \(\Trop(M)=\varnothing\), \(\Dr(M)=\{\varnothing\}\), and the theorem is immediate. Hence assume \(\operatorname{rk}(M)>0\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes, so
\[
F\longmapsto \bar F:=\pi(F\setminus L_0)
\]
is a lattice isomorphism
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S).
\]
Thus the given polarity induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb{TP}^{\bar E}\to\mathbb{TP}^{E}
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
The loop circuits \(\{e\}\) force \(x_e=\infty\), and the parallel-pair circuits \(\{e,f\}\) force \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, Lemma 1.1 gives \(p_I=\infty\). In particular, loops of \(M\) are loops of \(p\). If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, and the quotient circuit relation gives
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}
\]
for all \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\) with \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), with both sides possibly \(\infty\). Thus \(p\) is constant on choices of representatives from a parallel class and is infinite on \(k\)-sets containing two elements from the same parallel class.

Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any choice \(I\) of representatives of the classes \(\bar I\). The Plücker relations and the quotient circuit relations for \(\bar p\) are exactly the corresponding relations for \(p\) after choosing representatives, so \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, every valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\) pulls back to a valuated quotient of \(M\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing every element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. The two operations are inverse, and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Moreover, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then the ordinary quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) descends to
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
It is therefore enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple. We do so from now on.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice. Let \(\rho\) denote rank, and let \(\hat 0=\varnothing\), \(\hat 1=E\).

An order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) sends the interval \([\hat 0,F]\) anti-isomorphically onto \([F^\perp,\hat 1]\). Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is graded,
\[
\rho(F)=r-\rho(F^\perp),
\]
so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]

The lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
