Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using rank reversal and De Morgan’s laws, this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

---

## 4. Flat-constancy of quotients in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of the simple modular matroid \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 1.1.

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(b\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{a\})\) but \(b\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so the fundamental circuit of \(b\) with respect to \(A\cup\{a\}\) is contained in \(A\cup\{a,b\}\) and contains both \(a\) and \(b\). The quotient circuit relation for that circuit gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every restriction \(M|X\) is connected, it follows that \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\).

Thus, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). This is well-defined, possibly with value \(\infty\).

---

## 5. Two local lemmas for modular matroids

### Lemma 5.1: Rank-two testing

Let \(N\) be a finite modular matroid. Let \(z\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) be a formal vector such that \(z_e=\infty\) for loops and \(z\) is constant on parallel classes. Then \(z\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(N\) if and only if its restriction to every rank-two flat of \(N\) satisfies the rank-two Bergman circuit conditions.

Proof. Because \(z\) is infinite on loops and constant on parallel classes, we may pass to the simplification of \(N\). Thus assume \(N\) is simple and loopless.

For each real \(t\), set
\[
U_t=\{e\in E(N): z_e\ge t\}.
\]
A vector satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions if and only if every \(U_t\) is a flat. Indeed, if \(U_t\) is not closed, then there is a circuit \(C\subseteq U_t\cup\{e\}\) with \(e\notin U_t\); then \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(z\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(t\) strictly between \(z_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\); then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq U_t\) but \(e\notin U_t\), so \(U_t\) is not closed.

The rank-two hypotheses say precisely that every \(U_t\) is closed under lines: if two atoms of a rank-two flat lie in \(U_t\), then every atom on that line lies in \(U_t\).

It remains to use modularity. Let \(U\) be a line-closed set of atoms, and let \(F=\bigvee U\). We show every atom \(e\le F\) lies in \(U\). Choose a minimal subset \(\{a_1,\dots,a_m\}\subseteq U\) with
\[
e\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
By minimality, the \(a_i\) are independent. If \(m\le 2\), then line-closure gives \(e\in U\). Suppose \(m>2\). Let
\[
A=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad b=a_m.
\]
Then \(e\not\le A\), \(b\not\le A\), and \(e\le A\vee b\). Modularity gives
\[
\rho\bigl(A\wedge(e\vee b)\bigr)
=
\rho(A)+\rho(e\vee b)-\rho(A\vee b)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(A\wedge(e\vee b)\) is an atom \(y\le A\). By induction on \(m\), \(y\in U\). Since \(y,b\in U\) and \(e\le y\vee b\), line-closure gives \(e\in U\). Hence every line-closed set of atoms is the set of atoms of a flat, proving the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### Lemma 5.2: A rank-three matching lemma

Let \(P\) be a simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(P)}\) satisfy the Bergman circuit conditions of \(P\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be rank-two flats of \(P\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

Proof. If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, then two of the three displayed sums are equal and the remaining sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(P\) is disconnected, then either
\[
P\cong U_{1,1}^{\oplus 3}
\]
or
\[
P\cong U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}.
\]
In the first case there are only three rank-two flats, contradicting distinctness of the four \(L_i\). In the second case, let \(a\) be the rank-one component and let \(Q\) be the rank-two component. The rank-two flats are \(Q\) and the flats \(a\vee x\) for \(x\in Q\). If none of the \(L_i\) is \(Q\), all pairwise meets are \(a\), so all three sums are equal. If one of them is \(Q\), say \(L_1=Q\), then \(L_i=a\vee x_i\) for \(i=2,3,4\), and the three sums are
\[
w(x_2)+w(a),\qquad
w(x_3)+w(a),\qquad
w(x_4)+w(a).
\]
Since \(x_2,x_3,x_4\) form a circuit of the rank-two uniform component \(Q\), the minimum among \(w(x_2),w(x_3),w(x_4)\) is not unique.

Now assume \(P\) is connected. Then any two distinct lines meet in a point, because modularity gives
\[
\rho(L\wedge L')=\rho(L)+\rho(L')-\rho(L\vee L')=2+2-3=1.
\]
Thus \(P\) is a projective plane in the matroidal sense.

If \(w\) is identically \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise subtract \(\min w\), so \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 5.1, all upper level sets of \(w\) are flats. In a rank-three projective plane, a chain of proper flats has the form
\[
\varnothing \subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B\subseteq E
\]
for a point-line flag \(a\le B\). Therefore
\[
w=\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let
\[
\gamma_i=L_i\cap B.
\]
Then
\[
w(L_i\cap L_j)
=
\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j}
+
\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j=a}.
\]
Thus the three displayed sums are matching costs for four colored objects, where pairing two objects of different colors has cost \(0\), pairing two objects of the same color \(\gamma\ne a\) has cost \(\mu\), and pairing two objects of color \(a\) has cost \(\mu+\lambda\). Checking the possible color multiplicities \(4\), \(3+1\), \(2+2\), \(2+1+1\), and \(1+1+1+1\), the minimum is never unique.

If one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), write
\[
\gamma_i=L_i\cap B,\qquad i=2,3,4.
\]
If all three \(\gamma_i\) are equal, all three sums are equal. If exactly two are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two lines tie for the minimum. If the three \(\gamma_i\) are distinct, at most one equals \(a\), so at least two of the three sums have the same smallest value. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

## 6. The polar transform on valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of the simple modular matroid \(M\). Put \(m=r-k\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \(m\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(J\) is independent of size \(m\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(m\), and its polar has rank \(k\), so \(\bar p\) is defined there.

### Proposition 6.1

The function \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(m\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Moreover,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
as valuated matroids up to global tropical scalar. Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^F\) denotes the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

Proof. First, \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Choose \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). Then \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose a basis \(J\) of \(X^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(X)<\infty.
\]

The boundary cases are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(p\) is the rank-zero all-loop valuated matroid, and \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\) up to scalar. If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is \(M\) up to scalar, and \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Hence assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{m-2},\qquad a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S
\]
be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation holds. Thus assume none lies in \(X\).

For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=(X\vee i)^\perp=Y\wedge i^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and therefore \(p^\dagger_{Sij}=\infty\). If \(H_i\ne H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}=\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Thus the Plücker relation reduces to the following local statement.

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Put
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two hyperplanes coincide, the claim is immediate. Assume they are distinct, and set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since each \(H_i\) has corank one in \(Y\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) for \(i\ne j\), we have
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\). For \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), one has
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Indeed, \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), their meet is \(Z\), and modularity gives
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k
=
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is exactly the required relation.

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification \(P\) of \((M|Y)/Z\). It is a simple modular rank-three matroid. The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become rank-two flats of \(P\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) becomes their point of intersection. Define
\[
w(R)=\bar p(R)
\]
for every point \(R\) of \(P\), i.e. every rank-\(k\) flat \(R\) with \(Z<R<Y\).

We claim that \(w\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(P\). Let \(R_1,\dots,R_s\) be a circuit of \(P\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and representatives \(y_i\in R_i\setminus Z\). The set \(\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}\) is a circuit of the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\), so it lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}
\]
with \(D\setminus Z=\{y_1,\dots,y_s\}\). Applying the quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\), the terms from \(D\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), because \(A_0\) spans \(Z\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A_0\cup y_i}=\bar p(R_i)=w(R_i).
\]
Thus \(w\) satisfies the circuit condition on \(R_1,\dots,R_s\). Hence \(w\in\Trop(P)\) in the affine sense. Lemma 5.2 gives the desired matching relation.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to verify the circuit quotient relations. Let
\[
A\in\binom E{m-1}.
\]
If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+1\). Define a formal vector \(f\) on \(M/X\) by
\[
f(c)=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& c\in X,\\
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp),& c\notin X.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(c\notin X\), this equals \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\).

We claim \(f\) satisfies the Bergman circuit conditions of \(M/X\). It is constant on parallel classes because parallel elements in \(M/X\) have the same flat \(X\vee c\). By Lemma 5.1, it suffices to check rank-two flats of \(M/X\).

Let \(W/X\) be a rank-two flat of \(M/X\), and put
\[
Z=W^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Let \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) be three distinct atoms of \(W/X\). Their polars \(R_i^\perp\) are rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and elements
\[
y_i\in R_i^\perp\setminus Z.
\]
In the rank-two matroid \((M|Y)/Z\), the elements \(y_1,y_2,y_3\) form a circuit. Therefore there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}
\]
with \(D\setminus Z=\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}\). Applying the quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\), the terms from \(Z\) are \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_i \bar p(R_i^\perp)
\]
has no unique finite minimum. This is exactly the rank-two Bergman condition for \(f\) on \(W/X\). Thus \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\) in the affine sense.

Now let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a dependent set in \(M/X\) with no coloops: for each \(c\in C\setminus X\),
\[
c\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus\{c\}\bigr).
\]
If \(f\) had a unique finite minimum on \(C\setminus X\), then that minimum element would lie in some circuit of \((M/X)|(C\setminus X)\), contradicting \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\). Hence the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
over \(c\in C\) has no unique finite minimum. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Now we prove involutivity. Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}(I))
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to the irrelevant global tropical scalar.

Finally let \(F\) be a flat, and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). A rank-\((r-\rho(F))\) flat \(G\) satisfies
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), namely
\[
G\vee F=E,\qquad G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, finite bases of \(p^F\) are precisely bases of the contraction \(M/F\), and modularity gives the meet condition from the rank count:
\[
\rho(G\wedge F)=\rho(G)+\rho(F)-\rho(E)=0.
\]
Polarity swaps joins and meets, so \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
\(\square\)

---

## 7. Polar bases

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a rank-one flat, hence a unique element of \(E\), because \(M\) is simple.

For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\), write
\[
B_S=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The flats
\[
F_S:=\operatorname{cl}(B_S)
\]
form a Boolean sublattice. Indeed,
\[
F_S\vee F_T=F_{S\cup T},
\]
and modularity gives
\[
\rho(F_S\wedge F_T)
=
|S|+|T|-|S\cup T|
=
|S\cap T|,
\]
so
\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Applying the polarity to this Boolean sublattice yields
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
F_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S}^{\perp}.
\]
In particular, this flat has rank \(|S|\), so
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis. The same identity shows that the polar-basis construction is involutive:
\[
(B^*)^*=B.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Let \(N\) be its underlying matroid. By Lemma 1.1, every \(N\)-flat is an \(M\)-flat. Therefore an \(M\)-basis cannot be contained in a proper \(N\)-flat, so every \(M\)-basis spans \(N\). Hence \(p|_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid.

For \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p(F_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S})
=
p_{B_{\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

## 8. The point-annihilator theorem

For \(x\in\Trop(M)\), view \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient \(p_x\) of \(M\), with
\[
(p_x)_e=x_e.
\]
Define
\[
\alpha(x):=p_x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient of \(M\), with
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp},& I\in\binom E{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

### Theorem 8.1

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Proof. If \(k=0\), then \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\), while \(p^\dagger=M\), so both sides are true.

If \(k=r\), then \(p=M\) up to scalar and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus the left side is false. We show the right side is also false. Choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\), extend \(e\) to a basis \(B^*\), and let \(B=(B^*)^*\). Write \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The valuated cocircuit \(C_A^M\) is a point of \(\Trop(M)\). In the defining equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) indexed by the basis \(B\), all terms are \(\infty\) except the one indexed by \(b_i\), which equals \(x_e<\infty\). Hence \(C_A^M\notin\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)\nsubseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Now assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

First suppose \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and let \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) be the polar basis. Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Fix \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). By Lemma 1.1, the restriction \(C_A^p|_B\) satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(p|_B)\). Hence
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum. Since
\[
\alpha(x)_{B\setminus b_i}
=
x_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}
=
x_{b_i^*},
\]
this is exactly the equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) indexed by the basis \(B\) for the vector \(C_A^p\).

It remains to check equations indexed by dependent \(r\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\). If \(\rho(J)<r-1\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, so all corresponding \(\alpha(x)\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-1\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite \(\alpha(x)\)-terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\), the values \(\alpha(x)_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), the minimum of \(C_A^p\) on \(C\) has no unique finite minimum. Therefore \(C_A^p\) satisfies every defining equation of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\).

Thus every valuated cocircuit of \(p\) lies in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, with polar basis \(B^*\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it suffices to check orthogonality to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(b_i)=p_{S\cup b_i}
\]
for some \(S\subseteq B\), \(|S|=k-1\), and is the restriction of \(C_S^p\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\), the vector \(C_S^p\) satisfies the equations of \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Applying the equation indexed by the basis \(B\), we obtain
\[
\min_{i=1}^r \bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is tropically orthogonal to \(u_B\), proving
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]

Using
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*,
\]
we get
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
\]
for every \(M\)-basis \(B\). Since the polar-basis construction is involutive, every \(M\)-basis occurs as some \(B^*\).

Now let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). The equation for \(J\) in \(\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\rho(J)<r-k\), then all terms \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are \(\infty\). If \(\rho(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\), the values \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) over \(j\in C\) has no unique finite minimum. Hence the defining equation holds for every \(J\), and therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
\(\square\)

---

## 9. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), Theorem 8.1 gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\Dr(M)\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), we get
\[
\Phi^2(X)=X.
\]
Moreover, if \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x))
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

Finally, for a flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^F))
=
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p^{F^\perp})
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the theorem holds for simple \(M\).

---

## 10. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). Section 2 gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
compatible with the embedded flat subposets. The polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\), and the simple case gives an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi_M:=\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr),
\]
and \(\bar F^\perp\) corresponds to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

## Claim

For every finite matroid \(M\), if the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(\Dr(M)\) is the poset, ordered by inclusion, of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\).

---

## Proof

We use min-plus tropical conventions. Write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on a finite set \(E\) is written as a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to\mathbb T,
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We extend \(p\) by \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\ne k\).

Its tropical linear space is
\[
\Trop(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb{TP}^{E-1}:
\text{ for every }J\in\binom E{k+1},
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

For an ordinary rank \(r\) matroid \(M\), we regard \(M\) as the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

A valuated matroid \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) if
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]
Thus \(\Dr(M)\) is the collection of tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\) for valuated quotients \(p\) of \(M\), ordered by inclusion.

---

### 1. Quotients and circuit incidence

We first record the quotient criterion used throughout.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), define the valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e}.
\]
Then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\) if and only if, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, \(\Trop(M)\) is cut out by the ordinary circuit equations
\[
\min_{c\in C} x_c
\quad\text{has no unique finite minimizer}
\]
for circuits \(C\) of \(M\). Also, \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits \(C_A\), and ordinary tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex. Therefore \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to every valuated cocircuit \(C_A\) satisfying every circuit equation of \(M\), which is precisely the displayed condition.

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the \(k\)-subsets \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). The support-level consequence of the same criterion is that \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Concretely,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad
\text{for all }A\subseteq E.
\]

To see this, let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If some \(e\in C\) were a coloop of \(N|C\), choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), and choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\). Then \(A\cup e\) is an \(N\)-basis, while \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent for every \(c\in C-e\). Thus the circuit-incidence minimum for \(C\) and \(A\) would have a unique finite minimizer, contradiction. Hence no element of an \(M\)-circuit is a coloop in its restriction to \(N\), so every \(M\)-circuit is a union of \(N\)-circuits. Therefore, if \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), some \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup e\) contains \(e\), and then an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\) contains \(e\). Thus \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). This proves
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(A),
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We will also use the following consequence: if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\). Its ground set \(\bar E\) is the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of nonloop parallel classes. Hence
\[
\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S),
\]
and the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces one on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This descends to tropical projective space.

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(e\) is an \(M\)-loop, then \(\{e\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, and the circuit-incidence condition forces
\[
p_{A\cup e}=\infty
\]
for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\). Hence every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\).

If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. Thus for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\) disjoint from \(\{e,f\}\),
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Also, if a \(k\)-set contains a loop or two elements from the same parallel class, then it is \(M\)-dependent, and the circuit-incidence criterion forces its \(p\)-value to be \(\infty\). Therefore \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I.
\]
The Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and the quotient criterion for \(S\) follows from the quotient criterion for \(M\).

Conversely, if \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi\text{ is injective on }I,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce either to those for \(q\), or to trivial relations with two equal terms or all terms \(\infty\). Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid. The circuit-incidence criterion for \(M\) follows from the corresponding criterion for \(S\), together with the loop and parallel two-element circuit cases. Hence \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Moreover,
\[
\Trop(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\Trop(q)).
\]
Indeed, the tropical linear equations for loops impose \(x_e=\infty\), and the equations for zero-parallel pairs impose equality of the corresponding coordinates. Therefore \(\Delta\) identifies the quotient tropical linear spaces of \(S\) with those of \(M\), preserving inclusions.

Finally, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-parallel extension of the constant valuated matroid of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]

Therefore it is enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume from now on that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Then \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular. Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution, its dual lattice is isomorphic to \(\mathcal L(M)\), so the dual lattice is also semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) denote the rank function on flats. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\ge
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while semimodularity of the dual gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(M\) is modular.

The involution satisfies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F)
\]
for every flat \(F\).

---

### 4. Flat-constancy of quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the circuit-incidence condition forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Second, if \(I,I'\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]
It suffices to treat the case
\[
I=A\cup a,
\qquad
I'=A\cup b.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab)=F,
\]
there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(C\) and \(A\), the minimum of
\[
p_{A\cup a},\quad p_{A\cup b}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence they are equal, possibly both \(\infty\). Connectivity of the basis graph of \(M|F\) gives the result.

Thus for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

### 5. The dagger operation

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). By the quotient criterion, \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\).

Define a function on flats \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\) by
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and extend it to arbitrary subsets by
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
=
0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)
=
0,
\]
and
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)
=
r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)
=
n.
\]

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp).
\]
Using
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
we get
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)-\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0.
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone on flats, hence on all subsets.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp).
\]
Now
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\wedge H)^\perp).
\]
Therefore
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]

For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), let \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\) and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
Using monotonicity and the flat submodularity just proved, we obtain
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)+\rho^\dagger(B)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(A\cap B)+\rho^\dagger(A\cup B).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is submodular.

Also,
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))-k
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(A),
\]
because \(\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)\le k\). Hence
\[
0\le \rho^\dagger(A)\le |A|.
\]
Submodularity implies
\[
\rho^\dagger(A\cup e)-\rho^\dagger(A)
\le
\rho^\dagger(\{e\})-\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)
\le 1.
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function. Let \(N^\dagger\) be the resulting rank \(n\) matroid.

For \(J\in\binom En\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) if and only if
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n.
\]
Since \(\rho^\dagger(J)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n\), this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. Then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)
=
n-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),
\]
so \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\) if and only if
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the set of bases of \(N^\dagger\).

It remains to check the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient condition.

---

### 6. Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations.

Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all six \(n\)-sets appearing are \(M\)-dependent, so all three displayed terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then in each displayed summand at least one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent, so again all three terms are \(\infty\).

Assume therefore that \(S\) is independent and
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent, so
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty.
\]
If \(H_e\ne H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

Thus the desired Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

---

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Hence the second and third sums are equal, and the claim follows.

Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then each pairwise intersection \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) and contains \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). Therefore all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), let
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Then \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\). Indeed, in a modular rank-four interval, the meet of three hyperplanes has rank at least \(1\) over the bottom; if it had rank at least \(2\), then meeting with the fourth hyperplane could not give the bottom.

Moreover, if \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[12]=p_{A t_3 t_4},
\qquad
[34]=p_{A t_1 t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other pairings. Hence the desired relation is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

It remains to treat the case \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Then \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three modular geometry. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We claim \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the rank-three interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we get that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three fact. If \(G\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(G)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are lines of \(G\), then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, for a vector \(w\) on the points of a simple matroid, \(w\in\Trop(G)\) if and only if every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. In rank three, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a chain of the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, allowing either \(a\) or \(B\) to be absent. After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\).

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\). Thus the cost of pairing \(i,j\) is a nonnegative number depending only on the common color \(y_i=y_j\), and is \(0\) if the colors differ. For four colored objects, among the three perfect matchings, the minimum total equal-color cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case at least two matchings have minimal cost.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), then for \(i=2,3,4\) put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\ne a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is again attained at least twice.

Applying this rank-three fact to the interval \([Z,Y]\), with \(L_i=H_i/Z\), proves the local lemma.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

---

### 7. The quotient condition for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). The cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate from the definition, so assume \(0<k<r\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let
\[
A\in\binom E{n-1}.
\]
We must prove that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For every point \(P\) of the contraction \(M/X\), equivalently every rank \(n\) flat \(P\) with \(X<P\), define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Here \(P^\perp\) is a rank \(k\) flat contained in \(Y\).

We claim that \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\), regarded on the simple point geometry of \(M/X\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat.

A set of points in a finite simple modular matroid is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if \(S\) is line-closed, prove by induction on \(\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl}S)\) that \(S\) contains every point of its closure. Choose an independent spanning set \(a_1,\dots,a_m\subseteq S\), let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}),
\]
and use induction to get all points of \(H\) in \(S\). If \(x\in\operatorname{cl}S\setminus H\), modularity implies that the line \(\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Since \(h,a_m\in S\), line-closedness gives \(x\in S\).

So let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be any point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2
\]
implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\).

Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\), all containing
\[
Z:=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
which has rank \(k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([Z,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) to \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, therefore a flat. Thus \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\).

Now return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms in
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
are \(\infty\). Otherwise set
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
For \(c\in C\setminus A\) with \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so the corresponding term is \(\infty\).

We claim every \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D-c)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(X\cup C-c)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(X\cup C)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Hence \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\), and so it is contained in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\).

If the minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then restricting to a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would contradict \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). Therefore the circuit-incidence condition holds for \(p^\dagger\). Hence
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 8. Involutivity of dagger

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\) by definition.

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore dagger is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

---

### 9. Flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is
\[
r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

Let \(G\) be a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), which is equivalent to
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)=r
\]
and \(M\) is modular, this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

The polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) exchanges complements. Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

### 10. The annihilator description

We now show that the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) depends only on the tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp,
\]
and the meet inside the parentheses is
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
Thus \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\). Hence \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since the support \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis contains an \(N\)-basis; hence \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\):
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

We use the ordinary valuated annihilator theorem: for a valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(B\),
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
\]
where
\[
H_u=\{y:\min_i(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
This follows because the circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\), \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(H_u\) is tropically convex.

Now let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Regard \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), and define
\[
x^\perp:=x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(x^\perp\) is a rank \(r-1\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp).
\]

First assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*\), we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
By the ordinary annihilator theorem,
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\Trop(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But for the rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid \(x^\perp\),
\[
(x^\perp)_{B-b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore the defining tropical equation for \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\) holds on every \(M\)-basis \(B\).

It remains only to check dependent \(r\)-subsets \(K\subseteq E\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2\), then every \((r-1)\)-subset \(K-e\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1\), then \(K\) has a unique circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(x^\perp\) occur exactly for \(e\in D\), and for those \(e\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(K-e)
\]
is the same rank \(r-1\) flat. Hence the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(M)\), which holds because \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\). Thus \(y\in\Trop(x^\perp)\). Since \(y\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\).

It suffices to test the circuit equations of \(\mu_B^*\), equivalently the cocircuit equations of \(\mu_B\). Let \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(|S|=k-1\), and put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
i\longmapsto p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
e\longmapsto p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If this vector is not identically \(\infty\), it lies in \(\Trop(p)\), hence in \(\Trop(x^\perp)\). Testing the defining equation of \(\Trop(x^\perp)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Therefore
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, it follows that \(x\) restricts to \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

Now let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
We verify the defining equation for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on \(J\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le r-k-1\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally, suppose \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\), the flats
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
are all equal. Hence the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This proves the equivalence.

---

### 11. Construction of the involution in the simple case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) with
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
The annihilator equivalence shows that
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq \Trop(x^\perp)\},
\]
so \(\Phi(X)\) depends only on \(X\), not on the choice of \(p\).

Because dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp)
\implies
X\subseteq\Trop(x^\perp),
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by the flat quotient computation,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

This proves the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

### 12. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From the simplification reduction, there is a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M),
\]
and the involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced involution on flats.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is also an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\), and
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence the desired order-reversing involution exists for every finite matroid \(M\). ∎

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following statement, which is exactly the original problem with explicit conventions. Let \(E\) be a finite set and let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with tropical scalar multiplication given by adding a real constant to all finite coordinates and leaving \(\infty\) fixed. Let \(\mathbb{TP}^{E-1}=(\mathbb T^E\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R\mathbf 1\). For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\mathbb T^E\) be its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector, and let \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) be its projectivization, possibly empty when \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\). For an ordinary matroid \(M\) on \(E\), let \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) mean the projective tropical linear space of its constant valuated matroid. Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) be the poset, ordered by inclusion, of all projective tropical linear spaces \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), equivalently all \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) for valuated matroid quotients \(p\leftarrow M\). If the lattice of flats \(L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), and if a flat \(F\) is embedded in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) by
\[
F\longmapsto \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
where elements of \(F\) are added back as loops, then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to\operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). This is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 0. Conventions and standard facts

Let \(E\) be finite. We use
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
with tropical addition \(a\oplus b=\min(a,b)\) and tropical multiplication \(a\odot b=a+b\). Tropical projective space is
\[
\mathbb{TP}^{E-1}=(\mathbb T^E\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R\mathbf 1,
\]
where \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\) sends \(x=(x_e)\) to \(x+\lambda\mathbf 1\), with \(\infty+\lambda=\infty\).

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom E k\to\mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We write \(p_I=\infty\) for subsets \(I\) of size different from \(k\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer for every } |J|=k+1
\right\}.
\]
The projective tropical linear space is
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)
=
\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
For a rank-zero valuated matroid, the affine tropical space is \(\{\infty^E\}\), so the projective tropical space is empty.

An ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ is an }M\text{-basis},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same ground set, with ranks \(k\le \ell\), we write
\[
p\leftarrow q
\]
and say that \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) if the usual valuated flag incidence relations hold:
\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer for all \(|A|=k-1\), \(|B|=\ell+1\).

We use the standard theorem:

> **Quotient-containment theorem.**  
> For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on \(E\),
> \[
> p\leftarrow q
> \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
> \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
> \]
> Equivalently, after deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and projectivizing,
> \[
> \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(q).
> \]

Thus
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)
=
\{\operatorname{Trop}(p):p\leftarrow M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion.

We also use the standard cocircuit fact for valuated matroids: if \(p\) has rank \(k\), then for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the vector
\[
C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e}
\]
with \(C_A^p(e)=\infty\) when \(e\in A\), is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\) unless it is identically \(\infty\), and in any case belongs to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\).

---

# Step 1. Reduction to the simple modular case

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops, and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

## 1.1. Flat lattices

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if it contains one element of a non-loop parallel class, it contains the whole class. Therefore every flat of \(M\) is of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a unique flat \(\bar F\) of \(S\).

Define
\[
\psi:L(M)\to L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Its inverse is
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup\pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Indeed, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(F\) is closed in \(M\) if and only if \(\psi(F)\) is closed in \(S\). Hence \(\psi\) is a canonical lattice isomorphism.

If \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(L(S)\) inherits one:
\[
\bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]

## 1.2. The simplification is modular

Because \(S\) is simple, \(L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is finite, ranked, atomistic, and semimodular. Since \(L(S)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while lower semimodularity gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(L(S)\) is modular, and \(S\) is a simple modular matroid.

## 1.3. Zero-parallel extension and the Dressian

Define the diagonal map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(\bar p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(\Delta\bar p\) to \(E\) by
\[
(\Delta\bar p)_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar p_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is the valuated matroid obtained by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing every element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class.

From the circuit equations one obtains
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\Delta\bar p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loop circuits force coordinates of \(L_0\) to be \(\infty\), two-element parallel circuits force equality of coordinates inside each parallel class, and after imposing these equations, the remaining circuit equations are exactly those of \(\bar p\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

We now prove that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

First, if \(\bar p\leftarrow S\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S),
\]
so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\Delta\bar p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Thus \(\Delta\bar p\leftarrow M\).

Conversely, let \(q\leftarrow M\) have rank \(k\). We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated quotient of \(S\).

If \(e\in L_0\), then every point of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\). If \(e\) were not a loop of \(q\), then some \(q\)-basis \(B\) would contain \(e\). With \(A=B\setminus e\), the cocircuit \(C_A^q\) satisfies
\[
C_A^q(e)=q_B<\infty,
\]
contradicting
\[
C_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Therefore every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

Now let \(e,f\) be parallel non-loops in \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). For every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f),
\]
so
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
It follows that \(e\) and \(f\) are either both loops of \(q\), or neither is; if neither is, then no \(q\)-basis can contain both \(e\) and \(f\), and replacing \(e\) by \(f\) in a \(q\)-basis preserves the valuation.

Therefore \(q\) is uniquely the zero-parallel extension of a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I
\]
for any choice of representatives \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\). The preceding equalities make this well-defined, and the tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those of \(q\).

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\leftarrow S\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) gives a bijection
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar p)\mapsto \operatorname{Trop}(\Delta\bar p).
\]
Since \(\Delta\) is injective on affine cones modulo tropical scaling, this bijection preserves and reflects inclusion. Hence it is a poset isomorphism.

## 1.4. Compatibility with flats

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and put
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
be the matroid on \(E\) obtained from \(M/F\) by adding back the elements of \(F\) as loops. Similarly define
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]

For every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F).
\]
Using the rank formula for simplification,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(F)=\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
So \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), and
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(N_F)
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar N_{\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore the poset isomorphism \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) carries the embedded flat \(\bar F\) to the embedded flat \(F\).

Consequently it remains to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple modular.

---

# Step 2. The simple modular case and the dagger construction

Assume from now until Step 6 that \(M\) is simple modular of rank \(r\) on \(E\), and that \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Since \(L(M)\) is ranked and \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) reverses maximal chains,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

Let \(p\leftarrow M\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient.

## 2.1. Circuit incidence

The quotient relation \(p\leftarrow M\) is equivalent to the following circuit-incidence relations:

\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\quad\text{has no unique finite minimizer}
\]
for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the flag relation for \(p\leftarrow M\) says that for every \(|A|=k-1\) and every \(|T|=r+1\),
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+M_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), then all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C_T\), and the finite terms are exactly those with \(i\in C_T\). Hence the flag relation is precisely (CI). Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) occurs as such a \(C_T\): extend \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and take \(T=B\cup c\).

## 2.2. Flat constancy

First, if \(I\subseteq E\) has size \(k\) and is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I\setminus c\), we have \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), it follows that \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of each rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

Thus we may define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

## 2.3. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Define a rank-\((r-k)\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,&
J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), then \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and \(\bar p\) is defined there.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis, then \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) has rank \(k\), and any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

---

# Step 3. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\)

We prove first that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, and then that it satisfies the quotient relations with \(M\).

The boundary cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(p\) is rank zero and \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to an additive scalar. If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is an additive scalar multiple of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Hence assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

## 3.1. The local dual Plücker lemma

We need the following local statement.

### Lemma 3.1.1 — Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), and
\[
[13]+[24]=[13]+[14]=[14]+[23].
\]
Thus the minimum is not uniquely attained. So assume the \(H_i\) are distinct.

Since \(Y\) is modular, any two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with four hyperplanes lowers rank by at most four, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

**Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\).**  
Every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank-\(k\) flat containing \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). Therefore all three sums are equal.

**Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\).**  
Let
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Also \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_m\cup t_n)=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly the desired relation.

**Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\).**  
The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). The representatives \(e_P\) form a circuit in the contraction by \(Z\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) whose image is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while \(e_P\) contributes \(w(P)\). Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

We now use the following elementary rank-three fact.

### Lemma 3.1.2 — Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). For four distinct hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\),
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^E\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\) if and only if every superlevel set
\[
E_\lambda=\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. This is the standard Bergman fan criterion: a non-flat superlevel set produces a circuit with a unique minimum, and conversely a circuit with a unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set.

In rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a chain of length at most two, namely a point \(a\) contained in a line \(B\). After subtracting the global minimum of \(w\), we may write
\[
w=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{a\}}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\{x:x\le B\}}
\]
for some point \(a\le B\), with \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\). Missing members of the flag are allowed by taking the corresponding coefficient to be \(0\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\). Also \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs with equal colors \(y_i\). For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: the multiplicity types are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4,
\]
and in each case either all matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color equally.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(a)=\lambda+\mu,\qquad c(y)=\mu\text{ for }y\ne a.
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal \(\mu\), so the minimum is tied. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

Returning to Case 3, apply Lemma 3.1.2 to the four hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) of the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). Since
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
is the point \( (H_i/Z)\wedge(H_j/Z)\) in that interval, Lemma 3.1.2 gives the desired relation.

This completes the proof of Lemma 3.1.1. \(\square\)

## 3.2. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now verify the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every displayed sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial. Thus assume all four elements lie outside \(X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent and
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}=\infty.
\]
If \(H_e\ne H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-independent and
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly Lemma 3.1.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n=r-k\) valuated matroid.

## 3.3. A codimension-one polarity lemma

It remains to prove \(p^\dagger\leftarrow M\).

We use one more lemma.

### Lemma 3.3.1 — Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1=r-k-1\), and put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(Y\) has rank \(k+1\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp)
\]
for \(e\notin X\). Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X).
\]

#### Proof

We use the superlevel-flat criterion. It is enough to prove that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set of points
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of the simplification of \(M/X\).

Because \(M\) is modular, every contraction of \(M\) is modular. In a simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is closed under lines. Indeed, if a set of points is closed under lines, then by induction on rank it contains every point in the join of any finite subset: if \(q\le F\vee a\) with \(a\) a point and \(q\not\le F\), then modularity gives
\[
(a\vee q)\wedge F
\]
as a point \(b\le F\), so \(q\le a\vee b\), and line-closure puts \(q\) in the set.

Thus it suffices to prove that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2
\]
and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus Z\) so that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the contraction by \(A\), the elements \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit in a rank-two flat. Lifting back to \(M\), there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus A=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), we get
\[
\min\bigl(\bar p(H_1),\bar p(H_2),\bar p(H_3)\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
u(P_3)\ge \min(u(P_1),u(P_2)).
\]
In particular, if \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), then \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\). Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). \(\square\)

## 3.4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\) with \(M\).

Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. We must show
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]

By Lemma 3.3.1, the function
\[
u(X\vee c)=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

Suppose, for contradiction, that the displayed minimum over \(c\in C\setminus A\) has a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). In the contraction \(M/X\), every element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in the closure of the others, because \(C\) was a circuit of \(M\). Hence the point \(X\vee c_0\) lies in a circuit of the simplification of \(M/X\) contained in the set of points represented by \(C\setminus X\). On that circuit, \(u\) has a unique finite minimum, contradicting
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X).
\]
Therefore the circuit-incidence relation holds.

By the equivalence of circuit incidence with \(p^\dagger\leftarrow M\), we have proved:
\[
p^\dagger\leftarrow M.
\]

---

# Step 4. Involutivity and embedded flats

## 4.1. Involutivity

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\subseteq E\) have size \(k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F\) has rank \(k\), and \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

## 4.2. Embedded flats

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in the modular lattice \(L(M)\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\) exactly when it is disjoint from \(F\) and together with \(F\) spans \(E\); by modularity this is exactly the complement condition.

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\), because
\[
G\wedge F=0,\quad G\vee F=E
\]
is equivalent, after applying \(\perp\), to
\[
G^\perp\vee F^\perp=E,\quad G^\perp\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Therefore \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite exactly on the complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

# Step 5. Order reversal via point-annihilators

We now prove that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(q)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

The proof uses Boolean apartments and ordinary tropical annihilator duality.

## 5.1. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis

Let \(p\leftarrow M\) have rank \(k\). We claim that every \(M\)-basis \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). By Step 2, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, so it extends to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). The basis graph of \(M\) is connected, so connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) by basis exchanges.

Suppose \(B'\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis \(I'\), and suppose
\[
B''=B'-b+e
\]
is an adjacent \(M\)-basis.
