If \(b\notin I'\), then \(I'\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I'\), put
\[
A=I'\setminus b.
\]
Let \(C\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B'\). Since \(B'-b+e\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), the term \(p_{A\cup b}\) is finite, so there is some
\[
c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})
\]
with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. This propagates the finite \(p\)-basis along the path to \(B\).

## 5.2. Polar Boolean apartments

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis by
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, each \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed, the flats generated by subsets of \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice, and the polarity sends meets to joins. In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis.

Restrict \(p\) to \(B\):
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the previous subsection, \(\mu_B\) is a genuine rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\). For \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\).

## 5.3. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

We use the following standard fact.

### Lemma 5.3.1 — Ordinary annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its ordinary valuated dual. For \(u\in\mathbb T^R\), define
\[
H_u=
\{y\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

This is the usual circuit-cocircuit annihilator theorem for valuated matroids. Namely, circuits of \(\mu^*\) are cocircuits of \(\mu\); the affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\); and each \(H_u\) is tropically convex.

## 5.4. Polar hyperplanes

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) be not the all-\(\infty\) vector. Since \(M\) is simple, \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\). Its dagger is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient, denoted
\[
x^\dagger.
\]
Explicitly, for an \(M\)-independent \((r-1)\)-subset \(J\),
\[
x^\dagger_J=x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp},
\]
and \(x^\dagger_J=\infty\) when \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent. The projective tropical linear space
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(x^\dagger)
\]
is the polar tropical hyperplane associated to \(x\).

## 5.5. Point-annihilator theorem

We prove:

### Theorem 5.5.1

For every non-all-\(\infty\) point \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]

#### Proof

Fix an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]

### Forward implication

Assume
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Then the restriction of \(x\) to the polar basis \(B^*\) lies in
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
By Lemma 5.3.1,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\). Hence
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

This is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger)
\]
on the \(M\)-basis \(B\), since
\[
x^\dagger_{B\setminus b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), all rank-\(r\) independent circuit equations for \(x^\dagger\) hold.

If \(T\subseteq E\) has size \(r\) and is \(M\)-dependent, then either all \((r-1)\)-subsets \(T\setminus t\) are \(M\)-dependent, or \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), and the finite values \(x^\dagger_{T\setminus t}\) are constant for \(t\in D\). Since \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), the circuit equation for \(D\) holds. Therefore
\[
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]

### Reverse implication

Assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
We prove that \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\).

Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\). We first show
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
By Lemma 5.3.1, it suffices to show that every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\).

A coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) has the form
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{S\cup b_i},
\]
where \(|S|=k-1\). This is the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S(e)=p_{S\cup e}.
\]
Since \(C_S\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), by assumption
\[
C_S\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Testing the circuit equation of \(x^\dagger\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\), we get
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\perp u_B\). Hence
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

As \(B\) ranges over all \(M\)-bases, so does \(B^*\). Therefore, for every \(M\)-basis \(R\),
\[
x|_R\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_R).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(R\). Since
\[
x|_R\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_R),
\]
the circuit equation for \(J\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either all \(J\setminus j\) are \(M\)-dependent, in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), and the finite values \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are constant for \(j\in D\). Since \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), the \(M\)-circuit equation on \(D\) holds. Thus the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) holds for all \(J\).

Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves Theorem 5.5.1. \(\square\)

## 5.6. Order reversal

Suppose
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(q).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
\]

Let
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger).
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(x\) belongs to every affine tropical linear space, so certainly \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\). Otherwise, since \(q^\dagger\leftarrow M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
By Theorem 5.5.1 applied to \(q\),
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q),
\]
we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Applying Theorem 5.5.1 to \(p\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger),
\]
and hence projectively
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

Thus the assignment
\[
\Phi_M(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\]
is order-reversing.

It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces, not merely on chosen Plücker coordinates: if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)=\operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then both inclusions hold, so the order-reversal just proved gives
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)=\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger).
\]

By Step 4,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
so
\[
\Phi_M^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Therefore \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) in the simple modular case.

Moreover, Step 4.2 shows that for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

# Step 6. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to an arbitrary finite matroid \(M\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be its simplification.

By Step 1:

1. \(L(M)\cong L(S)\) canonically.
2. The given polarity of \(L(M)\) descends to a polarity of \(L(S)\).
3. \(S\) is simple modular.
4. The diagonal zero-parallel-extension map induces a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
   \]
5. This isomorphism is compatible with the embedded flat subposets.

By the simple modular case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending the descended polarity of \(L(S)\).

Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, if \(F\in L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S)\), then Step 1.4 gives
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
Since \(\Phi_S\) sends \(\bar F\) to \(\bar F^\perp\), and \(\psi(F^\perp)=\bar F^\perp\), we obtain
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the given flat-lattice involution.

The theorem follows. \(\square\)

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully self-contained statement.

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathcal L(M)\) denote its lattice of flats, ordered by inclusion, and suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M),\qquad F\mapsto F^\perp .
\]
Work over the min-plus tropical semifield
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
with tropical addition \(a\oplus b=\min(a,b)\) and tropical multiplication \(a\odot b=a+b\). Throughout, a tropical minimum condition “the minimum is attained at least twice” means: **there is no unique finite minimizer**; in particular, an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

For a rank-\(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), write \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) for its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector, and write
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)
=
\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)
\]
for its projectivization, possibly empty when \(d=0\). For an ordinary matroid \(N\), \(\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) means \(\operatorname{Trop}(p_N)\), where \(p_N\) is the constant valuated matroid finite exactly on bases of \(N\). A valuated matroid \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid \(M\) exactly when
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
equivalently \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) after projectivization. Define
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)
=
\{\operatorname{Trop}(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of }M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. We allow the empty projective tropical linear space coming from rank \(0\).

Then there exists an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}),
\]
where \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero all-loop matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\) by adding the elements of \(F\) back as loops.

This is exactly the original problem, with all affine/projective and rank-zero conventions made explicit; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 0. Conventions and standard facts

A rank-\(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We use the three-term form: for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\bigl(
p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\;
p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\;
p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases have the evident empty families of relations.

The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall S\in\binom E{d+1},\ 
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(x_e+p_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this gives \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\). For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\mathbb T^E\).

We use the following standard facts.

1. **Quotients and containment.** For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same ground set, \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) if and only if
   \[
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
   \]
   Equivalently after projectivization,
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q).
   \]

2. **Circuit-incidence criterion for quotients of an ordinary matroid.** If \(M\) is an ordinary matroid and \(p\) is rank \(k\), then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer, with the convention that non-\(k\)-subsets have value \(\infty\).

3. **Coordinate cocircuits.** For rank \(k\) \(p\) and \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   If \(C_A^p\) is not identically \(\infty\), then \(C_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). If it is identically \(\infty\), it is harmless in all minimum conditions.

4. **Tropical linear spaces determine Plücker vectors.** A projective tropical linear space \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated Plücker vector \(p\) up to adding a global scalar. Hence any construction on \(p\) that is compatible with global scalar translation descends to \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

5. **Superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
   \[
   \{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
   \]
   is a flat of \(N\). This is equivalent to the no-unique-finite-minimum condition on every circuit.

---

# Part I. Reduction to the simplification

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

## 1. Flat lattices

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if a flat contains one element of a non-loop parallel class, it contains the whole class. Thus every flat of \(M\) is of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a unique flat \(\bar F\) of \(S\). Explicitly,
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0)
\]
is a lattice isomorphism, with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
This follows from the rank identity
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]

Therefore the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}.
\]

## 2. Modularity of \(S\)

The flat lattice \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is finite, ranked, atomistic, and semimodular. Because \(\mathcal L(S)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be its rank function. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while lower semimodularity gives the reverse inequality. Hence equality holds for all flats \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular, and \(S\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

## 3. Diagonal zero-parallel extension

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This descends to projective tropical spaces.

If \(\bar p\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), its zero-parallel extension \(p=\Delta_*\bar p\) to \(E\) is defined by
\[
p_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar p_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loops force coordinates equal to \(\infty\), zero-parallel pairs force equality of coordinates, and after imposing those equations the remaining circuit equations are exactly the circuit equations for \(\bar p\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

## 4. Descent of arbitrary quotients

Let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
We show that \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\).

First, every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\). If \(e\in L_0\) were not a loop of \(q\), choose a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni e\), put \(A=B\setminus e\), and consider the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\). Its \(e\)-coordinate is finite. But \(C_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), while every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, original parallel classes are not split. If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Hence for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
This implies:

- no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same original parallel class;
- replacing an element of a finite \(q\)-basis by another element of the same original parallel class preserves finiteness and the valuation;
- an entire original parallel class is either looped in \(q\), or is a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

Therefore we may define a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I,
\]
where \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) maps bijectively to \(\bar I\). This is well-defined by the preceding paragraph, and the tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are obtained by lifting them to \(q\). Moreover \(q=\Delta_*\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Because
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Consequently \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar p)\mapsto \operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar p).
\]

## 5. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
as a matroid on \(E\), and let
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
as a matroid on \(\bar E\). For \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(N_F)
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar N_{\bar F})\bigr).
\]
So it remains to solve the theorem for finite simple modular matroids.

---

# Part II. The finite simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and that
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Since the involution reverses rank,
\[
r(F^\perp)=r-r(F).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

## 1. Flat-constancy of \(p\)

By the circuit-incidence criterion, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the minimum consists of the single term \(p_I\). Therefore \(p_I=\infty\).

Second, suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), it follows that \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

Thus, for each rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I,
\]
where \(I\) is any \(M\)-basis of \(F\).

Also, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Indeed, start from a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis, and move to any other \(M\)-basis along the basis graph of \(M\). Across an exchange \(B'=B''+b-e\), if the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), keep it. If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\). The fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B'\) contains \(b\), and circuit-incidence supplies another \(c\) in that circuit with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis.

## 2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a function \(p^\dagger:\binom En\to\mathbb T\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the unique rank-zero valuated matroid, up to scalar.

Henceforth assume \(0<k<r\).

## 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It is enough to check the three-term tropical Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms are \(\infty\). So assume \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent \(n\)-set involving \(e\), so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(r(X)=n-2\), we have \(r(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) exactly when \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent.

Thus the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

### Lemma 3.1: Four hyperplanes in a modular interval

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],\qquad [14]=[24].
\]
Hence the last two Plücker sums are equal, and the relation holds.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Its rank is \(k\), \(k-1\), or \(k-2\).

If \(r(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums equal \(2\bar p(Z)\).

If \(r(Z)=k-2\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is precisely the desired relation.

It remains to treat \(r(Z)=k-1\). Consider the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\), and let \(N\) be its simplification. The points of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). A circuit of the contraction \(M/Z\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the terms from \(C\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(\min_{P\in D}w(P)\) has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

Now the images of \(H_i\) in \(N\) are hyperplanes \(L_i\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\). The desired relation follows from the next lemma. ∎

### Lemma 3.2: Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\;
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\;
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nonempty proper superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats in a rank-three geometry, hence have the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane, with one or both levels possibly absent. Thus
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\). For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique: according to the color multiplicities \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), either all matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\]
and set
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are \(\beta\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

This proves the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

## 4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, it suffices to prove that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms are \(\infty\). So assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(r(X)=n-1\) and \(r(Y)=k+1\).

We need one lemma.

### Lemma 4.1: Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

#### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to prove that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We first prove \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(r(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
The three \(H_i/K\) are distinct points on a rank-two line, so they form a circuit in the simplification of \(M/K\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply circuit-incidence for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\) and the circuit \(D\). Terms from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), and
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda\), we must have \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed. If \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S\) is independent, we show by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness. For \(m\ge 3\), put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}),\qquad F=G\vee b_m.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise, the line \(\ell=p\vee b_m\) satisfies, by modularity,
\[
r(\ell\wedge G)=r(\ell)+r(G)-r(F)=2+(m-1)-m=1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in S\). Since \(q,b_m\in S\), line-closedness gives every point of \(\ell=q\vee b_m\), including \(p\), in \(S\).

Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat for every \(\lambda\), so \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). ∎

We now finish the quotient proof. Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
u(X\vee c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts from the simplification of \(M/X\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). The set \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\): for every \(e\in C\setminus X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus e\bigr).
\]
If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would have a unique finite minimum, contradicting \(u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(r-k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

## 5. Involutivity

Because \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), the same flat-constancy argument applies to it.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

## 6. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-r(F)\). For a rank-\((r-r(F))\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]
Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(r(F)\) flat. Then \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). By polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
up to the same global scalar normalization. Hence dagger sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

---

# Part III. Point-annihilator duality and order reversal

We now prove that dagger reverses inclusions.

## 1. Boolean apartments and polar bases

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),
\qquad
b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\), define the restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\):
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma above, \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
the ordinary dual valuated matroid. Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

## 2. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define the tropical hyperplane
\[
H_u=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

Indeed, the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are exactly orthogonality of \(u\) to the coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu\). The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those coordinate cocircuits, and \(H_u\) is a closed tropically convex set. Hence \(H_u\) contains \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) exactly when it contains all coordinate cocircuits, which is exactly \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\). The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases agree with this statement under the all-\(\infty\) convention.

## 3. The affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\)

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
by
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x
\]
if and only if, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

## 4. Affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
we have
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

### Proof for \(0<k<r\)

Assume first that
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also
\[
(y_{b_1},\dots,y_{b_r})\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), unless it is identically \(\infty\), in which case the desired relation is automatic. Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), applying the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) for the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is exactly the circuit equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup i)
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]
Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every \(M\)-basis.

Now check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since \(x|_C\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_C)\), the circuit equation on \(J\) holds.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(r_M(J)<r-k\), then all \(J\setminus j\) are \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(r_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-circuit equation are exactly those indexed by \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) is constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer, which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

### Boundary ranks

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Hence both sides of the equivalence hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Conversely, if
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
take an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), an index \(i\), and \(A=B\setminus b_i\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) has only one possibly finite coordinate on \(B\), namely at \(b_i\). The defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) for \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover \(E\). Hence \(x=\infty^E\). This proves the boundary case.

Thus the affine point-annihilator theorem holds for all \(k\).

## 5. Order reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)\). By the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Applying the point-annihilator theorem again gives
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
After projectivization,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

Because \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is involutive and respects global scalar translation, it defines an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_M:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
\Phi_M(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
for finite simple modular \(M\). By the compatibility with flat quotients,
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

# Part IV. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Part I, we have:

- a lattice isomorphism
  \[
  \psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
  \]
  transporting \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\);

- a poset isomorphism
  \[
  \Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M);
  \]

- compatibility with flat quotients:
  \[
  \Delta_*\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})\right)
  =
  \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
  \]

Since \(S\) is finite simple modular, Part III gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending the induced flat-lattice involution. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\Phi_S\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F^\perp)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F^\perp)})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the theorem. ∎

**Claim.** Let \(M\) be a finite matroid. If the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then the relative Dressian
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)=\{\text{projective tropical linear spaces contained in }\operatorname{Trop}(M)\}
\]
ordered by inclusion admits an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\). Moreover
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

We prove the claim for finite matroids.

---

## 1. Conventions and standard facts

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with tropical addition \(\min\) and tropical multiplication \(+\). For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on a finite set \(E\), write \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\ne k\), and define its affine tropical linear space by
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J\setminus j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer for every } |J|=k+1
\right\}.
\]
The projective tropical linear space is
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)
=
(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(M\), we use the same symbol for its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ an }M\text{-basis},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the standard quotient-containment theorem for valuated matroids: if \(p,q\) are valuated matroids on \(E\), then
\[
p\leftarrow q
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)=\{\operatorname{Trop}(p):p\leftarrow M\}.
\]

We also use two standard facts.

1. If \(p\) has rank \(k\), then for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the vector
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e}
   \]
   is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), possibly identically \(\infty\), and lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\).

2. For an ordinary matroid \(N\) and \(w\in\mathbb T^E\),
   \[
   w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
   \]
   iff every superlevel set
   \[
   E_\lambda=\{e\in E:w_e\ge \lambda\},\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R,
   \]
   is a flat of \(N\). Indeed, a non-flat superlevel set gives a circuit with a unique finite minimum, and conversely a circuit with a unique finite minimum gives a non-flat superlevel set.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simple modular case

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\), let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of non-loop parallel classes. Hence
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism, with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Thus the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces one on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Since \(S\) is simple, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular. Because it is anti-isomorphic to itself, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Therefore, for all flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
so \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular. Thus \(S\) is a simple modular matroid.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(\bar p\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), let \(\Delta\bar p\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\): loops are added on \(L_0\), and each element of \(\bar E\) is replaced by a zero-offset parallel class. Directly from the circuit equations,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\Delta\bar p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]
If \(\bar p\leftarrow S\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S),
\]
so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\Delta\bar p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
hence \(\Delta\bar p\leftarrow M\).

Conversely, let \(q\leftarrow M\). If \(q\) has rank \(0\), it is already the zero-parallel extension of the rank-\(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Suppose \(q\) has positive rank \(k\). If \(e\in L_0\) and \(e\) belonged to a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\), then for \(A=B\setminus e\), the cocircuit \(C_A^q\) has finite \(e\)-coordinate. But every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\), contradiction. Thus every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Since \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), we get
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}
\]
for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\). Hence no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\), and replacing one representative of a parallel class by another preserves the valuation. Therefore \(q=\Delta\bar q\) for a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\). Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, \(\bar q\leftarrow S\). Thus \(\Delta_*\) is a bijection, and it preserves and reflects inclusion.

It remains to check compatibility with the embedded flat subposets. Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). For
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\qquad
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F},
\]
one has, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
So \(N_F=\Delta\bar N_{\bar F}\), and hence
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem for simple modular matroids.

---

## 3. The simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is simple modular of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\), and that
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F),
\]
and
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,
\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp.
\]

Let \(p\leftarrow M\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient.

### 3.1. Flat-constancy of quotients

For \(k\ge 1\), the quotient relation \(p\leftarrow M\) is equivalent to the following circuit-incidence condition:
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\quad\text{has no unique finite minimizer}
\]
for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the valuated flag incidence relations for \(p\leftarrow M\) reduce, because \(M\) is constant-valued, to the assertion that whenever \(T\) has size \(r+1\), the minimum over those \(i\in T\) for which \(T\setminus i\) is an \(M\)-basis has no unique finite minimizer. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), those \(i\) are precisely the elements of the unique circuit of \(T\), and every circuit arises in this way.

It follows that if \(I\subseteq E\) has size \(k\) and is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose a circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\); applying (CI) to \(A=I\setminus c\) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

If \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same closure, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). We denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

For \(k=0\), we define \(\bar p(\varnothing)=p_\varnothing\).

### 3.2. Definition of the dagger operation

Put \(n=r-k\). Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ is }M\text{-independent of size }n,\\
\infty,&J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

We next prove that \(p^\dagger\leftarrow M\).

---

## 4. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\)

The boundary cases are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar multiple of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar multiple of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

### 4.1. A rank-three edge lemma

We first need a small fact about rank-three modular matroids.

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). For four distinct hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\),
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(w=\infty\), this is clear. Otherwise subtract the global minimum of \(w\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nonzero superlevel sets form a chain of proper flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, with either member possibly absent. Thus
\[
w(x)=
\begin{cases}
\mu+\lambda,&x=a,\\
\mu,&x\le B,\ x\ne a,\\
0,&x\nleq B,
\end{cases}
\]
for some \(\mu,\lambda\in[0,\infty]\), with the evident interpretation when \(a\) or \(B\) is absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) iff \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus the cost of a perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) only depends on which paired indices have the same color \(y_i\). The possible multiplicity types are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In each case, at least two of the three perfect matchings have the same minimal cost.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). Put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\mu+\lambda,&y=a,\\
\mu,&y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the values \(c(y_j)\) equal \(\mu\), so the minimum is tied. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those two equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. This proves the lemma.

### 4.2. A local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two of the \(H_i\) are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[13]+[14]=[14]+[23],
\]
so the minimum is not unique. Hence assume the \(H_i\) are distinct.

Since \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
Meeting three hyperplanes of \(Y\) lowers rank by at most three, so \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)\ge k-1\). Also \(T_i\wedge H_i=Z\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)\ge k\), then modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_i\vee H_i)
=
\operatorname{rk}(T_i)+\operatorname{rk}(H_i)-\operatorname{rk}(Z)
\ge
k+(k+1)-(k-2)
=
k+3,
\]
impossible because \(T_i\vee H_i\le Y\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). Thus \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\).

For complementary indices \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), we have
\[
T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j,
\qquad
T_m\wedge T_n=Z.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_m\cup t_n)=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Thus
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on the set \(A\cup\{t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\}\) is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to consider \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). The elements \(e_P\) form a circuit in the contraction by \(A\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) with
\[
C\setminus A=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), the terms are precisely \(w(P)\), \(P\in D\). Thus the minimum of \(w\) on \(D\) has no unique finite minimizer, so \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

The images of the \(H_i\) in \([Z,Y]\) are four distinct hyperplanes of \(N\). Applying the rank-three edge lemma gives the desired relation.

### 4.3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

If \(n\le 1\), the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\) are vacuous. Assume \(n\ge 2\).

Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, every term is \(\infty\). So assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then each of the three displayed sums contains an \(\infty\)-summand, so the relation is trivial. Hence assume all four elements lie outside \(X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For distinct \(e,f\), we have \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which is equivalent to \(S\cup e\cup f\) being \(M\)-dependent. If \(H_e\ne H_f\), then \(X\vee e\) and \(X\vee f\) are distinct covers of \(X\). Modularity gives
\[
(X\vee e)\wedge(X\vee f)=X
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X\vee e\vee f)=n,
\]
so \(S\cup e\cup f\) is independent. Moreover
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup e\cup f)^\perp
=
(X\vee e\vee f)^\perp
=
H_e\wedge H_f.
\]
Therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the local dual Plücker lemma applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\).

Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

### 4.4. A line-closure lemma

We need one elementary fact about simple modular matroids.

Let \(N\) be a simple modular matroid. A set \(A\) of points of \(N\) is the set of points of a flat iff it is closed under lines: whenever \(a,b\in A\), every point on the line \(a\vee b\) also lies in \(A\).

One direction is obvious. Conversely, let \(F=\bigvee A\). We show every point below \(F\) lies in \(A\). It suffices to prove this for flats generated by finitely many points of \(A\), and we argue by induction on the number of generators. Suppose \(F_0=G\vee a\), where \(a\in A\) and the statement holds for \(G\). Let \(q\le F_0\) be a point. If \(q\le G\), then \(q\in A\) by induction. Otherwise, if \(q\ne a\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}\bigl((a\vee q)\wedge G\bigr)
=
\operatorname{rk}(a\vee q)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F_0)
=
1.
\]
Thus
\[
b=(a\vee q)\wedge G
\]
is a point of \(G\), hence \(b\in A\). Since \(q\le a\vee b\), line-closure gives \(q\in A\). Therefore \(A\) is exactly the set of points of the flat \(F\).

### 4.5. A codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1=r-k-1\), and put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp),
\qquad e\notin X.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X).
\]

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of the simplification of \(M/X\). Since \(M/X\) is modular, by the line-closure lemma it is enough to prove that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is closed under lines.

Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the contraction by \(A\), the elements \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit in a rank-two flat. Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus A=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(H_1),\ \bar p(H_2),\ \bar p(H_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Equivalently, the minimum of
\[
u(P_1),\ u(P_2),\ u(P_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda\), we must also have \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat.

Therefore \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

### 4.6. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We verify the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\leftarrow M\).

Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and let
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By the codimension-one polarity lemma, the function
\[
u(X\vee c)=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

If the displayed minimum had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\), then \(c_0\notin X\). In the contraction \(M/X\), the point \(X\vee c_0\) lies in the closure of the points represented by \((C\setminus X)\setminus\{c_0\}\). Hence there is a circuit of the simplification of \(M/X\), contained in the points represented by \(C\setminus X\), on which \(u\) has a unique finite minimum. This contradicts \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Thus no such unique finite minimizer exists.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\leftarrow M\).

---

## 5. Involutivity and compatibility with flats

### 5.1. Involutivity

We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\subseteq E\) have size \(k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and also \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

Assume \(I\) is independent and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

### 5.2. Embedded flats

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite precisely when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(\mathcal L(M)\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when it is disjoint from \(F\) and together with \(F\) spans \(E\), and modularity makes this equivalent to the two complement conditions above.

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 6. Order reversal

We now prove that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

### 6.1. Bases and polar Boolean apartments

First, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. For \(k=0\), this is clear. For \(k>0\), choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) and extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any other \(M\)-basis, and connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose \(B'\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis \(I'\), and \(B''=B'-b+e\) is adjacent to \(B'\). If \(b\notin I'\), then \(I'\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I'\), set \(A=I'\setminus b\). Let \(C\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B'\). Since \(B''\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), and using that \(p_{A\cup b}<\infty\), there is some
\[
c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})
\]
with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Thus the property propagates along the path.

Now let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and for \(S\subseteq [r]\), write
\[
F_S=\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular,
\[
F_S\vee F_T=F_{S\cup T},
\qquad
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Thus the flats \(F_S\) form a Boolean sublattice.

Define
\[
b_i^*=F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, each \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}\right)^\perp
=
F_{[r]\setminus S}^\perp.
\]
Taking \(S=[r]\), we see that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis. Applying the same formula to \(B^*\) gives \((B^*)^*=B\), so \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases.

Let
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the previous paragraph, \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp\right)
=
\bar p(F_{[r]\setminus S})
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\).

### 6.2. Ordinary annihilator duality

For a valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(R\), let \(\mu^*\) be its ordinary valuated dual. For \(u\in\mathbb T^R\), define
\[
H_u=
\{y\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
The standard tropical annihilator duality says:
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
Indeed, \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every circuit of \(\mu^*\), equivalently to every cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(H_u\) is tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).

### 6.3. Dependent circuit equations

We will use the following observation.

Let \(v\) be a rank-\(s\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) such that:

- \(v_I=\infty\) whenever \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent;
- for independent \(I\), \(v_I\) depends only on \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\).

If \(z\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) and \(J\subseteq E\) is \(M\)-dependent with \(|J|=s+1\), then
\[
\min_{j\in J}(z_j+v_{J\setminus j})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<s\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=s\), then \(J\) has nullity one and hence a unique circuit \(D\). For \(j\in D\), the set \(J\setminus j\) is independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so the finite coefficients \(v_{J\setminus j}\) are constant. For \(j\notin D\), the set \(J\setminus j\) is dependent. Therefore the displayed tropical equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation for \(D\), which holds because \(z\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

### 6.4. Point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) be non-all-\(\infty\). Regard \(x\) as a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\). Its dagger \(x^\dagger\) is the rank-\((r-1)\) valuated matroid
\[
x^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp},&J\text{ is }M\text{-independent of size }r-1,\\
\infty,&J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

We prove:
\[
\tag{*}
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger=M\), and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so \((*)\) is immediate.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar multiple of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so the left side of \((*)\) is false for non-all-\(\infty\) \(x\). The right side is also false: if \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger)\), then for any \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the \(M\)-cocircuit \(C_{B\setminus b_i}^M\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger)\). Testing the circuit equation of \(x^\dagger\) on \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on bases, every element appears as some \(b_i^*\), so \(x=\infty^E\), contradiction.

Thus assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

First suppose
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
we have
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But for the rank-\((r-1)\) matroid \(x^\dagger\),
\[
x^\dagger_{B\setminus b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore the circuit equation for \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger)\) holds on every \(M\)-basis \(B\). If \(T\subseteq E\) has size \(r\) and is \(M\)-dependent, the dependent-equation lemma applies to \(v=x^\dagger\), because \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence all circuit equations for \(x^\dagger\) hold, and
\[
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]

Conversely assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\). By ordinary annihilator duality, it is enough to show that every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\).

A coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) has the form
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup b_i},
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S^p(e)=p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup e}.
\]
Since \(C_S^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), by assumption
\[
C_S^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Testing the circuit equation of \(x^\dagger\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\) yields
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\), and hence
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]
Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, it follows that for every \(M\)-basis \(R\),
\[
x|_R\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_R).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(n+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(R\). The circuit equation for \(J\) follows from
\[
x|_R\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_R).
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the dependent-equation lemma applies to \(v=p^\dagger\), because \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Thus every circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) holds, and
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves \((*)\).

### 6.5. Order reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
\]
Let
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger).
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\). Otherwise, since \(q^\dagger\leftarrow M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
By the point-annihilator theorem applied to \(q\),
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(x^\dagger).
\]
Applying the point-annihilator theorem to \(p\), we get
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger),
\]
and after projectivizing,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

Thus the assignment
\[
\Phi_M(\operatorname{Trop}(p))
=
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\]
is order-reversing. It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces rather than on chosen Plücker coordinates: if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)=\operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then both inclusions hold, so the order-reversal just proved gives
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)=\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger).
\]
Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), we also have
\[
\Phi_M^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Hence \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) in the simple modular case. By Section 5.2, it sends the embedded flat \(F\) to \(F^\perp\).

---

## 7. Transfer back to arbitrary finite matroids

Return to an arbitrary finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Section 2:

- \(\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S)\);
- the given polarity of \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to a polarity of \(\mathcal L(S)\);
- \(S\) is simple modular;
- the zero-parallel extension map gives a poset isomorphism
  \[
  \Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M);
  \]
- this isomorphism is compatible with the embedded flat subposets.

Let
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
be the order-reversing involution constructed in the simple modular case. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
Since \(\Phi_S\) sends \(\bar F\) to \(\bar F^\perp=\psi(F^\perp)\), we get
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\). This proves the claim.

## Claim proved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Work with affine tropical linear spaces
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E
\]
and projectivizations
\[
\Trop(p)=\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1),
\]
so that a rank-zero valuated matroid has affine tropical linear space \(\{\infty^E\}\) and empty projective tropical linear space. Define
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\},
\]
ordered by inclusion; equivalently, \(p\) ranges over valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\).

If the flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(\Dr(M)\) admits an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), by adding the elements of \(F\) back as loops.

---

## 1. Preliminaries

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus operations. A tropical minimum condition “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer”; in particular, an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall S\in\binom E{k+1},\ 
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(x_e+p_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(N\), \(\Trop(N)\) means the tropical linear space of its constant valuated matroid.

For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the coordinate cocircuit of \(p\)
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
We use the standard valuated-matroid facts that \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) unless it is identically \(\infty\), and that \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of its coordinate cocircuits.

### Circuit-incidence criterion

Let \(M\) be an ordinary matroid and \(p\) a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on the same ground set. Then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, where non-\(k\)-subsets are assigned value \(\infty\).

Indeed, if containment holds, each coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), so the circuit equation of \(M\) on \(C\) gives exactly the displayed condition. Conversely, if the displayed conditions hold, then every coordinate cocircuit of \(p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\); since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) is closed and tropically convex, it contains the closed tropical convex hull of those cocircuits, namely \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\).

### Superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

If a superlevel set \(A=\{w\ge\lambda\}\) is not flat, choose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\setminus A\). Then some circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\); then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\), but \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\), so that superlevel set is not a flat.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification
