Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes. Hence
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism, with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Thus the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Because \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular. Since it is anti-isomorphic to itself, it is also lower semimodular. Therefore the rank function satisfies both submodularity and supermodularity, hence
\[
r(X)+r(Y)=r(X\wedge Y)+r(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(S\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(\bar p\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(p=\Delta_*\bar p\) on \(E\) by
\[
p_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar p_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\ \text{and}\ \pi|_I\ \text{is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, the loop coordinates are forced to be \(\infty\), parallel elements are forced to have equal coordinates, and after imposing those equations the remaining circuit equations are exactly those for \(\bar p\). In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
If \(q\) has rank \(0\), it is immediately a zero-parallel extension. Suppose \(q\) has rank \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\). If \(e\in L_0\) were contained in some finite \(q\)-basis \(I\), then for \(A=I\setminus\{e\}\), the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) would have finite \(e\)-coordinate. But every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, original parallel classes are not split by \(q\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Hence for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
It follows that:

- no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\);
- if \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis with \(e\in I\) and \(f\notin I\), then
  \[
  q_I=q_{I-e+f}.
  \]

Thus each parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(q\), or becomes a zero-offset valuated parallel class. Therefore \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I
\]
whenever \(\pi(I)=\bar I\) bijectively. The Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are obtained by lifting them to \(q\), and
\[
q=\Delta_*\bar q.
\]
Since
\[
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Finally, this isomorphism is compatible with flat quotients. If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Indeed, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F).
\]
Hence it is enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, and modular.

---

## 3. The simple modular case

Assume from now on that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and that
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Since intervals are reversed, ranks satisfy
\[
r(F^\perp)=r-r(F).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

### 3.1. Flat-constancy

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). For \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\), the circuit-incidence criterion gives a minimum over the singleton \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so \(p_I=\infty\).

If \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The circuit-incidence criterion gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every restriction \(M|F\) is connected, \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of each rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

We shall also use the following consequence. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Start with a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis, and move to any chosen \(M\)-basis along the basis graph of \(M\). At one basis-exchange step, suppose
\[
B_{\mathrm{new}}=B_{\mathrm{old}}-\{e\}\cup\{b\}.
\]
If \(e\notin I\), keep \(I\). If \(e\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{e\}\). The fundamental circuit of \(b\) with respect to \(B_{\mathrm{old}}\) contains \(e\). By circuit-incidence there is some
\[
c\neq e
\]
in that circuit with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Since \(c\in B_{\mathrm{new}}\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is a finite \(p\)-basis inside \(B_{\mathrm{new}}\).

### 3.2. Definition of the dual valuated matroid

Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a function
\[
p^\dagger:\binom En\to\mathbb T
\]
by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\ \text{is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,& J\ \text{is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Thus assume for the moment that \(0<k<r\).

---

## 4. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid

It is enough to verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations.

Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker sum contains a dependent \(n\)-set, hence all three sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(r(X)=n-2\), we have \(r(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) exactly when \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent.

We need the following local lemma.

### Lemma 4.1: Four hyperplanes in a modular interval

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the last two Plücker sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. Distinct hyperplanes of the modular flat \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Then \(r(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}\).

If \(r(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(r(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
We claim \(r(T_i)=k-1\). Since \(T_i\) is the meet of three hyperplanes in a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, modularity gives \(r(T_i)\ge k-1\). If \(r(T_i)=k\), then
\[
r(T_i\wedge H_i)
\ge r(T_i)+r(H_i)-r(Y)
=
k+(k+1)-(k+2)
=
k-1,
\]
contradicting \(T_i\wedge H_i=Z\) and \(r(Z)=k-2\). Hence \(r(T_i)=k-1\).

For \(i\neq j\), \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then modularity gives
\[
r(T_m\vee T_n)
=
r(T_m)+r(T_n)-r(Z)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(T_m\vee T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,
\]
is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to treat \(r(Z)=k-1\). Consider the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\), and let \(N\) be the simplification of \((M|Y)/Z\). The points of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show that \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). The chosen representatives form a circuit in \((M|Y)/Z\). By the standard circuit description for contractions, this lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\). Applying circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), all terms from \(C\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(\min_{P\in D} w(P)\) has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

The images of the \(H_i\) in \(N\) are hyperplanes \(L_i\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) corresponds to \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Therefore the desired relation follows from the following rank-three statement. ∎

### Lemma 4.2: Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\ 
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\ 
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

Repeated hyperplanes are handled exactly as in Lemma 4.1, so assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the proper nonempty superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats in a rank-three matroid. Hence there are a point \(a\), a hyperplane \(B\), and numbers \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=
\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}
+
\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}},
\]
with the obvious convention that one of the two levels may be absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes positive cost precisely when the two colors \(y_i,y_j\) coincide; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\). Among four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique: according to the color multiplicities \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), either all matchings tie or at least two distinct matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\]
and define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]\,c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]\,c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]\,c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are equal to \(\beta\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Applying Lemma 4.1 to \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) proves the three-term Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(r-k\) valuated matroid.

---

## 5. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

We prove
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
using the circuit-incidence criterion.

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\), where \(n=r-k\). We must show
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(r(X)=n-1\) and \(r(Y)=k+1\).

We use the following lemma.

### Lemma 5.1: Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of \(M/X\). The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N).
\]

#### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we prove line-closedness. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
The \(H_i\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\), and
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(r(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) so that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
The three points \(H_i/K\) form a circuit in the simplification of \(M/K\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(D\), the terms from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

Now we show that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Let \(S\) be line-closed. Choose a maximal independent subset
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S.
\]
We claim every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). This is clear for \(m\le2\). For \(m\ge3\), let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}),
\qquad
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m
\]
satisfies, by modularity,
\[
r(\ell\wedge G)
=
r(\ell)+r(G)-r(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\subseteq S\). Since \(q,b_m\in S\) and \(S\) is line-closed, every point of \(\ell=q\vee b_m\), including \(p\), lies in \(S\).

Thus every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). By maximality of \(B\), we also have \(S\subseteq\operatorname{cl}(B)\). Hence \(S\) is exactly the point set of the flat \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\).

Therefore each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), so \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\). ∎

Return to the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
u(X\vee c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts from the simplification of \(M/X\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

If the minimum over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then \(e\notin X\). Since \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit, \(e\) lies in the closure, inside \(M/X\), of \((C\setminus X)\setminus\{e\}\). Hence some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) contains \(e\). On that circuit the lifted vector \(u\) would have a unique finite minimum at \(e\), contradicting \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). Thus \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the circuit-incidence criterion and is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Involutivity of \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\)

Since \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to it.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

---

## 7. Compatibility with flat quotients in the simple modular case

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-r(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank-\((r-r(F))\) flat of \(M\). Then \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(G\), then \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when
\[
r_M(F\cup I)=r(M).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
F\vee G=\hat 1.
\]
By modularity,
\[
r(F\wedge G)
=
r(F)+r(G)-r(F\vee G)
=
r(F)+(r-r(F))-r
=
0,
\]
so \(F\wedge G=\hat 0\). The converse is immediate.

Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(r(F)\) flat. By definition,
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). Applying the order-reversing involution, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
up to the irrelevant global scalar. Hence dagger sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

---

## 8. Order reversal

It remains to show that dagger reverses inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

### 8.1. Polar bases

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}),
\qquad
b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\).

For \(S\subseteq[r]\), put
\[
F_S=\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Modularity gives
\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
r(F_S\wedge F_T)
=
r(F_S)+r(F_T)-r(F_S\vee F_T)
=
|S|+|T|-|S\cup T|
=
|S\cap T|,
\]
and \(F_{S\cap T}\subseteq F_S\wedge F_T\) has the same rank.

Since \(H_i=F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}\), iterating gives
\[
F_S=\bigwedge_{i\notin S} H_i.
\]
Applying the polarity,
\[
F_S^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\), define the restriction of \(p\) to the Boolean apartment \(B\):
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma, \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\), and the Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\), so \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\).

Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
\]
as valuated matroids. Indeed, for \(R\subseteq[r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p(F_{[r]\setminus R})
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

### 8.2. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define the tropical hyperplane
\[
H_u
=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

The circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are exactly the orthogonality conditions between \(u\) and the coordinate cocircuits \(C_A^\mu\). Indeed, if \(T\subseteq N\) has size \(\operatorname{rank}(\mu^*)+1\) and \(A=N\setminus T\), then
\[
(\mu^*)_{T\setminus i}=\mu_{A\cup i}.
\]
Thus
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\]
if and only if \(H_u\) contains every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those cocircuits, and \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex, this is equivalent to
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

### 8.3. The polar hyperplane inside \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\)

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
by declaring \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
we have
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

### 8.4. Point-annihilator theorem

For every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\).

Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
and
\[
(y_{b_1},\dots,y_{b_r})\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and put again
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\). Then \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is identically \(\infty\), in which case the desired relation is automatic. Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), applying the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) for the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i},
\]
so this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, this proves that \(x\) restricts to
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)
\]
on every \(M\)-basis \(C\).

Now we check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). The circuit equation on \(J\) holds because \(x|_C\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(r_M(J)<r-k\), then all \(J\setminus\{j\}\) are \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(r_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-circuit equation are exactly those indexed by \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus\{j\})=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Hence the Plücker coefficient is constant over \(j\in C\), and the equation reduces to
\[
\min_{j\in C} x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer, which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary ranks are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Hence both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero. Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) has, on the basis \(B\), only one finite coordinate, namely the coordinate \(b_i\). The condition \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) therefore forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover all of \(E\): given \(e\in E\), choose a basis of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\) and add one element outside it. Hence \(x=\infty^E\). This proves the boundary case.

### 8.5. Definition of the involution in the simple modular case

For a projective tropical linear space \(L\in\Dr(M)\), let \(\widetilde L\) be its affine cone, including \(\infty^E\). Define
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M): \widetilde L\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
By the point-annihilator theorem, if \(L=\Trop(p)\), then
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}=\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(L)\in\Dr(M)\).

The definition is visibly inclusion-reversing: if \(L_1\subseteq L_2\), then
\[
\Phi(L_2)\subseteq \Phi(L_1).
\]
Moreover,
\[
\Phi^2(\Trop(p))
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^\dagger))
=
\Trop((p^\dagger)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p).
\]
Hence \(\Phi\) is an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\).

By Section 7, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 9. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\), with simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We have:

1. a lattice isomorphism
   \[
   \psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
   \]
   carrying the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\);

2. a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M);
   \]

3. compatibility with flat quotients:
   \[
   \Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})\right)
   =
   \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
   \]

Since \(S\) is finite, simple, loopless, and modular, the construction above gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced flat-lattice involution. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\Phi_S\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F^\perp)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F^\perp)})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus the required inclusion-reversing involution exists for every finite matroid whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution, including the rank-zero empty projective tropical linear space. ∎

**Claim.** Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let \(\Dr(M)\) denote the set of projective tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\) where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\), including the rank-zero quotient whose projectivization is empty. Order \(\Dr(M)\) by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. If the flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(\Dr(M)\) has an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We prove this claim.

Throughout, write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), put
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall S\in \binom E{k+1},\
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(x_e+p_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization is
\[
\Trop(p)=\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1).
\]
For rank \(0\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\).

A valuated matroid \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) if
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

We use two standard facts about valuated matroids. First, for a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), its coordinate cocircuits
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e}, & e\notin A,\\
\infty, & e\in A,
\end{cases}
\qquad A\in\binom E{k-1},
\]
lie in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) unless identically \(\infty\), and \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of them. Second, the three-term tropical Plücker relations characterize valuated matroids.

We shall also use the following elementary criteria.

### 1. Two criteria

**Circuit-incidence criterion.** Let \(M\) be an ordinary matroid and \(p\) a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on the same ground set. Then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, with the empty minimum interpreted as having no finite minimizer.

Indeed, if containment holds, then every coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), and the circuit equation for \(C\) is exactly the displayed condition. Conversely, if all displayed conditions hold, then all coordinate cocircuits of \(p\) lie in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) is closed and tropically convex, it contains their closed tropical convex hull, namely \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\).

**Superlevel-flat criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

If such a superlevel set is not flat, choose \(e\in \operatorname{cl}(A)\setminus A\). Then some circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\); then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\), but \(e\) lies in its closure.

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes. Hence
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism with inverse
\[
\bar F\longmapsto L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Therefore the involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

The lattice \(\mathcal L(S)\) is geometric, so its rank function is submodular:
\[
r(X)+r(Y)\ge r(X\wedge Y)+r(X\vee Y).
\]
Since \(\mathcal L(S)\) has an order-reversing involution, ranks satisfy
\[
r(X^\perp)=r(S)-r(X).
\]
Applying submodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality, hence equality:
\[
r(X)+r(Y)=r(X\wedge Y)+r(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(S\) is modular. Moreover every interval in a modular lattice is modular, and the flat lattice of \((S|B)/A\) is the interval \([A,B]\). Simplification does not change the flat lattice. Hence every restriction, contraction, interval, and simplification used below remains finite, simple, and modular.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(\bar p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(p=\Delta_*\bar p\) on \(E\) by
\[
p_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar p_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(p\) are exactly the pullbacks of those for \(\bar p\), together with automatic relations coming from loops and zero-valued parallel copies. Thus \(p\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\Delta_*\bar p)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)).
\]
Indeed, the elements of \(L_0\) are loops, so their coordinates are forced to be \(\infty\). Elements in the same parallel class are zero-valued parallel copies, so their coordinates are forced to be equal. After imposing those equations, the remaining tropical linear equations are precisely the equations for \(\bar p\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(q\) has rank \(0\), it is the zero-parallel extension of the rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(q\) has rank \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\). If \(e\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(q\)-basis \(I\), then for \(A=I\setminus\{e\}\), the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) would have finite \(e\)-coordinate. But every vector of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, parallel classes of \(M\) are not split by \(q\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then every vector of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Hence, whenever \(C_A^q\) is not identically \(\infty\), we have
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
It follows that no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class, and replacing one representative of a class by another does not change the \(q\)-value.

Therefore \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I
\]
for any choice of representatives \(I\) with \(\pi(I)=\bar I\) bijectively. The Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow by choosing representatives and applying those for \(q\). Thus
\[
q=\Delta_*\bar q.
\]
Since
\[
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q))
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

This isomorphism is compatible with the flat quotients. If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Indeed, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F).
\]

It is therefore enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, and equipped with an order-reversing involution of its flat lattice.

### 3. The simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and that
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Then
\[
r(F^\perp)=r-r(F)
\]
for every flat \(F\).

If \(r=0\), then \(\Dr(M)\) consists only of the rank-zero empty projective tropical linear space, and the identity map is the desired involution. Hence assume \(r>0\).

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

#### Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). For \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\), the circuit-incidence criterion gives a minimum over the singleton \(\{c\}\), so \(p_I=\infty\).

If \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The circuit-incidence criterion gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every matroid is connected, \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of each rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Denote this value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

Also, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Start with a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) and extend it to an \(M\)-basis. Moving along the basis graph of \(M\), suppose
\[
B_{\mathrm{new}}=B_{\mathrm{old}}-\{e\}\cup\{b\}.
\]
If \(e\notin I\), keep \(I\). If \(e\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{e\}\). The fundamental circuit of \(b\) with respect to \(B_{\mathrm{old}}\) contains \(e\). Since \(p_{A\cup e}<\infty\), the circuit-incidence criterion gives some \(c\neq e\) in that circuit with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Because \(c\in B_{\mathrm{new}}\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is a finite \(p\)-basis inside \(B_{\mathrm{new}}\).

#### Definition of the dual quotient

Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define \(p^\dagger\) on \(\binom En\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

When \(k=0\), this is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid of \(M\). When \(k=r\), this is the rank-zero valuated matroid. Thus the boundary ranks are automatic. For the proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient, we assume \(0<k<r\).

### 4. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid

If \(n=1\), then there are no three-term Plücker relations to check. So assume \(n\ge2\).

Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker sum contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]
Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(r(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following lemma.

**Four-hyperplane lemma.** Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24],
\]
so the last two sums are equal. Hence the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. In a modular flat, distinct hyperplanes meet in codimension \(2\), so each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Then \(r(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}\), omitting negative ranks.

If \(r(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(r(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(r(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, modularity gives \(r(T_i)\ge k-1\), and \(r(T_i)=k\) would force
\[
r(T_i\wedge H_i)\ge k-1,
\]
contradicting \(T_i\wedge H_i=Z\) and \(r(Z)=k-2\).

For \(i\neq j\), \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then modularity gives
\[
r(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)=k.
\]
Since \(T_m\vee T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j\), and the latter also has rank \(k\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to consider \(r(Z)=k-1\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}\bigl((M|Y)/Z\bigr).
\]
This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). These representatives form a circuit in \((M|Y)/Z\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\). Applying the circuit-incidence criterion for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the terms from \(C\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), while the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Therefore \(\min_{P\in D}w(P)\) has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

The images of \(H_i\) in \(N\) are hyperplanes \(L_i\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) corresponds to \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Thus the desired relation follows from the next rank-three lemma.

**Rank-three edge lemma.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Repeated hyperplanes are handled as above, so assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the proper nonempty superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
\text{point } a \ \subseteq\ \text{hyperplane } B,
\]
with possibly one of the two levels absent. Hence there are \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i,j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\neq y_j\), and is a nonnegative value depending only on the common color \(y_i=y_j\) otherwise. Among four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique: according to the multiplicities \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), either all matchings tie or at least two distinct matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\]
and let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two costs equal \(\beta\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, the four-hyperplane lemma, and therefore the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

### 5. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

We prove
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
using the circuit-incidence criterion.

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(r(X)=n-1\) and \(r(Y)=k+1\).

We need the following lemma.

**Codimension-one polarity lemma.** Let \(N=\operatorname{si}(M/X)\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(N).
\]

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). First we show it is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and
\[
H_3\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(r(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) so that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
The three points \(H_i/K\) form a circuit in the simplification of \((M|Y)/K\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying the circuit-incidence criterion for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(D\), the terms from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while the remaining terms are
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed and choose a maximal independent subset
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S.
\]
We claim every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). This is clear for \(m\le2\). For \(m\ge3\), let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}),
\qquad
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise \(\ell=p\vee b_m\) is a line, and modularity gives
\[
r(\ell\wedge G)=r(\ell)+r(G)-r(F)=2+(m-1)-m=1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\subseteq S\). Since \(q,b_m\in S\), line-closedness gives every point of \(\ell\), including \(p\), in \(S\). Hence all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lie in \(S\). Maximality of \(B\) gives \(S\subseteq \operatorname{cl}(B)\), so \(S\) is the point set of a flat.

Therefore each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), and \(u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

Now return to the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
u(X\vee c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

Suppose the minimum over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\). Then \(e\notin X\). Since \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit, \(e\) lies in the closure, inside \(M/X\), of \((C\setminus X)\setminus\{e\}\). Hence some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) contains \(e\). On that circuit, the lifted vector \(u\) would have a unique finite minimum at \(e\), contradicting \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). Therefore the circuit-incidence criterion holds, and \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

### 6. Involutivity of \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\)

Because \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy statement applies to it. Let \(I\in\binom Ek\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both \(p_I\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I\) are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### 7. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-r(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank-\((r-r(F))\) flat of \(M\). Then \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(G\), then \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when
\[
r_M(F\cup I)=r.
\]
Equivalently, \(F\vee G=\hat 1\). By modularity, this is equivalent to \(F\wedge G=\hat 0\) because
\[
r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r(F\vee G)=0.
\]

Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(r(F)\) flat. By definition, \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). Applying the order-reversing involution, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
up to an irrelevant global scalar. Hence dagger sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

### 8. Order reversal

We now construct the actual involution on tropical linear spaces.

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}),
\qquad
b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Because \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence a ground-set element.

For \(S\subseteq [r]\), put
\[
F_S=\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Modularity gives
\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Therefore
\[
F_S=\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Applying the polarity,
\[
F_S^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\), define its restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
The finite-basis lemma above shows that \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\), and the Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\). Thus \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
\]
as valuated matroids. Indeed, for \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p(F_{[r]\setminus R})
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

We also use the usual tropical annihilator duality: if \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and
\[
H_u=\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\},
\]
then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This follows because the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are precisely the orthogonality conditions between \(u\) and the coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu\), and those cocircuits tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\).

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
by declaring \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We claim that for every rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
while
\[
(y_{b_1},\dots,y_{b_r})\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and put again
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\). Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
If the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) is identically \(\infty\), the desired relation is automatic. Otherwise \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), and applying the defining condition for the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, this proves that \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)\) on every \(M\)-basis \(C\). It remains to check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(C\); the circuit equation on \(J\) holds because \(x|_C\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C)\).

If \(J\) is dependent and \(r_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all coefficients are \(\infty\). If \(r_M(J)=r-k\), let \(C\) be the unique \(M\)-circuit contained in \(J\), and put
\[
\gamma=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp).
\]
For \(j\notin C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so the coefficient is \(\infty\). For \(j\in C\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus\{j\})=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so the coefficient is the common value \(\gamma\). If \(\gamma=\infty\), the equation is automatic. If \(\gamma<\infty\), the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit condition that
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

The boundary cases are straightforward. If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero. Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Conversely, suppose \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus\{b_i\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) has, on the basis \(B\), only one finite coordinate, namely the \(b_i\)-coordinate. Since \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), the defining condition for \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover all of \(E\): given \(e\in E\), choose a basis of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\) and add one element outside it. Hence \(x=\infty^E\).

Therefore, for all ranks \(k\),
\[
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Now define, for \(L\in\Dr(M)\) with affine cone \(\widetilde L\),
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):\widetilde L\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
If \(L=\Trop(p)\), then by the equivalence just proved,
\[
\widetilde{\Phi(L)}=\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(L)\in\Dr(M)\).

The definition is visibly inclusion-reversing: if \(L_1\subseteq L_2\), then
\[
\Phi(L_2)\subseteq \Phi(L_1).
\]
Moreover,
\[
\Phi^2(\Trop(p))
=
\Phi(\Trop(p^\dagger))
=
\Trop((p^\dagger)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(p).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\).

Finally, by the compatibility with flat quotients,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). This proves the theorem in the finite simple loopless modular case.

### 9. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We have:

1. a lattice isomorphism
   \[
   \psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
   \]
   carrying the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\);

2. a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M);
   \]

3. compatibility with flat quotients:
   \[
   \Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})\right)
   =
   \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
   \]

The simple modular case gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the induced flat-lattice involution. Define
\[
\Phi_M=\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). For every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\Phi_S\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Trop(S/\psi(F^\perp)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F^\perp)})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(\Dr(M)\) admits the required inclusion-reversing involution extending the given order-reversing involution on the flat lattice.

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then the relative Dressian
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)=\{\text{projective tropical linear spaces contained in }\operatorname{Trop}(M)\},
\]
ordered by inclusion, admits an order-reversing involution \(\Phi\). Moreover, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}),
\]
so \(\Phi\) extends the given involution on the embedded copy of \(\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\subset \operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

We prove the theorem for finite matroids.

---

## 1. Conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with tropical addition \(\min\) and tropical multiplication \(+\). For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), we extend \(p\) to all subsets by setting \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\ne k\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde L(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer for every } |J|=k+1
\right\}.
\]
The projective tropical linear space is
\[
L(p)=\bigl(\widetilde L(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(N\), we also write \(N\) for its constant valuated matroid:
\[
N_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }N,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard facts.

1. **Cocircuit generation.** If \(p\) has rank \(k\ge 1\), then for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the vector
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e}
   \]
   is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), possibly identically \(\infty\), and lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\). Moreover, \(\widetilde L(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of these cocircuits.

2. **Ordinary Bergman criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\),
   \[
   x\in \widetilde L(N)
   \]
   iff for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\), the minimum of \(\{x_c:c\in C\}\) has no unique finite minimizer. Equivalently, for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the superlevel set
   \[
   \{e\in E:x_e\ge \lambda\}
   \]
   is a flat of \(N\).

3. **Restrictions.** If \(v\) is a rank-\(s\) valuated matroid on \(E\), \(R\subseteq E\) supports a rank-\(s\) restriction \(v|_R\), and \(x\in \widetilde L(v)\), then \(x|_R\in \widetilde L(v|_R)\).

We work with affine cones. Because every \(\widetilde L(p)\) contains \(\infty^E\) and is invariant under adding constants, inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces is equivalent to inclusion of the corresponding affine cones.

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) with \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\), the cocircuit-generation theorem gives the following useful criterion. If \(k\ge 1\), then
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)
\]
is equivalent to the condition that for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\) and every circuit \(C\) of \(M\),
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, with the convention that entries with \(c\in A\) contribute \(\infty\). Indeed, by cocircuit generation, containment is equivalent to \(C_A^p\in \widetilde L(M)\) for every \(A\), and the ordinary Bergman criterion for \(M\) gives exactly \((\mathrm{CI})\).

Two consequences will be used repeatedly.

- If \(I\subseteq E\) has size \(k\) and is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). For \(A=I\setminus c\), condition \((\mathrm{CI})\) says that the one-term minimum \(p_I\) is not uniquely finite, hence \(p_I=\infty\).

- If \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure, then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). There is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}\) containing both \(a\) and \(b\); applying \((\mathrm{CI})\) gives equality of the two finite-or-infinite values \(p_{A\cup a}\) and \(p_{A\cup b}\).

Thus, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) of \(M\), the value \(p_B\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases \(B\) of \(F\). We denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]
For \(k=0\), we put \(\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing))=p_\varnothing\).

---

## 2. Reduction to the simple modular case

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\), let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of non-loop parallel classes. Therefore
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism, with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Hence the given involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\).

Because \(S\) is simple, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice. Let \(\rho\) be its rank function and let \(r=\rho(\hat 1)\). Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Since \(\mathcal L(S)\cong \mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\), the opposite lattice is also semimodular. The rank function in the opposite lattice is \(\rho^{\mathrm{op}}(X)=r-\rho(X)\), so semimodularity in the opposite lattice gives the reverse inequality. Thus
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). This rank equality implies the modular law: if \(X\le Z\), then
\[
X\vee(Y\wedge Z)\le (X\vee Y)\wedge Z,
\]
and the two sides have the same rank by the displayed equality, hence are equal. Thus \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular, so \(S\) is a simple modular matroid.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(\bar p\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), let \(\Delta\bar p\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\): elements of \(L_0\) are loops, and each \(\bar e\in\bar E\) is replaced by the parallel class \(\pi^{-1}(\bar e)\) with zero offsets. Directly from the circuit equations,
\[
\widetilde L(\Delta\bar p)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde L(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde L(S)\bigr).
\]

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]
If \(\widetilde L(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde L(S)\), then
\[
\widetilde L(\Delta\bar p)
=
\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar p))
\subseteq
\Delta(\widetilde L(S))
=
\widetilde L(M),
\]
so \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined.

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]
If \(k=0\), then \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of the rank-\(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\). By the consequence of \((\mathrm{CI})\) above, \(q_I=\infty\) for every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-set \(I\). Hence no finite \(q\)-basis contains a loop of \(M\), and no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class.

Define a function \(\bar q\) on \(k\)-subsets of \(\bar E\) by
\[
\bar q_{\{\bar e_1,\dots,\bar e_k\}}
=
q_{\{e_1,\dots,e_k\}},
\]
where \(e_i\in \pi^{-1}(\bar e_i)\). This is well-defined: changing one representative replaces a \(k\)-set by another with the same \(M\)-closure if it is independent, and both values are \(\infty\) if it is dependent. The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow by choosing representatives and applying the Plücker relations for \(q\). Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid, and \(q=\Delta\bar q\). Since
\[
\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q))=\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)),
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde L(S).
\]
Therefore \(\Delta_*\) is bijective, and it preserves and reflects inclusion.

It remains to check compatibility with the embedded flat subposets. Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and set \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\qquad
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
For every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F=\Delta\bar N_{\bar F}\), and therefore
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Hence it suffices to prove the theorem for simple modular matroids.

---

## 3. The simple modular case: construction of the dagger operation

Assume now that \(M\) is simple modular of rank \(r\), and that
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F),
\]
and
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,
\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]
Put \(n=r-k\). Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ is }M\text{-independent of size }n,\\
\infty,&J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent of size }n,
\end{cases}
\]
and \(p^\dagger_J=\infty\) for \(|J|\ne n\).

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis, then \(I\) is \(M\)-independent; for \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid and that
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar multiple of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar multiple of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

### 3.1. A rank-three edge relation

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, and let \(w\in \widetilde L(N)\). For four distinct hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(N\),
\[
\min\Bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\Bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Indeed, if \(w=\infty^E\), this is immediate. Otherwise subtract the global minimum of \(w\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the positive superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of proper flats. In rank three, after harmless choices in degenerate cases, there are a line \(B\), a point \(a\le B\), and \(\mu,\lambda\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=
\begin{cases}
\mu+\lambda,&x=a,\\
\mu,&x\le B,\ x\ne a,\\
0,&x\nleq B.
\end{cases}
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), and contributes \(w(y_i)\) if \(y_i=y_j\). The possible multiplicity types of the colors \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\) are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In each case at least two of the three perfect matchings have the same minimal cost.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). Put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\) for \(j=2,3,4\), and write \(\gamma(y)=w(y)\). The three matching costs are
\[
\gamma(y_2)+\epsilon(y_3,y_4),\qquad
\gamma(y_3)+\epsilon(y_2,y_4),\qquad
\gamma(y_4)+\epsilon(y_2,y_3),
\]
where \(\epsilon(y,z)=\gamma(y)\) if \(y=z\), and \(\epsilon(y,z)=0\) otherwise. If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of \(\gamma(y_2),\gamma(y_3),\gamma(y_4)\) equal the minimum. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. This proves the rank-three edge relation.

### 3.2. A local dual Plücker relation

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two of the \(H_i\) are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[13]+[14]=[14]+[23],
\]
so the minimum is not unique. Thus assume the \(H_i\) are distinct.

Because \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j=Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
\]
One checks using modularity that \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Moreover, for complementary indices \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_m\cup t_n)=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A\cup\{t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\}\) is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to consider \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a simple modular rank-three matroid \(N\). Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in \widetilde L(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). In the contraction by \(A\), the elements \(e_P\) form a circuit, so there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) with
\[
C\setminus A=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying \((\mathrm{CI})\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the minimum of the values
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P),\qquad P\in D,
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in \widetilde L(N)\).

The images of the \(H_i\) are four distinct hyperplanes of \(N\). The rank-three edge relation applied to \(N\) gives the desired tropical Plücker relation.

### 3.3. The Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

If \(n\le 1\), the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\) are vacuous. Assume \(n\ge 2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\Bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\Bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, every term is \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), each of the three sums contains an \(\infty\)-summand, so the relation is trivial. Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\), we have \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which is equivalent to \(S\cup e\cup f\) being \(M\)-dependent. If \(H_e\ne H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
(X\vee e)\wedge(X\vee f)=X,
\]
and therefore \(S\cup e\cup f\) is independent, with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup e\cup f)^\perp
=
(X\vee e\vee f)^\perp
=
H_e\wedge H_f.
\]
Hence
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
The desired Plücker relation is therefore exactly the local dual Plücker relation for the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

### 3.4. A line-closure lemma

Let \(N\) be a simple modular matroid. A set \(A\) of points of \(N\) is the set of points of a flat iff it is closed under lines: whenever \(a,b\in A\), every point on the line \(a\vee b\) also lies in \(A\).

One direction is clear. Conversely, let \(F=\bigvee A\). We prove that every point of \(F\) lies in \(A\). It is enough to consider flats generated by finitely many points of \(A\). Induct on the number of generators. Suppose \(F_0=G\vee a\), where the claim is known for \(G\) and \(a\in A\). Let \(q\le F_0\) be a point. If \(q\le G\), then \(q\in A\). Otherwise, assuming \(q\ne a\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}\bigl((a\vee q)\wedge G\bigr)=1.
\]
Thus \(b=(a\vee q)\wedge G\) is a point of \(G\), hence \(b\in A\). Since \(q\le a\vee b\), line-closure gives \(q\in A\). Therefore \(A\) is precisely the set of points of the flat \(F\).

### 3.5. A codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1=r-k-1\), and put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(N).
\]

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is simple modular, the line-closure lemma reduces this to proving that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is closed under lines.

Let \(P_1,P_2\in \mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Otherwise, set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the contraction by \(A\), the elements \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit in a rank-two flat. Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus A=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Applying \((\mathrm{CI})\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(H_1),\ \bar p(H_2),\ \bar p(H_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since the first two values are at least \(\lambda\), the third must also be at least \(\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in \widetilde L(N)\).

### 3.6. The quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now verify that
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]
By the criterion \((\mathrm{CI})\), it is enough to show that for every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\) and every \(M\)-circuit \(C\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By the codimension-one polarity lemma, the function
\[
u(X\vee c)=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in the tropical linear space of the simplification of \(M/X\).

If the displayed minimum over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\), then \(c_0\notin X\). In the contraction \(M/X\), the point \(X\vee c_0\) lies in the closure of the points represented by \((C\setminus X)\setminus\{c_0\}\). Therefore there is a circuit in the simplification of \(M/X\), contained in the points represented by \(C\setminus X\), on which \(u\) has a unique finite minimum. This contradicts \(u\in \widetilde L(M/X)\). Hence no unique finite minimizer exists.

Thus \(\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\).

---

## 4. Involutivity and compatibility with flats in the simple modular case

We first show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\subseteq E\) have size \(k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). For a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite precisely when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\) exactly when it is disjoint from \(F\) and together with \(F\) spans \(E\); by modularity this is equivalent to the two complement conditions.

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
and therefore
\[
L((p^F)^\dagger)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 5. Order reversal in the simple modular case

We prove that if \(p,q\) are valuated matroid quotients of \(M\) and
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q),
\]
then
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

### 5.1. Polar Boolean apartments

First, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. For \(k=0\), this is clear. Suppose \(k>0\). Choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), and extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any other \(M\)-basis. Connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). It is enough to propagate the property across an adjacent exchange \(B''=B'-b+e\).

Assume \(B'\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis \(I'\). If \(b\notin I'\), then \(I'\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I'\), set \(A=I'\setminus b\). Let \(C\) be the fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B'\). Since \(B''\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). Applying \((\mathrm{CI})\) to \(A\) and \(C\), and using \(p_{A\cup b}<\infty\), there exists \(c\in C\setminus (A\cup\{b\})\) with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Thus every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and for \(S\subseteq [r]\), write
\[
F_S=\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular,
\[
F_S\vee F_T=F_{S\cup T},
\qquad
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]
Thus the flats \(F_S\) form a Boolean sublattice.

Define
\[
b_i^*=F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a point of \(M\). For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}\right)^\perp
=
F_{[r]\setminus S}^\perp.
\]
Taking \(S=[r]\), we see that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis. Applying the same construction to \(B^*\) gives \((B^*)^*=B\). Hence \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases.

For \(|T|=k\), define
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}.
\]
The previous paragraph shows that \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover, for \(|S|=n=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp\right)
=
\bar p(F_{[r]\setminus S})
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\).

### 5.2. Ordinary annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its ordinary valuated dual. For \(u\in\mathbb T^R\), define
\[
H_u=
\left\{
y\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+y_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
