Indeed, the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are exactly the orthogonality conditions between \(u\) and the coordinate cocircuits
\[
D_A(i)=\mu_{A\cup i},
\qquad |A|=\operatorname{rk}(\mu)-1,
\]
of \(\mu\). Thus \(u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)\) iff every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu\) lies in \(H_u\).

The set \(H_u\) is tropically convex: if \(y^{(1)},\dots,y^{(m)}\in H_u\) and
\[
z_i=\min_\ell(\lambda_\ell+y^{(\ell)}_i),
\]
then a unique finite minimum of \(u_i+z_i\) would already give a unique finite minimum for one of the \(u_i+y^{(\ell)}_i\). Since \(\widetilde L(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu\), the claimed equivalence follows.

### 5.3. A dependent-equation lemma

Let \(v\) be a rank-\(s\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) such that

- \(v_I=\infty\) whenever \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent;
- for independent \(I\), the value \(v_I\) depends only on \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\).

If \(z\in\widetilde L(M)\) and \(J\subseteq E\) is \(M\)-dependent with \(|J|=s+1\), then
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(z_j+v_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<s\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=s\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(D\). For \(j\in D\), the set \(J\setminus j\) is independent and has closure \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), so the values \(v_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. For \(j\notin D\), the set \(J\setminus j\) is dependent. Thus the displayed equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation for \(D\), which holds because \(z\in\widetilde L(M)\).

### 5.4. Point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\). Regard \(x\) as the rank-one valuated matroid with coordinates \(x_e\); its tropical linear space is the projective point \([x]\), so it is a quotient of \(M\). Write \(x^\dagger\) for its dagger.

For every quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
\tag{\(*\)}
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(p)=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar multiple of \(M\), so the left side is true because \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), and the right side is true because \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}(p)=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar multiple of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence the left side is false. The right side is also false. Indeed, if
\[
\widetilde L(M)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger),
\]
then for any \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and any \(i\), the \(M\)-cocircuit \(C_{B\setminus b_i}^M\) lies in \(\widetilde L(x^\dagger)\). Testing the circuit equation of \(x^\dagger\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on bases, every element of \(E\) occurs as some \(b_i^*\), so \(x=\infty^E\), contradiction.

Now assume \(1\le \operatorname{rk}(p)=k\le r-1\).

First suppose \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and define
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*\), restriction gives
\[
u_B\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\widetilde L(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\). Then \(y|_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
x^\dagger_{B\setminus b_i}=x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore the circuit equation for \(y\in\widetilde L(x^\dagger)\) holds on every \(M\)-basis \(B\). If \(J\subseteq E\) has size \(r\) and is \(M\)-dependent, the dependent-equation lemma applies to \(v=x^\dagger\), since \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\). Thus all circuit equations for \(x^\dagger\) hold, and
\[
y\in\widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*)\). By ordinary annihilator duality, it is enough to show that every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) lies in \(H_{u_B}\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup b_i},
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
It is the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S^p(e)=p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup e}.
\]
Since \(C_S^p\in\widetilde L(p)\), by assumption \(C_S^p\in\widetilde L(x^\dagger)\). Testing the circuit equation of \(x^\dagger\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{\{b_j:j\in S\}\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\in H_{u_B}\), and hence
\[
u_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*)
=
\widetilde L(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]
Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, it follows that for every \(M\)-basis \(R\),
\[
x|_R\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger|_R).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(n+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(R\). The circuit equation for \(J\) follows from
\[
x|_R\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger|_R).
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the dependent-equation lemma applies to \(v=p^\dagger\), since \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\). Thus every circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) holds, and
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves \((*)\).

### 5.5. Order reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\). If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\). Otherwise, because \(q^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde L(M).
\]
By the point-annihilator theorem applied to \(q\),
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]
Since \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q)\), we get
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(x^\dagger).
\]
Applying the point-annihilator theorem to \(p\), we conclude
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Therefore the assignment
\[
\Phi_M(L(p))=L(p^\dagger)
\]
is order-reversing. It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces rather than on chosen Plücker coordinates: if \(L(p)=L(q)\), then the two affine cones are equal, so the order-reversal just proved gives \(L(p^\dagger)=L(q^\dagger)\). Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), we also have
\[
\Phi_M^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) in the simple modular case. By Section 4, it sends the embedded flat \(F\) to \(F^\perp\).

---

## 6. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be an arbitrary finite matroid whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Section 2:

- \(\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S)\);
- the polarity of \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces a polarity of \(\mathcal L(S)\);
- \(S\) is simple modular;
- the zero-parallel extension map gives a poset isomorphism
  \[
  \Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M);
  \]
- this isomorphism is compatible with the embedded flat subposets.

Let
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
be the order-reversing involution constructed in the simple modular case. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and set \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Since
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
and since \(\Phi_S\) sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\), we obtain
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\). This proves the theorem.

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus tropical conventions, and let \(\Trop(M)\) denote the projective tropical linear space obtained from the affine Bergman cone of \(M\) by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. Let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset, ordered by inclusion, whose elements are projective tropical linear spaces \(X\subseteq\Trop(M)\), equivalently \(X=\Trop(p)\) for a valuated matroid quotient \(p\) of the constant valuated matroid associated to \(M\). For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), let \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) mean the matroid on the original ground set \(E\) whose restriction to \(E\setminus F\) is the contraction \(M/F\) and whose elements in \(F\) are loops. If the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then there exists an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This establishes the original finite-ground-set problem without relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Affine tropical conventions and standard facts

We work first affinely. Let
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
All tropical arithmetic is min-plus. Throughout, a statement that a minimum is “attained twice” means precisely:

> the displayed minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

Thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(q\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
q:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We extend \(q\) by \(q_I=\infty\) whenever \(|I|\ne d\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\left\{
z\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(z_j+q_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
This affine cone includes the all-\(\infty\) vector \(\infty^E\).

Boundary ranks are interpreted as follows.

- If \(d=0\), then the equations for one-element sets force every coordinate of \(z\) to be \(\infty\). Hence
  \[
  \widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\{\infty^E\}.
  \]
- If \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so
  \[
  \widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\mathbb T^E.
  \]

The projective tropical linear space \(\Trop(q)\) is obtained from \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\) by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation \(z\sim z+\lambda\mathbf 1\), \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\). In particular, a rank-zero affine tropical space projectivizes to the empty projective space.

For an ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\), we identify \(M\) with the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ is an \(M\)-basis},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M):=\widetilde{\Trop}(p^M)\) is the affine Bergman cone of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

We use the following standard facts from valuated matroid theory.

**Standard fact 1: quotients and containment.**  
For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(q\).

When \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid regarded as a constant valuated matroid, this quotient condition is equivalent, for rank \(k>0\) \(p\), to the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
having no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\). Rank-zero valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

The support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom Ek:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid \(N\), and if \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), then \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad
\text{for every }A\subseteq E.
\]

**Standard fact 2: coordinate cocircuits.**  
If \(p\) has rank \(k>0\), then for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the vector
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},&e\notin A,\\
\infty,&e\in A,
\end{cases}
\]
belongs to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

**Standard fact 3: ordinary affine annihilator duality.**  
Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its ordinary valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\{v\in\mathbb T^N:\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This includes the rank-zero and full-rank cases.

**Standard fact 4: Plücker uniqueness.**  
A tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated Plücker vector \(p\) up to addition of a global tropical scalar.

We shall also use the standard Bergman fan criterion: for a finite simple matroid \(G\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be its set of loops. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Thus the ground set \(\bar E\) of \(S\) is the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and we write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
for the quotient map.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This map commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical spaces.

We prove that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), then \(p\) descends uniquely to the rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(p\). Indeed, suppose \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\). Let \(A=B-\ell\). Then the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) belongs to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), but
\[
D_A^p(\ell)=p_B<\infty.
\]
This contradicts the loop equation for \(\ell\) in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), which forces the \(\ell\)-coordinate to be \(\infty\). Hence \(p_{A\cup \ell}=\infty\) for all \(A\).

Second, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I-c\). Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), but on the circuit \(C\) its only finite coordinate is
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I<\infty,
\]
because \(C-c\subseteq A\). This violates the circuit equation for \(C\) in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Third, original parallel classes cannot be split, and there is no internal valuated offset. Let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). The two-element set \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), so every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies equality of the two coordinates. Hence for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f).
\]
Consequently,
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f},
\]
with the convention that the value is \(\infty\) if the union is not a \(k\)-set.

This has two consequences. No finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\): if \(B\) did, take \(A=B-f\), so \(e\in A\), and obtain
\[
p_B=D_A^p(f)=D_A^p(e)=\infty.
\]
Also, if \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(f\) is in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
p_{A\cup f}=p_{A\cup e}.
\]

Therefore \(p\) descends to a unique valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\). The preceding paragraph shows that this is independent of the representatives. The Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and the quotient circuit-incidence relations for \(S\) are inherited by choosing representatives of circuits of \(S\). Hence \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(S\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce either to the corresponding relations for \(q\), or to relations with two equal finite terms, or to all-\(\infty\) relations. Hence \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid. Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loop equations impose \(\infty\) on \(L_0\), two-element zero-parallel circuits impose equality within each original parallel class, and after those equations are imposed the remaining circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(q\).

In particular, since
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]
if \(q\) is a quotient of \(S\), then \(q^\uparrow\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Thus \(\Delta\) identifies affine quotient tropical linear spaces of \(S\) with those of \(M\). After projectivizing, it gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
If \(M\) is all loops, then \(S\) is the empty matroid and the projective space \(\Trop(M)\) is empty; the Dressian has only the empty object, and the desired involution is trivial. Henceforth the all-loop case is harmless.

We also need compatibility with the embedded flat subposet. Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of nonloop parallel classes. Thus there is a lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). The matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\). Indeed, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F),
\]
while
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(B)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S\bigl(\pi(B\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}}
\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]

So it suffices to prove the theorem for simple matroids.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple. Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\ge
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\), where \(\rho\) is the lattice rank.

If \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(\mathcal L(M)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself. Hence its dual lattice is also semimodular. Semimodularity of the dual gives the reverse inequality
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Therefore \(M\) is modular.

If \(r=\operatorname{rk}(M)\), then the order-reversing involution satisfies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
In particular, hyperplanes are sent to atoms, and because \(M\) is simple, atoms are single elements.

It remains to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and equipped with a flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

### 4. Flat constancy for quotients in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]
Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the circuit-incidence relation forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Next suppose \(I,I'\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-independent and span the same rank \(k\) flat. It is enough to treat adjacent bases
\[
I=A\cup a,
\qquad
I'=A\cup b.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab),
\]
there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit incidence to \(C\) and \(A\), we get
\[
\min\bigl(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Connectivity of the basis graph of the restriction of \(M\) to a flat gives:

> If \(I,J\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\), then \(p_I=p_J\).

Thus for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\) of \(M\), we may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and set
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
When \(k=0\), this is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). When \(k=r\), this is the rank-zero valuated matroid. We now prove that for all \(k\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The boundary cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate from the definition, so for the Plücker and quotient-incidence proofs assume \(0<k<r\).

---

### 6. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It suffices to check the three-term valuated Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all six \(n\)-subsets in the displayed relation are \(M\)-dependent, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in every opposite pairing one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent, so again all three sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) is equivalent to \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which means \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent; otherwise modularity gives \(\operatorname{rk}(H_e\wedge H_f)=k\).

So the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

#### Lemma 6.1: local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),
& H_i\ne H_j\text{ and }\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,\\
\infty,
&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third sums are equal, and the claim follows.

Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, two distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\), so \([ij]=\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j)\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting four hyperplanes in a rank \(k+2\) modular flat gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank \(k\) flat containing \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). All three opposite sums are therefore equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j\), so
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the interval. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting in the contraction by \(Z\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image is \(D\). Applying circuit incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we get
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}([Z,Y])\).

Now apply the following rank-three edge lemma to the lines \(H_i/Z\) in the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). It gives precisely the desired relation. ∎

#### Lemma 6.2: rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). For distinct lines, write \(L_i\wedge L_j\) for their intersection point, and set
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two lines repeat, the same argument as in Lemma 6.1 gives two equal opposite sums, so assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, this chain has the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, with either level allowed to be absent. Therefore
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes positive cost only when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. For four colored objects, among the three perfect matchings, the minimum equal-color cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4,
\]
and in every case either all matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum. This remains valid when some costs are \(\infty\).

Now suppose one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), let
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of the three \(c(y_j)\) equal the minimum value \(\beta\). If exactly two of them are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Lemmas 6.1 and 6.2 prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

### 7. Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\in\binom E{n-1}.
\]
We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

We need a codimension-one polarity lemma.

#### Lemma 7.1: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

**Proof.** By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every real \(\lambda\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

A set of points in a finite simple modular matroid is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set
\[
a_1,\dots,a_m\in S.
\]
Induct on \(m\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness. For \(m\ge3\), let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, all points of \(H\) are in \(S\). If \(x\in F\setminus H\) and \(x\ne a_m\), modularity implies that the line \(\operatorname{cl}(x,a_m)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Then \(h,a_m\in S\), so line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is a flat.

Now let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in the contraction. Set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\), and
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2
\]
implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\).

Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) containing
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
which has rank \(k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting that circuit to \(M\) and applying circuit incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, therefore a flat. Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\). ∎

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) are loops in the contraction \(M/X\), and for them \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so they contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then \(P_c\) is a point of \(M/X\), and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
Parallel elements in \(M/X\) have the same value \(u(P_c)\), and loops have value \(\infty\), so \(u\) lifts from the simplification \(N\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise set
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
Every element of \(D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\): for \(c\in D\), since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\), we have
\[
c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c).
\]
Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of the restriction \((M/X)|D\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). Therefore the circuit-incidence condition holds for \(p^\dagger\).

Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
so \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 8. Involutivity and flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), the same flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\). We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both sides have value \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is involutive.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\) of \(M\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), equivalently
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)=r
\]
and \(M\) is modular, this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus finite bases of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) correspond exactly to lattice complements of \(F\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 9. The affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) as follows. For every \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). We say \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove:

#### Theorem 9.1: affine point-annihilator duality

For every rank \(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

**Proof.** We first treat \(0<k<r\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Indeed, if an element is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\), then its fundamental circuit over \(B\) uses some \(b_j\) with \(j\notin S\), so it is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_j)\).

Applying the polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Thus
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]=\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because the support of \(p\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has support-rank \(k\); hence \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from those of \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\). For \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
So
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Assume first that
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\) and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the global coordinate cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},&e\notin A,\\
\infty,&e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(D_A^p\) is all \(\infty\), the desired equation is automatic. Otherwise
\[
D_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(D_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is exactly the circuit equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T-i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\).

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now verify all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\). For those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
is independent of \(j\), so the Plücker coefficient is constant. The equation reduces to the circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), which holds. Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

This proves the theorem for \(0<k<r\).

Now consider the boundary cases.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
while \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), both sides of the equivalence hold.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero:
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B-b_i.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Hence this term cannot be finite, so \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Varying \(B\) and \(i\), and using that polar bases cover all elements of \(E\), we get \(x=\infty^E\). Thus the equivalence holds for \(k=r\) as well. ∎

Projectivizing Theorem 9.1 is immediate: delete \(\infty^E\) and quotient by tropical scalar translation. The equations defining \(\widetilde H_x\) are homogeneous, so the projective hyperplane \(H_x\) is well-defined. In rank zero, the affine singleton \(\{\infty^E\}\) becomes the empty projective tropical space; in top rank \(k=r\), the dagger is rank zero and therefore projectively empty.

---

### 10. The involution in the simple modular case

Let \(X\in\Dr(M)\). Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\{[x]\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq H_x\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then Theorem 9.1 gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi(X)\) is again an element of \(\Dr(M)\). This definition is intrinsic in \(X\); equivalently, by the standard Plücker uniqueness theorem, changing \(p\) by a global tropical scalar does not change \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\).

Because dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple modular matroids with a flat-lattice polarity.

---

### 11. Return to the original matroid

Let \(M\) now be an arbitrary finite matroid, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending this induced flat-lattice involution.

From Section 2, the diagonal map gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Section 2 gives
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Trop(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

The theorem follows. ∎

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained finite-ground-set theorem.

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus tropical conventions. For a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\mathbb T^E\) denote its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector, and let
\[
\Trop(p)=(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)
\]
be its projectivization, possibly empty. Regard an ordinary matroid \(M\) as its constant valuated matroid. Define
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p\text{ is a valuated matroid on }E\text{ and }\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), let \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) mean the matroid on the original ground set \(E\) obtained by contracting \(F\) and making every element of \(F\) a loop; this gives the standard embedded flat subposet
\[
F\longmapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If the flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp,
\]
then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

This is the original problem with the finite-ground-set convention made explicit; no other hypothesis is changed.

## Proof

### 1. Affine/projective conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts

Let \(E\) be finite and let
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
We use min-plus conventions: tropical scalar multiplication is addition of a constant, and tropical addition is minimum. A statement that a minimum is “attained at least twice” always means “there is no unique finite minimizer”; in particular, a minimum whose every term is \(\infty\) satisfies the condition.

A rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ek\to\mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), whose finite support is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(k\) matroid and whose Plücker coordinates satisfy the tropical Plücker relations. We extend \(p\) by \(p_I=\infty\) whenever \(|I|\ne k\).

The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{k+1},\
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J-j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
It always contains \(\infty^E=(\infty,\dots,\infty)\). Its projectivization is
\[
\Trop(p)=
(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\})/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1).
\]
If \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), then \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\).

An ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ is a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard facts about valuated matroids.

**Standard Fact 1: uniqueness.**  
The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated matroid \(p\) up to adding one global scalar to all Plücker coordinates. Therefore \(\Trop(p)\) also determines \(p\) up to global scalar.

**Standard Fact 2: containment and quotients.**  
For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). In particular, a valuated matroid \(p\) is a quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\) precisely when
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), and a \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), define the coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e},
\]
with the convention that \(C_A^p(e)=\infty\) if \(e\in A\). Then \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is the all-\(\infty\) vector.

**Standard Fact 3: tropical span of cocircuits.**  
The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the tropical convex span of its valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\). Consequently, because ordinary tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex, for an ordinary matroid \(M\),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if every coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) satisfies every circuit equation of \(M\). Equivalently, \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We shall use this circuit-incidence criterion repeatedly.

**Standard Fact 4: ordinary annihilator duality.**  
Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_{N\setminus B}=\mu_B.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This includes the boundary ranks: if \(\mu\) has rank \(0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty^N\}\); if \(\mu\) has full rank, then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\).

Finally, we record a useful support-level consequence of (CI). Let \(p\) be a valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\), and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the finite \(p\)-bases. Then \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), in the following sense:
\[
\tag{1}
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad\text{for all }A\subseteq E.
\]
Indeed, if \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit and \(e\in C\), then \(e\) cannot be a coloop of \(N|C\). Otherwise choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\), and apply (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\): the term \(p_{A\cup e}\) would be the unique finite term. Thus every element of every \(M\)-circuit lies in some \(N\)-circuit contained in that \(M\)-circuit. If \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup e\) containing \(e\); then an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\) contains \(e\), so \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Hence \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), which proves (1).

In particular, if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\tag{2}
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]

---

### 2. Simplification invariance

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the loop set, and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\). Write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
for the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
The map \(\Delta\) commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical space.

Given a valuated matroid \(q\) on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce to those for \(q\): a finite term can occur only when no loop and no repeated parallel class appears; if exactly one parallel class is repeated, the two potentially finite terms are equal; otherwise all terms are \(\infty\). Hence \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\tag{3}
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)).
\]
Indeed, loops force the corresponding coordinates to be \(\infty\), zero-offset parallel pairs force equality of coordinates inside each original parallel class, and after imposing these equations the remaining circuit equations are exactly those of \(q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid of \(S\), we get
\[
\tag{4}
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

If \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(p\), so descent is immediate. Suppose \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(p\). If \(\ell\in L_0\) occurred in a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), put \(A=B-\ell\). Then
\[
C_A^p(\ell)=p_B<\infty.
\]
But \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), while every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has coordinate \(\ell\) equal to \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, original parallel classes remain zero-offset parallel classes. Let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore, for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^p(e)=C_A^p(f),
\]
i.e.
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
This has two consequences.

1. No finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\). If \(B\) did, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\), while \(p_{A\cup f}=p_B<\infty\), contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation:
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]

Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any representative set \(I\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\). The Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)).
\]
Using (4) and injectivity of \(\Delta\) on the non-loop coordinates,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
So \(\bar p\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, if \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then by (3) and (4),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q))
\subseteq
\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S))
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
so \(q^\uparrow\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\tag{5}
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

It remains to check compatibility with the embedded flat subposets. Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). For
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\qquad
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F},
\]
we have, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F).
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)=\operatorname{rk}_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
we get
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{N_F}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is precisely the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,
\[
\tag{6}
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]

---

### 3. Reduction to the simple modular case

An order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through the lattice isomorphism \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S)\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Therefore, by Section 2, it is enough to prove the theorem for simple finite matroids.

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\). Its lattice of flats is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Let \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) be the order-reversing involution. Since \(\alpha\) reverses chains,
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Apply semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\):
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
and \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F)\), this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all flats:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a simple modular matroid. We do not use any classification of finite modular matroids.

---

### 4. The simple modular dagger construction

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\), and that \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is the given order-reversing involution of its flat lattice.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 4.1 Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same rank \(k\) flat. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\) shows that
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of every restriction \(M|F\) is connected, \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases of a fixed rank \(k\) flat \(F\). Define
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

#### 4.2 Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(J\in\binom En\), define
\[
\tag{7}
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),
&J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\). If \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

Boundary ranks are immediate.

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to global scalar.
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the rank \(0\) valuated matroid, up to global scalar.

Assume for the rest of this section that \(0<k<r\).

#### 4.3 The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). Define, for flats \(G\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)=\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and for arbitrary \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)=0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=0,
\]
and
\[
\rho^\dagger(E)=r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)=n.
\]

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). By (2),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp).
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
we get
\[
\rho^\dagger(H)\ge\rho^\dagger(G).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cap H^\perp)
+
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp\cup H^\perp).
\]
Now
\[
G^\perp\cap H^\perp=(G\vee H)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(G^\perp\cup H^\perp)
=
G^\perp\vee H^\perp
=
(G\wedge H)^\perp.
\]
Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), ranks in \(N\) do not change after taking \(M\)-closure. Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
Using monotonicity, this extends to arbitrary subsets \(A,B\):
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)+\rho^\dagger(B)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(A\cap B)+\rho^\dagger(A\cup B).
\]
Also
\[
0\le\rho^\dagger(A)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(A)\le |A|.
\]
Submodularity implies single-element increments are at most \(1\). Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function; call the resulting rank \(n\) matroid \(N^\dagger\).

For \(J\in\binom En\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\). Since \(\rho^\dagger(J)\le\operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n\), this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. Then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is precisely the set of bases of the matroid \(N^\dagger\).

#### 4.4 Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the three-term Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every appearing \(n\)-set is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in every displayed summand at least one of the two \(n\)-sets is dependent; hence all three summands are \(\infty\).

So assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), equivalently \(Sef\) is dependent.

Thus the desired Plücker relation follows from the following lemma.

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23])
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third displayed sums are equal, possibly both \(\infty\). So the claim holds.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank \(k\) flat containing \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). All three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\), and if \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) so that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
\quad
\text{whenever }\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}.
\]
Hence the displayed relation is exactly the usual tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\), after simplification, is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the simplified interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge lemma.

#### Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\Trop(G)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, with the convention that if two lines coincide then the corresponding meet term is \(\infty\).

If two lines coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument used above applies. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

For an ordinary matroid, \(w\in\Trop(G)\) iff every superlevel set
\[
\{x:w_x\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat. Since \(G\) has rank three, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a chain consisting of possibly a point \(a\) and possibly a line \(B\) with \(a\le B\). After subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) when one level is absent.

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), set
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
So a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes a nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree, and the cost depends only on that common color. Among the three perfect matchings of four colored objects, the minimum equal-color matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in every case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma. Applying it inside \([Z,Y]\), with \(L_i=H_i/Z\), proves the local dual Plücker lemma. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

Together with the support calculation, \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

#### 4.5 Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). It suffices to verify (CI) for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must show
\[
\tag{\(\dagger\)-CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
We first prove \(u\in\Trop(G)\).

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\). In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat iff it is line-closed. Indeed, if \(S\) is line-closed and \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) is an independent set spanning a flat \(F\), then induction on \(m\) shows every point of \(F\) lies in \(S\): for \(m\le2\) this is line-closedness; for \(m\ge3\), write \(H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\). If \(x\in F\setminus H\), modularity implies the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). By induction \(h\in S\), and line-closedness gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(G\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2
\]
implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise set
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying (CI) to \(B\) gives
\[
\min(u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3))
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\Trop(G)\).

Return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), then every term in \((\dagger\)-CI) is \(\infty\). Otherwise let
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
Every element \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\): indeed,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D-c)=\operatorname{rk}_{M/X}(D)
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\).

If the minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would violate \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). Hence \((\dagger\)-CI) holds. Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

So \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Involutivity and flat compatibility

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it as well.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
\tag{8}
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Now let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\) of \(M\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) means an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis after contracting \(F\), equivalently \(G\vee F=E\); by modularity and the rank condition this is equivalent to \(G\wedge F=\varnothing\).

Let \(H\) be a rank \(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). Applying the polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\tag{9}
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
up to the same harmless global scalar. Hence
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

### 6. The affine point-annihilator theorem

We continue in the simple modular case.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar atom
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\tag{10}
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
The last equality follows from fundamental circuits with respect to the basis \(B\).

Taking ranks in (10), \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis: if \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), then for an \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(B)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(B))=\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=k.
\]

Define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
It has rank \(k\). By (10), for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
Thus
\[
\tag{11}
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids.

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane inside \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) by
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_i\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
If \(x\ne\infty^E\), then \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), and the rank \(r-1\) quotient \(x^\dagger\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(x^\dagger).
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

We prove the affine point-annihilator theorem:

\[
\tag{12}
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Boundary ranks first. If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\qquad
p^\dagger=M,
\]
so both sides of (12) hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), both sides hold. Conversely, suppose \(x\ne\infty^E\). Choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let \(A=B-b_i\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has, on \(B\), only one finite coordinate, namely at \(b_i\). Thus the basis equation defining \(\widetilde H_x\) has a unique finite minimizer. Hence \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\nsubseteq\widetilde H_x\). So (12) holds for \(k=r\).

Now assume \(0<k<r\).

First suppose
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
By (11),
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also
\[
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), so
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and let \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=p_{A\cup e}
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\), testing \(C_A^p\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]

Therefore \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every polar Boolean apartment \(B^*\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly on every \(M\)-basis.

Now check all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis; the corresponding apartment equation gives the desired relation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the equation for \(p^\dagger\) occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
is independent of \(j\). Thus the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Hence
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves (12).

Projectivization is harmless: deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation gives the corresponding projective statement. Rank-zero affine cones become empty projective tropical spaces.

---

### 7. Construction of the involution and extension to the original matroid

First assume \(M\) is simple modular. For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\tag{13}
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This is well-defined. Indeed, by the uniqueness theorem for tropical linear spaces, \(p\) is determined by \(\Trop(p)\) up to global scalar, and adding a global scalar to \(p\) adds the same scalar to \(p^\dagger\). Equivalently, by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\},
\]
which depends only on \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\).

By (8),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q),
\]
then for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\), the point-annihilator theorem gives
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Trop(q^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(p^\dagger),
\]
and \(\Phi\) reverses inclusions.

Finally, by (9), for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the theorem is proved for simple \(M\).

Now return to an arbitrary finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). Section 2 gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M),
\]
compatible with flat quotients. The order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends to one on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending that descended flat-lattice involution.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by conjugation:
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta_*(X))=\Delta_*(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\), and by (6),
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the desired order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with the elements of \(F\) made loops.

---

## Proof

We use min-plus tropical conventions. Put \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A minimum is said to be attained twice if it has no unique finite minimizer; an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), extend \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq d\), and define its affine tropical linear space by
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) is obtained by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation \(x\sim x+\lambda\mathbf 1\).

We identify an ordinary matroid \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

The elements of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) are precisely the projective tropical linear spaces \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), equivalently the projectivizations of valuated matroid quotients \(p\) of \(M\).

We first record several standard facts that will be used explicitly.

### 1. Basic valuated-matroid facts

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Indeed, for every \(J\in\binom E{k+1}\), the condition
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(D_A^p(j)+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\]
