is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) indexed by \(A\) and \(J\).

The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) is the tropical cone generated by these valuated cocircuits. Consequently, for an ordinary matroid \(M\), a rank \(k>0\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
if and only if, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Rank \(0\) valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical linear space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

If \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), its finite support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom Ek:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(k\) matroid \(N\), and \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad
\text{for all }A\subseteq E.
\]
In particular, if \(X\le Y\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X).
\]

We shall also use the standard local criterion for valuated matroids: if a function \(q:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T\) has matroidal finite support, then \(q\) is a valuated matroid if and only if all three-term tropical Plücker relations
\[
\min\bigl(
q_{Sab}+q_{Scd},\
q_{Sac}+q_{Sbd},\
q_{Sad}+q_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
have no unique finite minimizer, for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\).

Finally, for a valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), put
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
Indeed, \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is tropically generated by the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and those cocircuits are precisely the valuated circuits defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\). Boundary ranks agree: rank \(0\) gives \(\{\infty^N\}\), and full rank gives all of \(\mathbb T^N\).

We shall also use the following Bergman criterion. If \(G\) is a finite simple matroid and \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\), then
\[
w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)
\]
if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\),
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\). If a superlevel set is not a flat, choose \(e\notin S_\lambda\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(S_\lambda)\); then some circuit \(C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit has a unique finite minimizer \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the next smallest value on the circuit; then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda\) but \(e\notin S_\lambda\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not flat.

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Its ground set \(\bar E\) is the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the projection to parallel classes.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends projectively.

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The finite support of \(q^\uparrow\) is obtained from the finite support of \(q\) by replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class and adding loops. Hence its support is matroidal. The three-term Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) are either ordinary Plücker relations for \(q\), or all-\(\infty\), or have two identical finite terms because two elements lie in the same parallel class. Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loop elements force the corresponding coordinates to be \(\infty\), zero-offset parallel pairs force equality of the corresponding coordinates, and after those equations are imposed the remaining tropical linear equations are exactly the equations defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]
Thus, if \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), it descends uniquely to the rank \(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

Every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\). If a loop \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), then with \(A=B-\ell\) the cocircuit \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) would have finite \(\ell\)-coordinate, contradicting the loop equation for \(M\).

Similarly, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite basis \(B\) of \(p\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\), set \(A=B-c\), and observe that \(D_A^p\) has a unique finite minimum on \(C\).

Now let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Since \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), every vector of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has equal \(e\)- and \(f\)-coordinates. Hence
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f)
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). Therefore no finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\), and whenever \(A\cup e\) is a finite basis with \(f\) parallel to \(e\), one has
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus \(p\) descends to a unique valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), it follows that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S),
\]
so \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Hence \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The flat lattices of \(M\) and \(S\) are canonically isomorphic:
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), writing \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), the matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless, of rank \(r\), and that its lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

A finite geometric lattice is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism of a ranked lattice of rank \(r\),
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp\) and \(Y^\perp\), and translating back using
\[
(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp,
\qquad
(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp,
\]
gives the reverse inequality
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

Since \(M\) is simple, the atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) are the elements of \(E\). The polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms.

---

## 4. Flat constancy for valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of this simple modular \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, if \(C\subseteq I\) is an \(M\)-circuit and \(c\in C\), then for \(A=I-c\) the quotient incidence condition gives a one-term minimum unless \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose \(I,J\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\). The basis graph of \(M|X\) is connected, so it suffices to handle adjacent bases \(I=A\cup a\) and \(J=A\cup b\). Since both span \(X\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying quotient incidence to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore \(p_I=p_J\).

Hence for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\) of \(M\), one may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

## 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and set
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,&
J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

We prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), a rank \(k\) matroid quotient of \(M\), and write
\[
\nu(X)=\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\]
for flats \(X\) of \(M\).

Define, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
h(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(A)
+
\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)
-
k.
\]
We claim that \(h\) is a matroid rank function of rank \(r-k=n\).

Indeed, \(h(\varnothing)=0\) and \(h(E)=r-k\). Also
\[
0\le h(A)\le |A|.
\]
For \(X\le Y\) flats of \(M\),
\[
h(Y)-h(X)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X)
-
\bigl(\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)\bigr)
\ge 0,
\]
because \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), so rank increments in \(N\) are bounded by rank increments in \(M\). Thus \(h\) is monotone.

For flats \(X,Y\), modularity of \(M\) cancels the \(\rho\)-terms:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&h(X)+h(Y)-h(X\vee Y)-h(X\wedge Y)\\
&=
\nu(X^\perp)+\nu(Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp)
\ge 0,
\end{aligned}
\]
by submodularity of \(\nu\). For arbitrary subsets, replace them by their closures and use monotonicity. Therefore \(h\) is submodular, hence a matroid rank function.

The bases of this matroid are exactly the finite-support sets of \(p^\dagger\). Indeed, for \(J\in\binom En\),
\[
h(J)=n
\]
if and only if \(J\) is \(M\)-independent and
\[
\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{supp}(p^\dagger)\) is matroidal.

It remains to prove the valuated Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Because the support is already matroidal, it suffices to prove the three-term relations.

Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all six terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every opposite pairing contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all three sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), put
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

We need the following local lemma.

### Lemma 5.1

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\
[13]+[24],\
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Hence the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting successively with hyperplanes lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), and all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\), so
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to consider \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the interval. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\) for each \(P\in D\). The circuit \(D\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}([Z,Y])\).

Now apply the following rank-three edge lemma to the four lines \(H_i/Z\) in \([Z,Y]\). ∎

### Lemma 5.2

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\). For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(G\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two lines coincide, two of the three opposite sums are equal, so there is nothing to prove. Thus assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the result is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so \(\min w=0\). By the Bergman superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, this chain has the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, either of which may be absent. Thus
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}
+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Therefore a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes positive cost only when \(y_i=y_j\). Among four colored objects, the three perfect matchings never have a unique minimum equal-color cost: the color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad
2+1+1,\quad
2+2,\quad
3+1,\quad
4,
\]
and in each case either all matchings tie or at least two avoid the repeated color. The conclusion follows.

Now suppose one of the lines is \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the \(c(y_j)\) attain the minimum. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Lemmas 5.1 and 5.2 prove the three-term Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Since the support of \(p^\dagger\) is matroidal, \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\) and \(\rho(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G).
\]
By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every real \(\lambda\),
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\). Inducting on \(m\), all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) lie in \(S\). If \(x\in F\) is outside that hyperplane, modularity forces the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) to meet the hyperplane in a point already in \(S\); line-closedness then gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). Set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise the three hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) contain the rank \(k-1\) flat
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting to \(M\) and applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, and therefore flat. Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\), constant on parallel classes and \(\infty\) on loops.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise let \(D=C\setminus X\). Every \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\), because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\) implies \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c)\). Therefore a unique finite minimum on \(D\) would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Hence the quotient incidence condition holds.

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Dagger is involutive and sends flat quotients to flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it. We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore dagger is an involution.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\). For a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when \(G\vee F=E\). Since \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(G)+\rho(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\). The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 7. Affine annihilator duality inside \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\)

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define an affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) as follows.

For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). We declare that \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Hence
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on bases.

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because \(\operatorname{supp}(p)\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has support-rank \(k\), so \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual \(\mu_B^*\). Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For a fixed \(M\)-basis \(B\), put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\).

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the valuated cocircuit \(D_A^p\). If \(D_A^p\equiv \infty\), the corresponding equation for \(\mu_B^*\) is automatic. Otherwise,
\[
D_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on \(B\), we obtain
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the tropical linear equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\). Therefore
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now check the global equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\in\binom E{r-k+1}\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis; the desired equation follows from the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for such \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is independent of \(j\). Thus the equation reduces to the circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\).

The boundary cases are immediate. If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\) and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), testing the cocircuits associated to \(M\)-bases forces every coordinate of \(x\) to be \(\infty\). Hence the equivalence also holds in rank \(r\).

Therefore, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Projectivizing gives the corresponding statement in tropical projective space.

---

## 8. The involution in the simple modular case

For a projective tropical linear subspace \(X\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)=
\left\{
[x]\in \operatorname{Trop}(M):
X\subseteq H_x
\right\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), then the projectivized annihilator equivalence gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Since dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x \implies X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty projectively, \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has one element, and the result is trivial.

Otherwise, the flat-lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given order-reversing involution to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, \(S\) has an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending this polarity on flats.

Using the poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and write \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). From the simplification compatibility,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

This completes the proof. ∎

## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Define
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid on }E,\ 
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\},
\]
ordered by inclusion after projectivization. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
M_F:=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with all elements of \(F\) made loops.

If the flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

We use min-plus tropical conventions, and “a minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer”; thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

---

## 1. Standard valuated-matroid facts

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), extended by \(p_I=\infty\) if \(|I|\neq k\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{k+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization is
\[
\Trop(p)=\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1).
\]

We use the following standard Dress–Wenzel/Murota–Tamura valuated-matroid facts.

1. For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
   \[
   C_A^p(e):=p_{A\cup e}.
   \]
   Then every non-all-\(\infty\) \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the tropical convex span of these cocircuits.

2. For an ordinary matroid \(M\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) is cut out by the circuit equations
   \[
   \min_{c\in C} x_c
   \quad\text{has no unique finite minimizer}
   \]
   for all ordinary circuits \(C\) of \(M\).

3. Hence, for \(k>0\),
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
   \]
   if and only if for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \tag{CI}
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so containment is automatic.

4. A function \(p:\binom E k\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with matroidal finite support is a valuated matroid if and only if all three-term tropical Plücker relations hold:
   \[
   \min\bigl(
   p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\
   p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\
   p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
   \bigr)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer for every \(S\in\binom E{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\).

5. If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for
   \[
   H_u=\{v\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^N:
   \min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   \tag{AD}
   u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
   \Longleftrightarrow
   \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]
   Indeed, circuits of \(\mu^*\) are cocircuits of \(\mu\). Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)\) precisely when \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\) is the tropical convex span of the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(H_u\) is tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).

We shall also use the following elementary ordinary Bergman-fan criterion.

**Superlevel-flat criterion.** Let \(G\) be an ordinary matroid on \(E(G)\), and let \(w\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{E(G)}\). Then
\[
w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)
\]
if and only if every superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda=\{e\in E(G): w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\), for every real \(\lambda\).

Indeed, if some \(S_\lambda\) is not flat, choose \(e\notin S_\lambda\) and a circuit \(C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). Then \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C-e\subseteq S_\lambda\), \(e\notin S_\lambda\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not flat.

---

## 2. A support quotient lemma

Let \(p\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the finite \(p\)-bases.

First, if \(I\in\binom E k\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I-c\), the condition (CI) applied to \(C\) and \(A\) has only the term \(p_I\), so \(p_I\) cannot be finite.

Next, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\tag{1}
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]
It suffices to show \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq \operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Let \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Choose an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). We claim that \(e\) lies in an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\). If not, then \(e\) is a coloop of \(N|C\). Choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), and let \(B\) be an \(N\)-basis of \(H\). Then \(p_{B\cup e}<\infty\), while \(p_{B\cup c}=\infty\) for every \(c\in C-e\). This contradicts (CI). Hence \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), proving (1).

In particular, if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\tag{2}
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]

---

## 3. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\), and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes. Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\).

Define
\[
\Delta:(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{\bar E}\to(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, hence descends projectively.

If \(q\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce to those for \(q\), with repeated parallel classes contributing either no finite terms or two equal finite terms. Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid, and
\[
\tag{3}
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)).
\]
In particular,
\[
\tag{4}
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Conversely, let \(p\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
If \(p\) has positive rank, then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), and every parallel class of \(M\) remains a zero-offset parallel class for \(p\). Indeed, if \(\ell\in L_0\) appeared in a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), then the cocircuit \(C_{B-\ell}^p\) would have finite \(\ell\)-coordinate, contradicting \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=x_f\). Hence, for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
This implies that no finite \(p\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class, and that replacing a representative of a parallel class preserves the valuation.

Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\) with
\[
p=\bar p^\uparrow.
\]
Using (3) and (4),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\tag{5}
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Flats of \(M\) are exactly unions of non-loop parallel classes together with \(L_0\). Hence
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism. Moreover, if \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\tag{6}
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore it is enough to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

## 4. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\), and suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Because \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
The involution reverses maximal chains, so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
So \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Hence, in the simple case, \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

It remains to construct the involution for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) equipped with the fixed polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

## 5. The dagger construction

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

### 5.1 Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom E k\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). If \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same rank \(k\) flat, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of a matroid is connected, \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of any rank \(k\) flat \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

Define a function \(p^\dagger:\binom E n\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) by
\[
\tag{7}
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty\).

### 5.2 The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). Define, for a flat \(G\) of \(M\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and for arbitrary \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)=0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=0,
\qquad
\rho^\dagger(E)=r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)=n.
\]
If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats, then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\). By (2),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
so \(\rho^\dagger(H)\ge \rho^\dagger(G)\). Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\), together with (1), gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\vee H)^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N((G\wedge H)^\perp).
\]
Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
Using monotonicity, this extends to arbitrary subsets \(A,B\):
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)+\rho^\dagger(B)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(A\cap B)+\rho^\dagger(A\cup B).
\]
Also,
\[
0\le \rho^\dagger(A)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(A)\le |A|.
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function. Let \(N^\dagger\) be the resulting rank \(n\) matroid.

For \(J\in\binom E n\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) if and only if \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\). Since \(\rho^\dagger(J)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n\), this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent, and then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n
\Longleftrightarrow
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k
\Longleftrightarrow
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Thus the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is precisely the set of bases of \(N^\dagger\).

### 5.3 Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

The boundary cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate: if \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\); if \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\).

Assume \(0<k<r\). We verify the three-term Plücker relations.

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(n\)-sets are dependent, so all three Plücker terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then each displayed Plücker summand contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all terms are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while \([13]=[23]\) and \([14]=[24]\). Thus the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, intersecting three hyperplanes inside the rank \(k+2\) flat \(Y\) lowers rank by at most \(3\), so \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)\ge k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) could lower rank by at most \(1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\).

If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
T_m\wedge T_n=Z.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)=k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
\qquad
\text{whenever }\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification \(G\) of the interval \([Z,Y]\). This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show that \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(G\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup \{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\), the elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(w\) satisfies every circuit equation of \(G\), so \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\).

We now use the following rank-three fact.

**Rank-three edge lemma.** Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). If \(L_i=L_j\), define \(w(L_i\wedge L_j)=\infty\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), this is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel flats form a chain. In rank three, such a chain consists of at most a point \(a\) contained in at most a line \(B\). Hence
\[
w(x)=\alpha\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, the repeated-line argument above applies. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) if and only if \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a pair contributes a nonzero cost exactly when two colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. Among the three perfect matchings of four colored objects, the minimum equal-color matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4,
\]
and
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite.

Applying the rank-three edge lemma inside \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies all three-term tropical Plücker relations. Since its finite support is the matroid \(N^\dagger\), the three-term characterization implies that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

### 5.4 \(p^\dagger\) is again contained in \(\Trop(M)\)

We prove
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For \(k=0\), \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\), and for \(k=r\), \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so both cases are immediate. Assume \(0<k<r\).

By (CI), it suffices to check the following: for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\),
\[
\tag{8}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
We claim \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\).

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show every superlevel set of \(u\) is a flat. In a finite simple modular matroid, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if a point \(x\) lies in the closure of independent points \(a_1,\dots,a_m\), induction on \(m\), together with modularity, shows \(x\) can be obtained by repeatedly taking points on lines spanned by points already present.

So take \(P_1,P_2\) in a superlevel set, and let \(P_3\) be another point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise set
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying (CI) to \(B\) gives
\[
\min(u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3))
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Therefore if \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), then \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus every superlevel set is line-closed, hence flat, and \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\).

Now return to the circuit \(C\). If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms in (8) are \(\infty\). Otherwise let
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
Every \(c\in D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\), because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Since the pullback of \(u\) to \(M/X\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) cannot be uniquely finite. Hence (8) holds.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated matroid whose tropical linear space is contained in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

### 5.5 Involutivity

We show
\[
\tag{9}
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\in\binom E k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

### 5.6 Compatibility with flats

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) precisely means that an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) becomes a basis after contracting \(F\), equivalently \(G\vee F=E\). By modularity and the rank condition, this is equivalent to \(G\wedge F=\varnothing\).

Now let \(H\) be a rank \(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) if and only if \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), which is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
\tag{10}
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
up to an irrelevant global scalar. Thus
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 6. The affine annihilator theorem

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\tag{11}
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp.
\]
It remains to note that
\[
\bigcap_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
For \(e\notin B\), this follows from the fundamental circuit \(C(e,B)\): one has \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\) if and only if \(b_i\notin C(e,B)\). Elements of \(B\) are immediate. This proves (11).

Taking \(S=\{1,\dots,r\}\), we see that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis. Moreover, applying (11) with \(S=[r]\setminus\{i\}\) gives
\[
(B^*)^*=B.
\]
Also, every element \(e\in E\) occurs as a polar atom \(b_i^*\): choose a basis \(A\) of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\), choose \(b\notin e^\perp\), and set \(B=A\cup\{b\}\). Then the polar atom corresponding to \(b\) is \(e\).

Now let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}.
\]
The support of \(\mu_B\) is nonempty because \(B\) contains a basis of the support matroid \(N\): indeed,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(B)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(B))=\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=k.
\]

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\), equation (11) gives
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\bigr)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
Thus
\[
\tag{12}
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_i\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We prove the affine annihilator theorem:
\[
\tag{13}
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\Longleftrightarrow
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger=M\), so both sides hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Hence \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}\). If \(x=\infty^E\), both sides hold. Conversely, if \(x_e<\infty\) for some \(e\), choose a basis \(B=\{b_i\}\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let \(A=B-b_i\), and consider the cocircuit \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). On \(B\), it has a unique finite coordinate at \(b_i\). Therefore the defining condition for \(\widetilde H_x\) fails. Thus \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\not\subseteq \widetilde H_x\).

Now assume \(0<k<r\).

First suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
By (12),
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By annihilator duality (AD),
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we get \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\). Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and let \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired circuit equation is automatic. Since \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\), testing on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is exactly the circuit equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]

Thus \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every polar Boolean apartment \(B^*\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check every circuit equation of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{n+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis; the corresponding apartment equation gives the desired relation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<n\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=n\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). For \(j\notin D\), the set \(J-j\) is dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{J-j}=\infty\). For \(j\in D\), the sets \(J-j\) are independent and all have the same \(M\)-closure, so the values \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) are all equal. Therefore the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) reduces to the ordinary circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D} x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

This proves (13).

---

## 7. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

Let \(X\in\Dr(M)\). Let \(\widetilde X\) be its affine cone, including \(\infty^E\). Choose \(p\) with
\[
\widetilde X=\widetilde{\Trop}(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined by the affine annihilator theorem, because
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\},
\]
which depends only on \(\widetilde X\), not on the choice of \(p\).

By (9),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]

Projective inclusion is equivalent to affine-cone inclusion after adjoining \(\infty^E\), because tropical linear spaces are invariant under adding scalar multiples of \((1,\dots,1)\). Thus, if \(X\subseteq Y\), then \(\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde Y\). Hence
\[
\{x:\widetilde Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}
\subseteq
\{x:\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\},
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by (10), for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the theorem holds for finite simple \(M\).

---

## 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). By Section 3, there is a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
compatible with the embedded flat subposets. The flat-lattice involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through the lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending the descended flat-lattice involution.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by conjugation:
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta_*(X))=\Delta_*(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Since \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\). Using (6),
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the desired order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

## The finite-ground-set theorem

Let \(M\) be a **finite** matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with all elements of \(F\) made loops.

We prove the theorem only for finite matroids.

---

### 1. Conventions and basic valuated-matroid facts

Work over the min-plus tropical semiring \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A minimum is said to have **no unique finite minimizer** if either it is attained at least twice among finite terms, or all terms are \(\infty\).

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), extend \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq d\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Its projectivization is denoted \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\).

An ordinary matroid \(M\) is identified with its constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of \(M\) if
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\). The relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) is the set of projective tropical linear spaces \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), ordered by inclusion.

We use the following standard facts.

#### Valuated cocircuit generation and quotient incidence

Let \(p\) be rank \(d\). For \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), define
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) is the tropical span of these valuated cocircuits.

Hence, for an ordinary matroid \(M\), a rank \(d>0\) valuated matroid \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Rank \(0\) valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical linear space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

#### Support quotients

If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), its finite support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom E d:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary rank \(d\) matroid \(N\). Moreover \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\): for every \(R\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(R)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M R).
\]

Indeed, suppose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(R)\) but \(\operatorname{rk}_N(R\cup e)>\operatorname{rk}_N(R)\). Choose an \(N\)-basis \(I\subseteq R\) of \(R\), extend \(I\cup\{e\}\) to an \(N\)-basis \(B\), and put \(A=B-e\). Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq R\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). For every \(c\in C-e\), one has \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_N(I)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), hence \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent, so \(p_{A\cup c}=\infty\). But \(p_{A\cup e}=p_B<\infty\), contradicting the quotient-incidence condition. Therefore ranks in \(N\) are constant on \(M\)-closures.

Consequently, if \(X\le Y\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(Y)-\operatorname{rk}_M(X).
\]

#### Local Plücker criterion

If a function \(q:\binom E d\to\mathbb T\) has matroidal finite support, then \(q\) is a valuated matroid iff all three-term tropical Plücker relations
\[
\min\bigl(
q_{Sab}+q_{Scd},\
q_{Sac}+q_{Sbd},\
q_{Sad}+q_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
have no unique finite minimizer, for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). For \(d=0\) or \(d=1\), there are no such relations, so matroidal support is enough.

#### Affine annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This follows because \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is tropically generated by the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and the conditions that those cocircuits lie in \(H_u\) are precisely the tropical linear equations defining \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\).

#### Bergman superlevel criterion

If \(G\) is a finite simple matroid and \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\), then
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)
\]
iff, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

Indeed, if a superlevel set \(S\) is not flat, choose \(e\notin S\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_G(S)\). Then some circuit \(C\subseteq S\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and \(e\) is the unique finite minimum of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the next smallest value on \(C\); then \(C-e\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\) but \(e\notin\{w\ge\lambda\}\), so that superlevel set is not flat.

---

### 2. Reduction to finite simple matroids

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\), with ground set \(\bar E\) the set of nonloop parallel classes of \(M\). Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
be the projection.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scalar translation, hence descends projectively.

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Let \(q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Its finite support is obtained from the support of \(q\) by replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class and adding loops, so the support is matroidal. The three-term Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) either reduce to those for \(q\), are all \(\infty\), or contain two identical finite terms coming from two elements in the same parallel class. Thus \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\uparrow)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, the loop equations force the loop coordinates to be \(\infty\), the two-element parallel circuits force equality of coordinates inside each parallel class, and after imposing these equations the remaining tropical equations are exactly those for \(q\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]
Therefore every valuated quotient of \(S\) gives a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(p\) be a rank \(d\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(d=0\), it clearly descends to the rank \(0\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Suppose \(d>0\).

Every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\). For if a loop \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\), then with \(A=B-\ell\), the cocircuit \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) would have finite \(\ell\)-coordinate, contradicting the loop circuit equation of \(M\).

Similarly, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and put \(A=B-c\). Then the circuit equation for \(C\) applied to \(D_A^p\) would have the unique finite term \(p_B\), impossible.

Now let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). Since \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has equal \(e\)- and \(f\)-coordinates. Hence
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f)
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\). It follows that no finite \(p\)-basis contains two parallel elements, and that replacing an element of a finite \(p\)-basis by a parallel element preserves the value of \(p\).

Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and \(p=\bar p^\uparrow\). Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr),
\]
we get \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\). Therefore \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). This proves the claimed poset isomorphism.

The flat lattices of \(M\) and \(S\) are canonically isomorphic:
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), writing \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), one has
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is projectively empty, \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has one element, and the theorem is trivial.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume from now on that \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless, of rank \(r\). Let
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
be an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
The anti-automorphism reverses rank:
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality for \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(M\) is modular.

Because \(M\) is simple, atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) are precisely elements of \(E\), and the polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms.

---

### 4. Flat constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of this simple modular \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom E k\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). With \(A=I-c\), the quotient-incidence condition gives a one-term minimum \(p_I\), so \(p_I\) cannot be finite.

Now suppose \(I,J\in\binom E k\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\). The basis graph of \(M|X\) is connected, so it is enough to consider adjacent bases
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad J=A\cup\{b\}.
\]
Since both span \(X\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying quotient incidence to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Therefore \(p_I=p_J\).

Thus, for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\), one may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\in\binom E n\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,&
J\in\binom E n\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\), a rank \(k\) quotient of \(M\), and write
\[
\nu(X)=\operatorname{rk}_N(X)
\]
for flats \(X\) of \(M\).

Define, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
h(A)=\rho(\operatorname{cl}_M A)+\nu(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)^\perp)-k.
\]
We show that \(h\) is a matroid rank function of rank \(n\).

Let \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Since \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
k-\nu(X^\perp)\le r-\rho(X^\perp)=\rho(X).
\]
Hence
\[
h(A)=\rho(X)+\nu(X^\perp)-k\ge 0.
\]
Also \(\nu(X^\perp)\le k\), so
\[
h(A)\le \rho(X)\le |A|.
\]
Moreover,
\[
h(\varnothing)=0,\qquad h(E)=r-k=n.
\]

If \(X\le Y\) are flats, then
\[
h(Y)-h(X)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X)-\bigl(\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)\bigr).
\]
Because \(Y^\perp\le X^\perp\) and \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\nu(X^\perp)-\nu(Y^\perp)
\le
\rho(X^\perp)-\rho(Y^\perp)
=
\rho(Y)-\rho(X),
\]
so \(h(Y)\ge h(X)\). Thus \(h\) is monotone.

For flats \(X,Y\), modularity of \(M\) gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\vee Y)+\rho(X\wedge Y).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
&h(X)+h(Y)-h(X\vee Y)-h(X\wedge Y)\\
&=
\nu(X^\perp)+\nu(Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)
-\nu(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp)
\ge 0
\end{aligned}
\]
by submodularity of \(\nu\).

For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), put \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\) and \(Y=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=X\vee Y,
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\le X\wedge Y.
\]
Using the flat case and monotonicity,
\[
h(A)+h(B)\ge h(A\cup B)+h(A\cap B).
\]
Thus \(h\) is an integer-valued, monotone, submodular function with \(0\le h(A)\le |A|\). Hence \(h\) is a matroid rank function.

Now let \(J\in\binom E n\), and set \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\). Then \(h(J)=n\) iff \(J\) is \(M\)-independent and \(\nu(X^\perp)=k\). For a rank \(k\) flat \(Y\), \(\bar p(Y)<\infty\) iff \(\nu(Y)=k\). Therefore the bases of the matroid with rank function \(h\) are exactly the finite-support sets of \(p^\dagger\). Thus \(\operatorname{supp}(p^\dagger)\) is matroidal.

If \(n=0\) or \(n=1\), this already implies that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid. Assume \(n\ge 2\). We prove the three-term Plücker relations.

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\) and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},\
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},\
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all six \(n\)-subsets are dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(S\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in each opposite pairing one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

We need the following lemma.

#### Lemma 5.1

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
[12]+[34],\
[13]+[24],\
[14]+[23]
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

##### Proof

If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, two distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\), because their join is \(Y\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Successively intersecting with hyperplanes lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Each \(T_i\) has rank at least \(k-1\). It cannot have rank \(k\), because then intersecting it with \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Hence \(\rho(T_i)=k-1\).

If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
T_m\wedge T_n=Z.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\rho(T_m\vee T_n)=\rho(T_m)+\rho(T_n)-\rho(Z)=k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then, for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), the set
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\).

It remains to treat the case \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification of the interval \([Z,Y]\), a finite simple modular rank-three matroid \(G\). Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(G\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). The circuit \(D\) lifts to a circuit of \(M/Z\), hence to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image in \(M/Z\) is \(D\). Applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we get that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

We now use a rank-three edge lemma.

#### Lemma 5.2

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\). For lines \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) of \(G\), define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

##### Proof

If two lines coincide, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(\langle12\rangle=\infty\), while
\[
\langle13\rangle=\langle23\rangle,
\qquad
\langle14\rangle=\langle24\rangle.
\]
Thus the second and third matching costs are equal.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so that \(\min w=0\).

By the Bergman superlevel criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
\varnothing\subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B\subseteq E(G),
\]
with the point \(\{a\}\) or the line \(B\) possibly absent. Equivalently,
\[
w(x)=\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}+\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), with the obvious interpretation when one of \(a,B\) is absent.

First suppose no \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\neq j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus the pair \(\{i,j\}\) has cost \(0\) unless the two colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree, in which case the cost is the nonnegative value \(w(y_i)\).

For four colored objects, the three perfect matching costs have no unique minimum, even with arbitrary nonnegative or infinite same-color weights. The possible color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In the first case all matchings have cost \(0\). In the \(2+1+1\) and \(2+2\) cases, two matchings avoid same-color pairs. In the \(3+1\) case, each matching contains exactly one same-color pair of the same color. In the \(4\) case, all matching costs are equal.

Now suppose one line is \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\qquad
c(y_j)=w(y_j).
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at most one of them is the distinguished point \(a\), so at least two of the three numbers \(c(y_j)\) are minimal. If exactly two colors agree, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three colors agree, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Applying Lemma 5.2 to the four lines \(H_i/Z\) in \(G\) proves the remaining case of Lemma 5.1. ∎

Lemma 5.1 proves the three-term Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

It remains to show that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). If \(n=0\), this is automatic. Assume \(n>0\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit and \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\). We must prove that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(X)=n-1\) and \(\rho(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of \(M/X\). Its flat lattice is the interval \([X,E]\), so \(G\) is finite, simple, and modular. Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G).
\]
By the Bergman superlevel criterion, it is enough to prove that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

A subset of points in a finite simple modular matroid is a flat iff it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) for \(F\). By induction, all points of \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) lie in \(S\).
