If \(x\) is a point of \(F\) outside this smaller flat, modularity forces the line through \(x\) and \(a_m\) to meet \(\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) in a point already in \(S\). Line-closedness then gives \(x\in S\).

Now take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\). Otherwise, the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) contain
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
a rank \(k-1\) flat. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting this circuit to \(M\), and applying quotient incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), gives that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(G)\).

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\), constant on parallel classes and \(\infty\) on loops.

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise set \(D=C\setminus X\). For every \(c\in D\), since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\), we have
\[
c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c).
\]
Therefore \(c\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). If the minimum of the values \(u(P_c)\) on \(D\) were uniquely finite at some \(c\), that circuit would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Thus the quotient-incidence condition holds.

Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 6. Dagger is involutive and sends flat quotients to flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy result applies to it. We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom E k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is an involution on valuated quotients of \(M\).

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\). For a rank \(r-\rho(F)\) flat \(G\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(G)+\rho(F)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\). The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 7. Affine annihilator duality inside \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\)

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). For an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\) is a hyperplane and the polarity sends hyperplanes to atoms, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

Define \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) by declaring that \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) iff, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove the key equivalence:
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Assume first \(0<k<r\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\), one has
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Indeed, if \(T=\{1,\dots,r\}\setminus S\), then by induction on \(|T|\),
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in T}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus T),
\]
using modularity at each step.

Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Taking \(S=\varnothing\) shows that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
spans rank \(r\), hence is an \(M\)-basis. Taking \(S=\{i\}\) shows that the construction is involutive:
\[
(b_i^*)^*=b_i.
\]

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because the support of \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has \(N\)-rank \(k\), so \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual \(\mu_B^*\). Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
&=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)\\
&=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)\\
&=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\end{aligned}
\]

Suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For a fixed \(M\)-basis \(B\), put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove that \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\).

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and set \(S=[r]\setminus T\). Then \(|S|=k-1\). Put
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the valuated cocircuit \(D_A^p\). If \(D_A^p\equiv\infty\), the corresponding tropical equation for \(\mu_B^*\) is automatic. Otherwise
\[
D_A^p\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), we get
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the tropical equation defining
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Thus \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\) for every \(M\)-basis \(B\).

Because \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now verify the global equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis. The desired equation follows from the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all coefficients \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for such \(j\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)\) is independent of \(j\). Hence the coefficients \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) for \(j\in D\) are all equal to a common value \(\gamma\in\mathbb T\). If \(\gamma=\infty\), all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\gamma<\infty\), the equation reduces to
\[
\min_{j\in D} x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer, which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

It remains to handle the boundary ranks.

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Thus
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\]
for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) is automatic.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
Suppose \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). For each atom \(a\in E\), choose the hyperplane \(H=a^\perp\), choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\), and choose \(e\notin H\). Then \(B=A\cup\{e\}\) is an \(M\)-basis, and the cocircuit \(D_A^M\) has finite coordinate only at elements outside \(H\). Testing \(D_A^M\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) forces
\[
x_{H^\perp}=x_a=\infty.
\]
Thus \(x=\infty^E\). Hence the equivalence also holds for \(k=r\).

Therefore, for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### 8. The involution in the simple modular case

For a projective tropical linear subspace
\[
X\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi(X)=
\left\{
[x]\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):
X\subseteq H_x
\right\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), then the affine annihilator equivalence gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Since dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x \implies X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Hence \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

### 9. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be an arbitrary finite matroid. If \(M\) is all loops, the result is trivial, as noted above.

Otherwise let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The flat-lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given polarity to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto
\bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending this polarity.

Using the poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Since
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\right),
\]
we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

This proves the finite-ground-set theorem. ∎

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\Dr(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M)\), where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroids on \(E\), ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
M_F:=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
as a matroid on \(E\), so every element of \(F\) is made a loop.

Assume that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

We use min-plus conventions. A minimum is said to have no unique finite minimizer if either it is attained at least twice among finite terms or all terms are \(\infty\).

---

### 1. Valuated-matroid preliminaries

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). We extend \(p\) by \(p_I=\infty\) when \(|I|\neq k\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{k+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
The projective space \(\Trop(p)\) is obtained from
\(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\) by quotienting by tropical scalar translation.

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts in their precise needed forms.

1. **Three-term Plücker characterization.** A function
   \(p:\binom E k\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) whose finite support is the set of bases of a matroid is a valuated matroid iff, for every \(S\in\binom E{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
   \[
   \min\bigl(
   p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\
   p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\
   p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
   \bigr)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

2. **Valuated cocircuits and spanning.** For \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
   \[
   C_A^p(e):=p_{A\cup e}.
   \]
   Every non-all-\(\infty\) \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), and \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of these valuated cocircuits.

3. **Ordinary Bergman equations.** For an ordinary matroid \(M\),
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
   =
   \left\{
   x:
   \forall\text{ circuits }C\text{ of }M,\ 
   \min_{c\in C}x_c
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
   \right\}.
   \]

4. **Containment criterion.** If \(p\) has positive rank \(k\), then
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
   \]
   iff, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\),
   \[
   \tag{CI}
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so containment is automatic.

5. **Annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for
   \[
   H_u=\left\{
   v\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^N:
   \min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
   \right\},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   \tag{AD}
   u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
   \Longleftrightarrow
   \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
   \]

6. **Restriction.** If \(q\) is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), and \(U\subseteq E\) contains a \(q\)-basis, then \(q|_U\) is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(U\). Moreover, if \(z\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\), then \(z|_U\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q|_U)\).

We also repeatedly use the following ordinary criterion.

#### Superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(G\) be an ordinary matroid on \(E(G)\), and let
\(w\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{E(G)}\). Then
\[
w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)
\]
iff every superlevel set
\[
S_\lambda=\{e\in E(G): w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\), for every real \(\lambda\).

Indeed, if some \(S_\lambda\) is not flat, choose
\(e\notin S_\lambda\) and a circuit
\(C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\). Then \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer of \(w\) on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C-e\subseteq S_\lambda\), \(e\notin S_\lambda\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not flat.

---

### 2. A support lemma

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
and let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\) be the ordinary matroid whose bases are the finite \(p\)-bases.

If \(k=0\), then \(N\) has rank \(0\), and all statements below are immediate. Assume \(k>0\).

First, if \(I\in\binom E k\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I-c\). In (CI), applied to \(C\) and \(A\), the only possibly finite term is \(p_I\), so \(p_I\) cannot be finite.

Next, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\tag{1}
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]
It suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\subseteq \operatorname{cl}_N(A)\). Let \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\setminus A\), and choose an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). We claim that \(e\) belongs to an \(N\)-circuit contained in \(C\). If not, then \(e\notin\operatorname{cl}_N(C-e)\). Hence there is an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\). Let \(B\) be an \(N\)-basis of \(H\). Then \(p_{B\cup e}<\infty\), while \(p_{B\cup c}=\infty\) for every \(c\in C-e\), contradicting (CI) applied to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\). Thus \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(A)\), proving (1).

Consequently, if \(G\subseteq H\) are flats of \(M\), then
\[
\tag{2}
\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
To see this, choose an \(N\)-basis \(B_G\) of \(G\) and extend it to an \(N\)-basis \(B_H\) of \(H\). Put \(C=B_H\setminus B_G\). Order the elements of \(C\). If some \(c\in C\) lay in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C')\), where \(C'\) denotes the earlier elements of \(C\), then by (1),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G\cup C'\cup\{c\})
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C'\cup\{c\}))
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(G\cup C'))
=
\operatorname{rk}_N(G\cup C'),
\]
contradicting \(N\)-independence. Therefore \(C\) is \(M\)-independent over \(G\), and hence
\[
|C|\le \operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Since \(|C|=\operatorname{rk}_N(H)-\operatorname{rk}_N(G)\), (2) follows.

---

### 3. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\) on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^{\bar E}\to(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(q\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
A direct check of the three-term Plücker relations shows that \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid: if all relevant elements project to distinct classes, one obtains the corresponding relation for \(q\); if two elements project to the same class, the finite terms occur in equal pairs or all terms are \(\infty\).

Moreover,
\[
\tag{3}
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)).
\]
Indeed, loops force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and two-element parallel circuits force equal coordinates on each non-loop parallel class. After quotienting by these equalities, the tropical Plücker equations are exactly the equations for \(q\).

In particular,
\[
\tag{4}
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)).
\]

Conversely, suppose \(p\) is a valuated matroid on \(E\) with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
If \(p\) has positive rank, then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), because every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has loop-coordinate \(\infty\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=x_f\). Applying this to the valuated cocircuits \(C_A^p\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}
\]
for every \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\). Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), and
\[
p=\bar p^\uparrow.
\]
The rank-zero case is also immediate. Using (3) and (4),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]
Thus \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\tag{5}
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Flats of \(M\) are precisely preimages of flats of \(S\):
\[
\tag{6}
F\in\mathcal L(M)
\Longleftrightarrow
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\quad\text{for some }\bar F\in\mathcal L(S).
\]
Therefore
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism. Moreover, if \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\tag{7}
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is simple.

---

### 4. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple of rank \(r\), and that \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Because \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, it is semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
The involution reverses maximal chains, so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality, hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Therefore, in the simple case, \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid equipped with a fixed polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

We now construct the desired involution for such \(M\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid satisfying
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 5.1. Flat constancy

If \(I\in\binom E k\) is dependent in \(M\), then \(p_I=\infty\). If \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets spanning the same rank \(k\) flat, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the value \(\infty\). Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of \(F\). Denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

Define a function
\[
p^\dagger:\binom E n\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
by
\[
\tag{8}
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(I\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

---

#### 5.2. The support of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). For a flat \(G\) of \(M\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp),
\]
and for an arbitrary subset \(A\subseteq E\), define
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)=\rho^\dagger(\operatorname{cl}_M(A)).
\]

We claim that \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

First,
\[
\rho^\dagger(\varnothing)=0-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=0,
\qquad
\rho^\dagger(E)=r-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(\varnothing)=n.
\]

For nonnegativity, apply (2) to \(G^\perp\subseteq E\):
\[
k-\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)
\le
r-\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger(G)\ge 0\). Also,
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)-k+\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G),
\]
because \(\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)\le k\).

If \(G\subseteq H\) are flats, then \(H^\perp\subseteq G^\perp\), and (2) gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\le
\operatorname{rk}_M(G^\perp)-\operatorname{rk}_M(H^\perp)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(H)-\operatorname{rk}_M(G).
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger(H)\ge \rho^\dagger(G)\), so \(\rho^\dagger\) is monotone.

For flats \(G,H\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(G)+\operatorname{rk}_M(H)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(G\wedge H)+\operatorname{rk}_M(G\vee H).
\]
Submodularity of \(\operatorname{rk}_N\), together with (1), gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(G^\perp)+\operatorname{rk}_N(H^\perp)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\vee H)^\perp)+
\operatorname{rk}_N((G\wedge H)^\perp).
\]
Hence
\[
\rho^\dagger(G)+\rho^\dagger(H)
\ge
\rho^\dagger(G\wedge H)+\rho^\dagger(G\vee H).
\]
For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), put \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\) and \(H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H,
\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
Using monotonicity, the same submodularity inequality holds for \(A,B\). Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is an integer-valued, normalized, monotone, submodular function with
\[
0\le \rho^\dagger(A)\le |A|.
\]
Therefore \(\rho^\dagger\) is the rank function of a matroid, call it \(N^\dagger\).

For \(J\in\binom E n\), \(J\) is a basis of \(N^\dagger\) iff \(\rho^\dagger(J)=n\). Since
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)\le \operatorname{rk}_M(J)\le n,
\]
this forces \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent. Then
\[
\rho^\dagger(J)=n
\Longleftrightarrow
\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)=k
\Longleftrightarrow
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
\]
Hence the finite support of \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the set of bases of \(N^\dagger\).

---

#### 5.3. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus assume \(0<k<r\).

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Let \(S\in\binom E{n-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all relevant \(n\)-sets are dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every displayed Plücker summand contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so the relation is automatic.

Assume therefore that \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]

Thus the needed Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

---

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while \([13]=[23]\) and \([14]=[24]\). Therefore the second and third sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), and all three sums are equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z
\]
with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Therefore
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary three-term Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

It remains to handle \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Consider the simplification \(G\) of the interval \([Z,Y]\). This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]

We first show that \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(G\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image modulo \(Z\) is \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(C\) and \(A\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(w\) satisfies the circuit equation for every circuit \(D\) of \(G\), so
\[
w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G).
\]

We use the following rank-three fact.

---

#### Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). If \(L_i=L_j\), define \(w(L_i\wedge L_j)=\infty\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), this is clear. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets form a chain of proper flats. In rank three, such a chain has the form
\[
\varnothing \subseteq \{a\}\subseteq B
\]
for some point \(a\) and some line \(B\). Hence
\[
w(x)=\alpha\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

If two of the \(L_i\) coincide, the repeated-line argument already used above applies. Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\). Thus a matching cost receives a nonzero contribution exactly from pairs with the same color \(y_i\). Among the three perfect matchings of four colored objects, the minimum equal-color cost is never uniquely attained: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case at least two matchings attain the minimum.

If one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,\qquad j=2,3,4.
\]
Let
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two have the same minimal \(c\)-value. If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Thus the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma.

---

Applying the rank-three edge lemma to the lines \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) in the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies all three-term tropical Plücker relations. Since its finite support is the matroid \(N^\dagger\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

#### 5.4. Containment of \(p^\dagger\) in \(\Trop(M)\)

We prove
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For \(k=0\), \(p^\dagger\) is \(M\), and for \(k=r\), \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), so both cases are immediate. Assume \(0<k<r\).

By (CI), it suffices to show that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \(A\in\binom E{n-1}\),
\[
\tag{9}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

Let \(G\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

We claim that
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G).
\]
By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to show that every superlevel set of \(u\) is a flat of \(G\). In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat: if \(x\in\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_m)\), induct on \(m\). For \(m\ge3\), let \(A_0=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\) and \(L=\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\). Modularity gives \(\operatorname{rk}(A_0\cap L)=1\), so \(A_0\cap L\) is a point already obtained by induction, and then \(x\) lies on a line spanned by known points.

Thus it suffices to prove that every superlevel set of \(u\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\) lie in a superlevel set, and let \(P_3\) be another point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) of \(G\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise set
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup\{e_i\})=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Lifting this circuit to \(M\) and applying (CI) to \(B\) gives that
\[
\min(u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3))
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore if \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), then \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence every superlevel set is line-closed, so \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\).

Now return to (9). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise put
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
For \(c\in D\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c)).
\]
The vector \(u\) pulls back from the simplification \(G\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). If the minimum over \(D\) were uniquely finite at some \(c\), then since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c)\), there would be a circuit \(D'\subseteq D\) of \(M/X\) containing \(c\), contradicting the circuit equation for \(u\) on \(D'\). Hence (9) holds, proving
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

---

#### 5.5. Involutivity

We prove
\[
\tag{10}
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\in\binom E k\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).

---

#### 5.6. Compatibility with flats

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\).

For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) becomes a basis after contracting \(F\), equivalently iff
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
By modularity and the rank condition, this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) iff \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

Now let \(H\) be a rank \(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) iff \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), which is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore, up to an irrelevant global additive scalar,
\[
\tag{11}
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

### 6. The affine annihilator theorem

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\tag{12}
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp
=
\left(\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)\right)^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\bigcap_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
The last equality follows from fundamental circuits with respect to the basis \(B\).

Taking \(S=\{1,\dots,r\}\), we see that
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis. Applying (12) to \(S=[r]\setminus\{i\}\) gives \((B^*)^*=B\). Also every element \(e\in E\) occurs as some polar atom \(b_i^*\): choose a basis \(A\) of the hyperplane \(e^\perp\), choose \(b\notin e^\perp\), and take \(B=A\cup\{b\}\).

Now let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}.
\]
This is a valuated matroid because \(B\) contains a basis of the support matroid \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\):
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(B)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(B))=\operatorname{rk}_N(E)=k.
\]

For \(R\subseteq [r]\) with \(|R|=r-k\), equation (12) gives
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\bigr)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
Therefore
\[
\tag{13}
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_i\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We prove the affine annihilator theorem:
\[
\tag{14}
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\Longleftrightarrow
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so (14) is immediate for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}\). If \(x=\infty^E\), both sides of (14) hold. If \(x_e<\infty\) for some \(e\), choose a basis \(B=\{b_i\}\) with \(b_i^*=e\). Let \(A=B-b_i\). The cocircuit \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) has, on \(B\), a unique finite coordinate at \(b_i\). Therefore the defining condition for \(\widetilde H_x\) fails. Hence (14) also holds in this case.

Assume now that \(0<k<r\).

First suppose \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
By restriction and (13),
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By annihilator duality (AD),
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we get
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We show that
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. Since \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\), testing on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is precisely the circuit equation for
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]

Thus \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every polar apartment \(B^*\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check every circuit equation of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\in\binom E{n+1}\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis. The corresponding apartment equation gives the desired relation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<n\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=n\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). For \(j\notin D\), \(J-j\) is dependent, so
\[
p^\dagger_{J-j}=\infty.
\]
For \(j\in D\), the sets \(J-j\) are independent and have the same \(M\)-closure, so the values \(p^\dagger_{J-j}\) are all equal. Thus the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) reduces to the ordinary circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

This proves (14).

---

### 7. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

Let \(X\in\Dr(M)\), and choose a valuated matroid \(p\) with
\[
X=\Trop(p),
\qquad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X):=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]

This is well-defined. Indeed, by the affine annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
=
\left\{
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M):
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\right\},
\]
so \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) depends only on \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), not on the chosen valuation \(p\).

By (10),
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then their affine cones satisfy
\[
\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde Y.
\]
Therefore
\[
\{x:\widetilde Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}
\subseteq
\{x:\widetilde X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\},
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, by (11), for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple \(M\).

---

### 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) be arbitrary finite, and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). By (5), there is a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
The flat-lattice involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through the lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). By the simple case, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending that descended involution.

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by conjugation:
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta_*(X))=\Delta_*(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) corresponds to \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S)\), then \(F^\perp\) corresponds to \(\bar F^\perp\). Using (7),
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the required order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). ∎

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathcal L(M)\) be its lattice of flats, ordered by inclusion, and suppose there is an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with tropical minimum conventions as below. Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) denote the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), equivalently the projectivizations of affine tropical linear spaces \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) where \(p\) ranges over valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), modulo addition of a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates. The order is inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces.

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), let
\[
N_F:=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
be the matroid on \(E\) obtained from the contraction \(M/F\) on \(E\setminus F\) by adding all elements of \(F\) back as loops. Equivalently,
\[
r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
\qquad(A\subseteq E).
\]
The embedded flat object associated to \(F\) is \(\operatorname{Trop}(N_F)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

We prove that there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). Thus the original question has an affirmative answer in the finite-ground-set setting in which relative Dressians and tropical projective spaces are defined here. No weakening is made beyond making the finite-ground-set convention explicit.

## Proof

### 1. Affine conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts

We work first with affine tropical cones. Put
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
Throughout, a tropical condition saying that a minimum is “attained at least twice” means:

> the minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

Thus a minimum whose all terms are \(\infty\) satisfies the condition.

Let \(p\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\), with Plücker coordinates \(p_I\in\mathbb T\) for \(I\in\binom Ed\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
defined by the circuit equations
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\iff
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\quad
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), these equations force
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\mathbb T^E.
\]

Projectivization is obtained by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and quotienting by tropical scalar translation:
\[
x\sim x+\lambda\mathbf 1,\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R.
\]
Thus a rank-zero affine tropical linear space becomes empty projectively.

We identify an ordinary matroid \(M\) with its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_I=
\begin{cases}
0,& I\text{ is a basis of }M,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is the affine Bergman fan of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

We use the following standard facts from valuated matroid theory.

#### Standard Fact 1: containment equals valuated quotient

For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\).

In particular, if \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid with constant valuation and \(p\) has rank \(d>0\), then \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((d-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer, where
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

For \(d=0\), \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so it is contained in every affine tropical linear space.

#### Standard Fact 2: coordinate cocircuits lie in the tropical linear space

If \(p\) has rank \(d>0\) and \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), then the vector
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A
\end{cases}
\]
lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

#### Standard Fact 3: ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u^N=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u^N.
\]
This includes the boundary ranks. If \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)=\{\infty^N\}\) and \(\mu^*\) has full rank, so both sides hold for every \(u\). If \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=|N|\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), and the right side holds exactly when \(u=\infty^N\), matching the rank-zero space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)=\{\infty^N\}\).

#### Standard Fact 4: uniqueness of valuated Plücker coordinates

The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) determines the valuated matroid \(p\) up to addition of a global tropical scalar to all Plücker coordinates. Hence constructions invariant under global scalar addition are well-defined on tropical linear spaces rather than only on chosen Plücker representatives.

We shall also use the following elementary characterization of ordinary Bergman fans.

#### Lemma 1.1: superlevel-flat criterion

Let \(N\) be an ordinary matroid on a finite set \(E_N\). For \(w\in\mathbb T^{E_N}\),
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)
\]
if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e\in E_N:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** The ordinary Bergman condition says that no circuit has a unique finite minimum.

Suppose first that \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let
\[
S_\lambda=\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}.
\]
If \(S_\lambda\) were not a flat, there would be \(e\notin S_\lambda\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda)\). Then some circuit \(C\) satisfies
\[
e\in C\subseteq S_\lambda\cup\{e\}.
\]
Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for all \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\), contradiction.

Conversely, suppose every real superlevel set is a flat. If a circuit \(C\) had a unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose a real number \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and all other finite values on \(C\), and larger than \(w_e\) if all other values are \(\infty\). Then
\[
C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq S_\lambda,
\qquad
e\notin S_\lambda.
\]
But \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_N(C\setminus\{e\})\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_N(S_\lambda)\), so \(S_\lambda\) is not a flat. This contradiction proves the criterion. \(\square\)

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(M\) be any finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be its loop set. Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\longrightarrow \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This map commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical spaces.

#### 2.1. Zero-parallel extension

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) to \(E\) is defined by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing
\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\qquad I\in\binom Ed.
\]
This is a valuated matroid: its tropical Plücker relations either project to those of \(\bar q\), or contain only \(\infty\)-terms, or, when two representatives of one parallel class are interchanged, contain two equal finite terms. Thus adding loops and replacing an element by a zero-offset parallel class preserves valuated matroids.

Moreover,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, the loop circuits force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\), the two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) when \(e,f\) lie in the same non-loop parallel class of \(M\), and after imposing these equations the remaining circuit equations are precisely those for \(\bar q\).

Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

#### 2.2. Every quotient of \(M\) descends to \(S\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(d\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
If \(d=0\), then \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

Assume \(d>0\). For every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\), hence in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

First, every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis containing an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then on the circuit \(C\),
\[
D_A^q(c)=q_I<\infty,
\]
while
\[
D_A^q(e)=\infty
\qquad(e\in C\setminus\{c\}),
\]
because \(C\setminus\{c\}\subseteq A\). Thus \(D_A^q\) has a unique finite minimum on the \(M\)-circuit \(C\), contradicting \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Second, loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\). If \(\ell\in L_0\), every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate equal to \(\infty\). If some finite \(q\)-basis \(I\) contained \(\ell\), then with \(A=I\setminus\{\ell\}\) we would have
\[
D_A^q(\ell)=q_I<\infty,
\]
again contradicting \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Third, original parallel classes are not split and have zero valuated offset. Let \(e,f\) be parallel non-loops in \(M\). Then every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies
\[
x_e=x_f,
\]
because \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. Hence for every \(A\in\binom E{d-1}\),
\[
D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f).
\]
It follows that no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\). Indeed, if \(I\) contained both, take \(A=I\setminus\{f\}\). Then
\[
D_A^q(e)=\infty,
\qquad
D_A^q(f)=q_I<\infty,
\]
a contradiction. Also, if \(I=A\cup\{e\}\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
q_{A\cup e}=D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f)=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus replacement within an original parallel class preserves both finiteness and valuation.

Now define a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) by choosing representatives. For a \(d\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq\bar E\), choose any \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The preceding paragraph shows that this is independent of the representative set \(I\). The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved parallel classes. Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Since
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, if \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\), then its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
so \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Therefore \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

#### 2.3. Compatibility with flat quotients

Flats of \(M\) are exactly the loop set \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Therefore
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on \(E\), and
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Using
\[
r_M(A)=r_S\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr),
\]
we get
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S\bigl(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr)-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), and
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N_F)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar N_{\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Hence the isomorphism \(\Delta_*\) identifies the flat object indexed by \(\bar F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) with the flat object indexed by \(F\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The flat-lattice involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) descends through \(\psi\) to an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ(\,\cdot\,)^\perp\circ\psi^{-1}.
\]

If \(M\) has rank \(0\), then all elements are loops, \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) is empty projectively, and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has only the empty object. The desired involution is then the identity. Hence, from now on in the construction, we may reduce to the case where \(M\) is simple and loopless.

---

### 3. Polarity forces modularity

Let \(L\) be a finite geometric lattice of rank \(r\), with rank function \(\rho\). Suppose \(L\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
X\longmapsto X^\perp.
\]
Because \(L\) is graded, order reversal sends an interval of length \(\rho(X)\) from the bottom to \(X\) to an interval of length \(\rho(X)\) from \(X^\perp\) to the top. Therefore
\[
\rho(X^\perp)=r-\rho(X).
\]

Geometric lattices are semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\ge
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Apply semimodularity to \(X^\perp\) and \(Y^\perp\). Since
\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,
\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]
we get
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho((X\vee Y)^\perp)+\rho((X\wedge Y)^\perp).
\]
Substituting \(\rho(Z^\perp)=r-\rho(Z)\) gives
\[
2r-\rho(X)-\rho(Y)
\ge
2r-\rho(X\vee Y)-\rho(X\wedge Y),
\]
or
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(L\) is modular.

Applying this to the flat lattice of the simplification \(S\), we conclude that after the simplification reduction we are in the following situation:

- \(M\) is finite, simple, and loopless;
- \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular;
- \(M\) has rank \(r\);
- there is an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) of \(\mathcal L(M)\).

We now construct the desired involution in this simple modular case.

---

### 4. The simple modular dagger construction

Assume throughout this section that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), and equipped with a flat-lattice polarity
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

#### 4.1. Flat constancy for quotients

Assume first \(k>0\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), Standard Fact 1 gives the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then all elements of \(C\setminus\{c\}\) lie in \(A\), so
\[
D_A^p(e)=\infty
\qquad(e\in C\setminus\{c\}),
\]
while
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I.
\]
The circuit-incidence relation forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus
\[
I\text{ \(M\)-dependent}
\implies
p_I=\infty.
\]

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Since \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) and \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(A\) and \(C\), all terms indexed by \(C\cap A\) are \(\infty\), so
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb T\).

The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). We write this common value as
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

We shall also need the following consequence. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). It is \(M\)-independent by the preceding paragraph, so extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any \(M\)-basis. Connect \(B_0\) to \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose at one step we move from \(B'\) to
\[
B''=B'-\{b\}\cup\{e\}.
\]
If the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), then \(I\subseteq B''\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{b\}\). Let \(C\subseteq B'\cup\{e\}\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit containing \(b\) and \(e\). Since
\[
p_{A\cup b}=p_I<\infty,
\]
circuit-incidence forces another finite term
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty
\]
for some \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})\). Such \(c\) lies in \(B''\), so \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Moving along the path proves the claim.

#### 4.2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
& J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(0<k<r\), this is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), put \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p\) has only the coordinate \(p_\varnothing\), and \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), shifted by \(p_\varnothing\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) is the rank-zero valuated matroid.

So for the main construction we assume
\[
0<k<r.
\]

#### 4.3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We verify the three-term tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set involving that element, so all three matching sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\), \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent exactly when \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), equivalently \(H_e=H_f\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
Therefore the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

#### Lemma 4.1: local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the other two matching sums are equal, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\):
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k
\qquad(i\neq j).
\]
Set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
The possible ranks of \(Z\) are \(k\), \(k-1\), and, when \(k\ge2\), \(k-2\).

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is equal to \(Z\). Hence all three matching sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)=k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), we get
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
t_i\in T_i\setminus Z.
\]
Then \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(T_m\vee T_n\). The ordinary Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr),
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

It remains to handle \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Consider the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). Its simplification \(N\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each point \(P\in D\), choose an element \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). Circuit lifting from the contraction by \(Z\) gives an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Apply the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

The hyperplanes \(H_i\) descend to lines \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\). Thus the desired relation follows from the rank-three edge lemma below. \(\square\)

#### Lemma 4.2: rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let
\[
w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]
Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(N\), and define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two lines coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies. So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w=\infty^E\), the statement is immediate. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By Lemma 1.1, the real superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats. In a simple rank-three matroid, proper nonempty flats are points and lines. Since superlevel sets are nested, the nonzero part of \(w\) is supported on a point-line flag: there are a line \(B\), a point \(a\le B\), and numbers \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}},
\]
with the conventions that \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) allows one level of the flag to be absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\iff
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\iff
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus the edge \(\{i,j\}\) contributes nonzero cost only when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\).

For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique. The color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\). In these cases, respectively, all matchings tie, or two matchings avoid the repeated color, or two cross-matchings tie, or all matchings have the same one repeated-color edge, or all matchings are identical in cost. This remains true with cost \(\infty\).

Now suppose one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) are equal to the minimum value \(\beta\). If exactly two of them are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three matching costs are equal. Hence no unique finite minimum occurs. \(\square\)

Lemmas 4.1 and 4.2 prove the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated matroid.

#### 4.4. Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now show that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By Standard Fact 1, it is enough to prove the circuit-incidence relations:
for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1,
\qquad
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1.
\]

We need the following lemma.

#### Lemma 4.3: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat, and let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.1, it suffices to show that every real superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

First we prove that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line spanned by \(P_1,P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp
\supseteq
(P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\). For \(i=1,2,3\), choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\). In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Circuit lifting gives an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(B\) and \(D\). Terms coming from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces
\[
u(P_3)\ge\lambda.
\]
So \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

It remains to show that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Let \(\mathcal S\) be line-closed. We prove that every point in \(\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal S)\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Choose an independent set
\[
b_1,\dots,b_m\in\mathcal S
\]
spanning a flat \(F\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(\mathcal S\).

For \(m\le2\), this is exactly line-closedness. Suppose \(m\ge3\), and let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(\mathcal S\). Let \(p\) be a point of
\[
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), then \(p\in\mathcal S\). Otherwise consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in G\), hence \(q\in\mathcal S\). Since \(q,b_m\in\mathcal S\) and \(\mathcal S\) is line-closed, every point of the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(\mathcal S\).

Thus line-closed point sets are flats. Therefore every \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat, so \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). \(\square\)

We return to the quotient-incidence relation for \(p^\dagger\). For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\), the flat \(P=X\vee c\) is a point of \(M/X\), and
\[
D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
=
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p(P^\perp)
=
u(P).
\]
By Lemma 4.3, \(u\) lies in the Bergman fan of the simplification of \(M/X\). Lifting \(u\) to \(M/X\) gives a vector with value \(\infty\) on loops and equal values on parallel elements, hence an element of \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

If \(C\subseteq X\), then all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in the contraction \(M/X\). If the minimum of \(D_A^{p^\dagger}\) on \(C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would also have a unique finite minimum, contradicting the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the quotient circuit-incidence relations. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

#### 4.5. Involutivity

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\) as well.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty,
\qquad
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

#### 4.6. Compatibility with ordinary flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Let \(f=\operatorname{rk}(F)\). The rank of \(p^F\) is \(r-f\). Its finite bases are precisely the \(M\)-bases of rank-\((r-f)\) flats \(G\) complementary to \(F\), meaning
\[
G\wedge F=0,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Indeed, if \(G\) is spanned by a basis of \(M/F\), then \(G\vee F=E\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)-\operatorname{rk}(G\vee F)
=
(r-f)+f-r=0.
\]
The converse is immediate from the rank formula.

Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(f\) flat. The dagger \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on \(M\)-bases of \(H\) exactly when \(H^\perp\) is complementary to \(F\). By polarity,
\[
H^\perp\wedge F=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
H^\perp\vee F=E
\]
are equivalent to
\[
H\vee F^\perp=E
\quad\text{and}\quad
H\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
That is, \(H\) is complementary to \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
with the same normalization of finite coordinates.

---

### 5. The point-annihilator theorem

Continue to assume \(M\) is simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat polarity.

For an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
define
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a rank-one flat, identified with its unique element.

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M):
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\quad
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We prove:

#### Theorem 5.1: affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Then for every
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
one has
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

**Proof.**

We first record the Boolean-apartment calculation. Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis, and put
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\}).
\]
For \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
One inclusion is immediate. For the other, if an element \(e\) lies in every \(H_i\) with \(i\notin S\), then its fundamental circuit over \(B\), if \(e\notin B\), uses only elements \(b_i\) with \(i\in S\). Hence \(e\in\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\). The case \(e\in B\) is immediate.

Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases.

Assume first \(0<k<r\). Let
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). The preceding Boolean-apartment identity gives, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Now suppose
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). For every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), we have
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). We prove
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size
\[
r-k+1,
\]
and put
\[
S=[r]\setminus T,
\qquad |S|=k-1.
\]
Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The global coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(D_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is exactly the circuit equation for
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*),
\]
because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus\{i\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus\{i\}))
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus \(x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\) for every \(B\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

It remains to check all global circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). The restriction of \(x\) to \(C\) lies in the tropical linear space of \(p^\dagger|_C\), so
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus\{j\}}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\notin C\), \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so the corresponding term is \(\infty\).
