The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\), with the only new cases being loop terms or repeated parallel classes; those give either all-\(\infty\) relations or two equal finite terms. Hence \(q\) is a valuated matroid, and
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(q\) has rank \(0\), it descends trivially. Suppose its rank is \(k>0\). Since every coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\), the equations defining \(\widetilde L(M)\) force:

- loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\);
- if \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}
  \]
  for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\), with the usual \(\infty\) convention;
- no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class.

Also, finite \(q\)-bases are \(M\)-independent: otherwise an \(M\)-circuit inside such a basis would give a coordinate cocircuit with a unique finite minimum on that circuit.

Thus \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The preceding equalities make this well-defined, and the Plücker relations are inherited from \(q\). Moreover \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Since \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S))\) and \(\Delta\) is injective on simplified coordinates,
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde L(S).
\]
Therefore \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Hence \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

### Compatibility with flats

Flats of \(M\) are precisely \(L_0\) together with unions of nonloop parallel classes. Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

If \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Indeed, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 3. Polarity forces modularity in the simple case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite and simple of rank \(r\). Then \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
If \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

So from now on, in the core construction, \(M\) is finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\), with polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

## 4. Flat constancy of quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). In the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(p_I\), so it must be \(\infty\).

Second, if two independent \(k\)-sets \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) have the same \(M\)-closure, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(C,A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
By connectivity of the basis graph of \(M|F\), it follows that \(p\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of any rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Write this common value as
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

We will also use that every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Start with one finite \(p\)-basis and extend it to an \(M\)-basis. Then move to any other \(M\)-basis along the basis graph. Across an exchange \(B'=B-b+e\), if the current finite \(p\)-basis uses \(b\), apply the circuit-incidence relation to the fundamental circuit of \(e\) over \(B\) to replace \(b\) by another element of \(B'\) while keeping finite \(p\)-value.

---

## 5. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\in\binom En\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,&J\in\binom En\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(k=0\), this is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). For \(k=r\), it is a rank-zero valuated matroid. Assume below \(0<k<r\).

### Core lemma: \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

The proof has two local ingredients.

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Sketch of proof. If two hyperplanes coincide, one bracket is \(\infty\) and the other two matching sums are equal. Otherwise set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
By modularity, \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), all pairwise meets equal \(Z\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), choosing elements in the triple intersections reduces the displayed relation to an ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\), the interval \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three simple modular matroid; the induced weight \(w(P)=\bar p(P)\) on its points lies in its Bergman fan by circuit-incidence for \(p\), and a rank-three matching lemma gives the displayed relation. The rank-three lemma follows from the superlevel-flat criterion: in rank three, a tropical point is supported by a point-line flag, and the three perfect-matchings of four colored objects cannot have a unique minimum.

#### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1\), and let \(N=\operatorname{si}(M/X)\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(N).
\]

Sketch of proof. By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that every set
\[
\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is simple modular, it is enough to prove line-closure. If \(P_1,P_2\) lie in the superlevel set and \(P_3\) is a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\), then \(H_i=P_i^\perp\) are hyperplanes of \(Y=X^\perp\) with
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choosing a basis of \(H_1\wedge H_2\) and representatives in the three \(H_i\), one lifts the rank-two circuit in the contraction to an \(M\)-circuit. Circuit-incidence for \(p\) gives that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence if \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), then \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus the superlevel sets are line-closed, hence flats.

Now verify the two conditions for \(p^\dagger\).

### Plücker relations

Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), again every matching has an \(\infty\)-term.

Otherwise put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\), and for \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) set
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Then \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and for distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the local dual Plücker lemma.

### Quotient circuit-incidence

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). If \(A\) is dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By the codimension-one polarity lemma, the function
\[
X\vee c\longmapsto \bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in \(\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\), then in \(M/X\) the element \(c_0\) would lie in a circuit contained in \(C\setminus X\), and that circuit would have a unique finite minimum, contradicting the preceding paragraph. Hence the quotient circuit-incidence relations hold.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Involutivity and compatibility with flats

### Involutivity

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

If \(I\) is independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### Flat quotients

For a flat \(F\), let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
If \(G\) is a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) flat, then \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) iff \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when \(F\vee G=E\), and modularity converts this, with the rank condition, into \(F\wedge G=\hat 0\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 7. Order reversal via the point-annihilator theorem

We now prove that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). Modularity gives, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

For \(x\in \widetilde L(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\) by
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x
\]
iff for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Point-annihilator theorem

For every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in \widetilde L(M)\),
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Proof sketch. For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). The Boolean-apartment calculation above gives
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

If \(x\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger)\) and \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\), then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde L(\mu_B).
\]
Ordinary tropical annihilator duality gives
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Conversely, assume \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\). Fix \(B\). To show
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\]
use ordinary annihilator duality. The coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu_B\) are restrictions of global coordinate cocircuits \(D_A^p\). Since \(D_A^p\in \widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on \(B\) gives exactly the cocircuit equations for \(x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*)\).

Thus \(x\) restricts correctly to every Boolean apartment. To verify the remaining equations for \(p^\dagger\), let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation. If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, or \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit and the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\).

Boundary ranks \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are handled affinely: for \(k=0\), \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\); for \(k=r\), \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\) and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), and the only \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) with \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\) is \(x=\infty^E\).

### Inclusion reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q).
\]
If \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\), then by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Thus, in the finite simple modular case,
\[
\Phi_M(L(p))=L(p^\dagger)
\]
defines an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces because the point-annihilator description is intrinsic:
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in \widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
Involutivity follows from \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), and flat compatibility follows from
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

Projectivizing gives the desired order-reversing involution on the projective relative Dressian in the simple modular case.

---

## 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be any finite matroid with a flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Since \(S\) is simple, the preceding modularity argument applies, and the simple modular construction gives an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Using the simplification isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
This is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
and the polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Hence
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Therefore \(\Phi_M\) extends the prescribed order-reversing involution on the embedded copy of \(\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\subseteq \operatorname{Dr}(M)\). ∎

---

## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a **finite** matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), including the empty projective rank-zero space, ordered by inclusion. Then \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\) such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(U_{0,F}\) denotes the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with all elements of \(F\) made loops.

---

## 1. Tropical preliminaries

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with min-plus conventions. If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), write \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) for its affine tropical linear space:
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p)
\]
if and only if, for every \((k+1)\)-subset \(J\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), this convention gives
\[
\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
The associated projective space is
\[
L(p)=\bigl(\widetilde L(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1,
\]
so rank zero gives the empty projective space.

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), we also write \(N\) for its constant valuated matroid:
\[
N_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }N,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), and for \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate valuated cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

We use three standard finite-valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Cocircuit span.** Each \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), and \(\widetilde L(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the \(D_A^p\).

2. **Quotient/containment criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(M\), a rank-\(k>0\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfies
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)
\]
if and only if, for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\), \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\) automatically.

3. **Bergman superlevel criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\),
\[
w\in \widetilde L(N)
\]
if and only if every superlevel set
\[
\{e\in E(N): w_e\ge \lambda\},\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R,
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We also need the following annihilator form of tropical duality.

### Lemma 1.1: ordinary tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\), and define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+v_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]

Proof. For \(A\in\binom{R}{k-1}\), define
\[
D_A^\mu(i)=
\begin{cases}
\mu_{A\cup i},&i\notin A,\\
\infty,&i\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
The circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) say that, for every
\[
J\in\binom{R}{|R|-k+1},
\]
the minimum
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(u_j+\mu^*_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Put \(A=R\setminus J\). Since
\[
\mu^*_{J\setminus j}=\mu_{A\cup j},
\]
this condition is exactly
\[
\min_{i\in R}\bigl(u_i+D_A^\mu(i)\bigr)
\]
having no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(u\in\widetilde L(\mu^*)\) if and only if \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^\mu\).

Because \(\widetilde L(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the \(D_A^\mu\), and because \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex, this is equivalent to
\[
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]
∎

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of nonloop parallel classes, let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\longrightarrow \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

### Lemma 2.1

The map \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Proof. Let \(\bar q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q\) to \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(q\) is again a valuated matroid, and its tropical linear space is exactly
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Indeed, the new loop coordinates are forced to be \(\infty\), and elements in the same original parallel class are forced to have equal coordinates; after imposing those equalities, the remaining equations are precisely those of \(\bar q\).

Applying this to the constant matroids gives
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)).
\]
Hence if \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(S\), then \(q\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), it plainly descends to the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

If \(e\in L_0\), then \(\{e\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. The quotient-incidence condition for \(\{e\}\) forces
\[
q_{A\cup e}=\infty
\]
for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\). Thus every loop of \(M\) remains a loop for \(q\).

If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit. For every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) with \(e,f\notin A\), the quotient-incidence condition gives
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
If \(e\in A\) and \(f\notin A\), it gives
\[
q_{A\cup f}=\infty,
\]
and similarly with \(e\) and \(f\) exchanged. Therefore no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class, and the valuation is constant on choices of representatives inside a parallel class.

Finally, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). In the quotient-incidence condition for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(q_I\), so \(q_I=\infty\). Hence finite \(q\)-bases are \(M\)-independent.

Thus \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those of \(q\), and \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\). Therefore
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Since \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S))\) and \(\Delta\) is injective on \(\mathbb T^{\bar E}\), we get
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde L(S).
\]
So \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Thus \(\Delta_*\) is a bijection on relative Dressians, and it preserves inclusions. ∎

The flats of \(M\) are precisely the inverse images of flats of \(S\), together with the loop set. More explicitly,
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\longrightarrow \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Indeed, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F),
\]
and this rank depends only on
\[
\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite and simple.

---

## 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, and has rank \(r\). Its flat lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution, it sends maximal chains to maximal chains. Therefore
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using order-reversal and rank-reversal, this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Hence \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Thus \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

From now until the final reduction, assume \(M\) is finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\), equipped with the polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

## 4. Flat constancy of valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]
Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), choose \(c\in C\), and put \(A=I\setminus c\). In the quotient-incidence condition for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(p_I\), so \(p_I=\infty\).

Second, if \(I\) and \(I'\) are two \(M\)-bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]
It suffices to prove this for adjacent bases of \(M|F\). Thus suppose
\[
I=A\cup a,\qquad I'=A\cup b,
\]
where \(|A|=k-1\). Since \(A\cup a\) and \(A\cup b\) are both bases of \(F\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying quotient-incidence to \(C,A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected, so the claim follows.

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) we may define
\[
\bar p(F)
\]
to be the common value of \(p_I\) over all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\), possibly \(\infty\).

We also need the following fact: every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I_0\), and extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any other \(M\)-basis. The basis graph of \(M\) is connected, so move from \(B_0\) to \(B\) by single exchanges. Suppose \(B'=B-b+e\) is adjacent to \(B\), and \(I\subseteq B\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B'\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\). Let \(C\) be the fundamental circuit of \(e\) over \(B\); since \(B-b+e\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). In the quotient-incidence condition for \(C,A\), the term at \(b\) is finite, so there must be some
\[
c\in C\setminus A,\qquad c\neq b,
\]
with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
But \(c\in B'\), hence \(A\cup c\subseteq B'\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Induction along the basis path proves the claim.

---

## 5. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(n\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,&J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero.

Assume for the moment that \(0<k<r\). The function \(p^\dagger\) has at least one finite basis: choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Since
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-k=n,
\]
we have
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)=\bar p(F)=p_I<\infty.
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

### Lemma 5.1: line-closed point sets in modular matroids

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. If a set \(A\) of points of \(N\) is closed under lines, meaning that whenever \(x,y\in A\), every point on the line \(x\vee y\) also lies in \(A\), then \(A\) is the set of points of a flat.

Proof. Let \(F=\bigvee A\). We show that every point below \(F\) lies in \(A\). It is enough to prove this for a flat generated by finitely many independent points of \(A\), by induction on rank.

The claim is clear in ranks \(0,1,2\), with rank \(2\) being exactly line-closure. Suppose it is known up to rank \(d-1\), and let
\[
F=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_d
\]
with \(a_i\in A\) independent. Let
\[
G=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{d-1}.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(A\). Let \(x\le F\) be a point. If \(x\le G\), we are done. If \(x=a_d\), we are also done. Otherwise, the line \(x\vee a_d\) is not contained in \(G\). By modularity inside the interval \([\hat 0,F]\),
\[
\rho((x\vee a_d)\wedge G)=\rho(x\vee a_d)+\rho(G)-\rho(F)=2+(d-1)-d=1.
\]
Thus
\[
y=(x\vee a_d)\wedge G
\]
is a point of \(G\), hence \(y\in A\). Since \(x\) lies on the line \(a_d\vee y\), line-closure gives \(x\in A\). ∎

### Lemma 5.2: rank-three matching lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\), let \(w\in\widetilde L(N)\), and let \(\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3,\ell_4\) be lines of \(N\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(\ell_i\wedge \ell_j),&\ell_i\neq \ell_j,\\
\infty,&\ell_i=\ell_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof. If two of the \(\ell_i\) coincide, one bracket is \(\infty\), and the other two matching sums are equal, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(\ell_i\) are distinct.

By the Bergman superlevel criterion, the superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats. In rank \(3\), a nested family of proper flats is contained in a flag
\[
P\le L,
\]
where \(P\) is a point and \(L\) is a line. Thus, after subtracting the minimum value of \(w\), we may write
\[
w=m+\alpha\mathbf 1_L+\beta\mathbf 1_P
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb T\) with \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), allowing \(\infty\).

The constant \(m\) contributes equally to all three matching sums, so ignore it.

First suppose none of the \(\ell_i\) equals \(L\). Let
\[
q_i=\ell_i\wedge L.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the point \(\ell_i\wedge\ell_j\) lies on \(L\) exactly when \(q_i=q_j\). If \(q_i=q_j\), its contribution is
\[
\gamma(q_i)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&q_i=P,\\
\alpha,&q_i\neq P.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_i\neq q_j\), the contribution is \(0\). Thus the three matching sums are determined by the equality pattern of the four labels \(q_1,q_2,q_3,q_4\). The possible partitions of four labels are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
The corresponding matching-sum patterns are respectively
\[
(0,0,0),\quad (\gamma,0,0),\quad (\gamma+\delta,0,0),\quad (\gamma,\gamma,\gamma),\quad (2\gamma,2\gamma,2\gamma),
\]
up to permutation. None has a unique minimum.

Now suppose one line, say \(\ell_1\), equals \(L\). Put \(q_i=\ell_i\wedge L\) for \(i=2,3,4\). Then
\[
[1i]=\gamma(q_i),
\]
and for \(i,j\in\{2,3,4\}\),
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\gamma(q_i),&q_i=q_j,\\
0,&q_i\neq q_j.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_2,q_3,q_4\) are all distinct, the three sums are
\[
\gamma(q_2),\gamma(q_3),\gamma(q_4).
\]
At most one of these equals \(\alpha+\beta\), and the others equal \(\alpha\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two labels agree, the sums are, up to permutation,
\[
(\gamma,\gamma,\gamma+\delta),
\]
whose minimum is attained at least twice. If all three agree, all three sums are equal. ∎

### Lemma 5.3: local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof. If two \(H_i\)'s coincide, the claim is immediate, as in Lemma 5.2. Assume they are distinct, and put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular, intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most \(1\), so
\[
\rho(Z)\ge k-2.
\]
Also \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and for distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\),
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k.
\]
Hence
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) and contains \(Z\), so
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z
\]
for all \(i\neq j\). All three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), choose an atom \(e_i\) of \([Z,Y]\) such that
\[
e_i\le \bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j,
\qquad
e_i\not\le H_i.
\]
This is possible because
\[
\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j
\]
strictly contains \(Z\), but its meet with \(H_i\) is \(Z\). The elements \(e_1,e_2,e_3,e_4\) are independent over \(Z\): indeed, \(e_i\) is not contained in the join of the other three, since that join lies inside \(H_i\).

Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For \(\{i,j,\ell,m\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z\vee e_\ell\vee e_m.
\]
Therefore
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup e_\ell\cup e_m}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on
\[
A,e_1,e_2,e_3,e_4.
\]

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(Y/Z),
\]
a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and define a weight on points \(P\) of \(N\) by
\[
w(P)=\bar p(Z\vee P).
\]
This is the restriction of the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) to the contraction \(Y/Z\), hence lies in \(\widetilde L(N)\). The hyperplanes \(H_i\) descend to lines \(\ell_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and
\[
[ij]=w(\ell_i\wedge\ell_j).
\]
The claim follows from Lemma 5.2. ∎

### Lemma 5.4: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1\), and let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(N).
\]

Proof. By the Bergman superlevel criterion, it suffices to show that every superlevel set
\[
\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is finite, simple, and modular, Lemma 5.1 says it is enough to show that these superlevel sets are closed under lines.

Let \(P_1,P_2\) be points of \(N\) with
\[
u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda,
\]
and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise put
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and the condition \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\) gives
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
W=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\rho(W)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(W\), and choose
\[
h_i\in H_i\setminus W.
\]
In the contraction by \(W\), the three elements \(h_1,h_2,h_3\) form a rank-two circuit. Lifting to \(M\), there is an \(M\)-circuit contained in
\[
A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}
\]
and containing \(h_1,h_2,h_3\). Applying quotient-incidence for \(p\) with this circuit and \(A\), we get that
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup h_1},p_{A\cup h_2},p_{A\cup h_3}\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
p_{A\cup h_i}=\bar p(H_i)=\bar p(P_i^\perp)=u(P_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), we must have \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus the superlevel set is line-closed, hence a flat. ∎

### Proposition 5.5

For \(0<k<r\), \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

Proof. We already showed that \(p^\dagger\) has at least one finite basis. We verify the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient-incidence relations.

#### Plücker relations

Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(n\)-set appearing in the Plücker relation is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent. If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum has an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.

Thus assume none of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
S\cup e\cup f
\]
is independent exactly when \(H_e\neq H_f\), and in that case
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is dependent and the corresponding value is \(\infty\). Hence the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is precisely Lemma 5.3.

#### Quotient-incidence

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, and all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By Lemma 5.4, the function
\[
X\vee c\longmapsto \bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in \(\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\).

If the minimum over \(c\in C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\), then \(c_0\notin X\). In the contraction \(M/X\), the image of \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of \(C\setminus\{c_0\}\). Therefore there is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) containing the point \(X\vee c_0\) and contained in the set of points \(X\vee c\) for \(c\in C\setminus X\). On that circuit, the above function would have a unique finite minimum at \(X\vee c_0\), contradicting that it lies in \(\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\).

Thus the quotient-incidence relations hold. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). ∎

---

## 6. Involutivity and compatibility with flat quotients

### Involutivity

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both sides are \(\infty\). Suppose \(I\) is independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### Flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is
\[
r-\rho(F).
\]

Let \(G\) be a rank-\(r-\rho(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), equivalently
\[
F\vee G=E.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(F)+\rho(G)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\hat 0.
\]
Thus \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) exactly when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\), because
\[
(G\vee F)^\perp=G^\perp\wedge F^\perp,
\qquad
(G\wedge F)^\perp=G^\perp\vee F^\perp.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 7. Order reversal in the simple modular case

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a single element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed, the left-hand side is contained in the right-hand side, and both have rank \(|S|\). Consequently,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on the set of \(M\)-bases.

For \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde L(M):
\text{ for every }M\text{-basis }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\ \min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

### Proposition 7.1: point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Proof. First assume \(0<k<r\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). The dual-basis identity gives, for \(|T|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in T\}}
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin T\}}
=
\mu_B^*(T).
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose first that \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\), and let \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\). Then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B).
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\). For each \(A\subseteq B\) of size \(k-1\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), we find that
\[
x|_{B^*}
\]
is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*).
\]
Thus \(x\) satisfies all \(p^\dagger\)-equations supported inside every Boolean apartment \(B^*\).

It remains to check an arbitrary circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\subseteq E,\qquad |J|=r-k+1.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since every \(M\)-basis is \(B^*\) for another \(M\)-basis \(B\), the equation for \(J\) is one of the equations already checked in the apartment \(C\).

Now suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent. If every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, then every term in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation is \(\infty\), and there is nothing to prove. Otherwise \(J\) has rank \(|J|-1\), hence contains a unique circuit \(C_0\). The only finite terms occur for \(j\in C_0\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Therefore the \(p^\dagger\)-equation for \(J\) reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) on the circuit \(C_0\). Hence
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary ranks are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Since \(\infty^E\in\widetilde H_x\) for every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), the equivalence becomes
\[
x\in\widetilde L(M)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\{\infty^E\}\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
which is true.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
We must show that
\[
\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x
\]
holds only for \(x=\infty^E\). Suppose \(x\neq\infty^E\), and choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Let
\[
H=e^\perp,
\]
a hyperplane of \(M\). Choose an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_1)=H.
\]
Then \(b_1^*=e\). Choose \(A\gg 0\), and define
\[
y_f=
\begin{cases}
A,&f\in H,\\
0,&f\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
The superlevel sets of \(y\) are flats, so \(y\in\widetilde L(M)\). For \(A\) sufficiently large,
\[
y_{b_1}+x_{b_1^*}=x_e
\]
is the unique finite minimum among the numbers
\[
y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Thus \(y\notin\widetilde H_x\). Hence \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) forces \(x=\infty^E\), proving the rank-\(r\) case. ∎

### Corollary 7.2: inclusion reversal

If
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q),
\]
then
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Indeed, if \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\), then Proposition 7.1 gives
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Since \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(q)\), we get
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
and Proposition 7.1 again gives
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Thus, in the finite simple modular case, the rule
\[
\Phi_M(L(p))=L(p^\dagger)
\]
defines an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces, not merely on valuated Plücker vectors, because Proposition 7.1 gives the intrinsic description
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\},
\]
which depends only on \(\widetilde L(p)\).

The affine cones are invariant under adding constants to finite coordinates, and
\[
\widetilde H_{x+\lambda\mathbf 1}=\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore the construction descends to projective tropical linear spaces. Rank-zero affine spaces projectivize to the empty space, which is included in the poset.

Finally, by the flat-quotient computation above,
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

---

## 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be any finite matroid whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given involution to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Since \(S\) is finite and simple, the construction above gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Using the simplification isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
This is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
and the transported polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence \(\Phi_M\) is the desired inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), extending the prescribed order-reversing involution on the embedded copy of \(\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\). ∎

## Theorem proved

Let \(M\) be a matroid on a **finite** ground set \(E\). Suppose that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), including the empty projective rank-zero space, ordered by inclusion. Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_M:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Here \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\), with every element of \(F\) made a loop.

---

### 1. Tropical and valuated-matroid preliminaries

Work over \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with min-plus conventions. If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), define its affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) by the tropical Plücker circuit equations
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p)
\iff
\text{for every }J\in \binom{E}{k+1},\ 
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(k=0\), we use the convention
\[
\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
The associated projective tropical linear space is
\[
L(p):=\bigl(\widetilde L(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
Thus a rank-zero valuated matroid gives the empty projective space.

An ordinary matroid \(N\) is identified with the constant valuated matroid
\[
N_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ is a basis of }N,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), and \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate valuated cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the standard finite-valuated-matroid cocircuit-generation theorem: every \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), and \(\widetilde L(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of the \(D_A^p\).

For \(u\in\mathbb T^R\), set
\[
H_u
=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^R:
\min_{i\in R}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

If all \(u_i=\infty\), then \(H_u=\mathbb T^R\). Otherwise, let \(\nu\) be the rank-one valuated matroid on \(R\) with \(\nu_{\{i\}}=u_i\). Then \(H_u=\widetilde L(\nu^*)\), because the only rank-\(|R|-1\) circuit equation for \(\nu^*\) is precisely
\[
\min_i(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
Hence \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex.

We need the following annihilator form of tropical duality.

**Lemma 1.1.** Let \(\mu\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\). Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]

**Proof.** If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde L(\mu)=\{\infty^R\}\), while \(\widetilde L(\mu^*)=\mathbb T^R\). Since \(\infty^R\in H_u\) for every \(u\), the statement is immediate.

Assume \(k>0\). The circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) say that for every
\[
J\in\binom{R}{|R|-k+1},
\]
the minimum
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(u_j+\mu^*_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Put \(A=R\setminus J\). Then \(|A|=k-1\), and
\[
\mu^*_{J\setminus j}=\mu_{A\cup j}.
\]
Thus this condition is exactly that \(u\) be tropically orthogonal to the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^\mu\):
\[
\min_{i\in R}\bigl(u_i+D_A^\mu(i)\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
So \(u\in\widetilde L(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu\).

Because \(\widetilde L(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those cocircuits, and because \(H_u\) is closed and tropically convex, this is equivalent to
\[
\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
\]
∎

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), the Bergman fan admits the following useful description.

**Lemma 1.2.** Let \(N\) be an ordinary finite matroid, and let \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\). Then
\[
w\in\widetilde L(N)
\]
iff every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\},\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb R,
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** The ordinary Bergman condition says that every circuit of \(N\) has no unique finite \(w\)-minimum.

Suppose some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\). Choose \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\) with
\[
w_e<\lambda\le w_c
\quad\text{for all }c\in C\setminus\{e\}.
\]
Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\), but \(e\notin\{w\ge\lambda\}\). Since \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\), this superlevel set is not a flat.

Conversely, suppose \(S=\{w\ge\lambda\}\) is not a flat. Choose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(S)\setminus S\). Then there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq S\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). Since \(w_e<\lambda\le w_c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{e\}\), the circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimum at \(e\). ∎

We also use the following containment criterion.

**Lemma 1.3.** Let \(M\) be an ordinary finite matroid, and let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E(M)\). For \(k=0\), one always has \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\). For \(k>0\),
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)
\]
iff for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\),
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** For a circuit \(C\) of \(M\), define \(u^C\in\mathbb T^E\) by
\[
u^C_i=
\begin{cases}
0,& i\in C,\\
\infty,& i\notin C.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}H_{u^C}.
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq H_{u^C}
\iff
u^C\in\widetilde L(p^*).
\]
The circuit equations for \(p^*\) are indexed by \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), and the equation corresponding to \(A\) is precisely
\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
\]
having no unique finite minimizer. ∎

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of nonloop parallel classes, let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\).

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

We claim that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

First, if \(\bar q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q\) to \(E\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is obtained from \(\bar q\) by repeatedly adding loops and zero-valued parallel copies. These two single-element operations preserve valuated matroids: a loop extension sets every basis containing the new element to \(\infty\), and a zero-parallel extension of \(e\) by \(f\) sets bases containing \(f\) but not \(e\) equal to the corresponding bases with \(e\), while bases containing both \(e\) and \(f\) have value \(\infty\). The tropical Plücker relations reduce immediately to the old ones after deleting loops or identifying parallel elements.

The corresponding tropical linear space is exactly
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Indeed, a loop extension forces the new coordinate to be \(\infty\). If \(f\) is added parallel to \(e\), then the equation on \(A\cup\{e,f\}\), where \(A\cup e\) is a finite basis if \(e\) is not a loop, forces \(x_e=x_f\); if \(e\) is already a loop, then both coordinates are forced to be \(\infty\). After imposing these equalities and loop coordinates, the remaining equations are precisely those of \(\bar q\).

Applying this to the ordinary matroid \(S\) gives
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)).
\]
Thus every valuated quotient of \(S\) extends to a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), then \(q\) descends uniquely to the rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

If \(e\in L_0\), then \(\{e\}\) is a circuit of \(M\). Lemma 1.3 implies that for every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\) with \(e\notin A\),
\[
q_{A\cup e}=\infty.
\]
So every loop of \(M\) remains a loop for \(q\).

If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit. Lemma 1.3 gives:
- if \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f};
  \]
- if \(e\in A\) and \(f\notin A\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup f}=\infty,
  \]
  and similarly with \(e\) and \(f\) exchanged.

Therefore no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same parallel class, and the value of a finite basis is independent of the choice of representatives.

Finally, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is dependent in \(M\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then in the condition of Lemma 1.3 for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(q_I\). Hence \(q_I=\infty\). Thus every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.

Consequently \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those of \(q\), and \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\). Since
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q))
\quad\text{and}\quad
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)),
\]
the inclusion \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\) implies
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq\widetilde L(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Therefore \(\Delta_*\) is a bijection on relative Dressians and preserves inclusions.

The flats of \(M\) are precisely inverse images of flats of \(S\). Explicitly,
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

If \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then the rank formula
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
\]
shows that \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite and simple.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is finite and simple of rank \(r\). If \(r=0\), then \(E=\varnothing\), and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) consists only of the rank-zero projective space. The identity map is the required involution.

Assume \(r>0\). The lattice \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Since \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution, it sends maximal chains to maximal chains, so
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
\[
\rho(X^\perp)+\rho(Y^\perp)
\ge
\rho(X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp)+\rho(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp).
\]
Using order reversal and rank reversal, this becomes
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular. Hence \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

---

### 4. Flat constancy for valuated quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), meaning
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
\]

First, if \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) is dependent in \(M\), then
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]
Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\), choose \(c\in C\), and put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). In Lemma 1.3, the only possibly finite term for \(C,A\) is \(p_I\), so \(p_I=\infty\).

Second, if \(I\) and \(I'\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), then
\[
p_I=p_{I'}.
\]
It suffices to check adjacent bases of \(M|F\). Suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},
\qquad
I'=A\cup\{b\},
\]
with \(|A|=k-1\). Since both sets are bases of \(F\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Lemma 1.3 applied to \(C,A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected, so the claim follows.

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F)
\]
to be the common value of \(p_I\) over all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\). This value may be \(\infty\).

We also need one more fact: every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I_0\). Since \(I_0\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\). Let \(B\) be any other \(M\)-basis. The basis graph of \(M\) is connected, so move from \(B_0\) to \(B\) by single exchanges. Suppose \(B'=B-b+e\) is adjacent to \(B\), and \(I\subseteq B\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. If \(b\notin I\), then \(I\subseteq B'\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus\{b\}\). Let \(C\) be the fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B\). Since \(B-b+e\) is a basis, \(b\in C\). In Lemma 1.3 for \(C,A\), the term \(p_I\) is finite, so there must be some
\[
c\in C\setminus A,\qquad c\neq b,
\]
with
\[
p_{A\cup c}<\infty.
\]
Since \(c\in B'\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\subseteq B'\) is a finite \(p\)-basis. Induction along the path in the basis graph proves the claim.

---

### 5. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(n\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the ordinary matroid \(M\). If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero. Hence those boundary cases are immediate.

Assume for the moment that
\[
0<k<r.
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) has at least one finite basis: choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
and choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Since \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-k=n\),
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I
<\infty.
\]

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

#### 5.1. Line-closed sets in modular matroids

**Lemma 5.1.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(A\) is a set of points of \(N\) such that whenever \(x,y\in A\), every point on the line \(x\vee y\) also lies in \(A\). Then \(A\) is the set of points of a flat.

**Proof.** Let
\[
F=\bigvee A.
\]
We show that every point below \(F\) lies in \(A\). Since \(N\) is finite, it suffices to prove this for a flat generated by finitely many independent points of \(A\). We argue by induction on the rank \(d\) of such a flat.

The claim is clear for \(d=0,1,2\), with \(d=2\) being exactly the line-closure assumption. Assume \(d\ge3\), and write
\[
F=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_d
\]
with \(a_i\in A\) independent. Let
\[
G=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{d-1}.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(A\). Let \(x\le F\) be a point. If \(x\le G\) or \(x=a_d\), then \(x\in A\). Otherwise, the line \(x\vee a_d\) is not contained in \(G\). By modularity inside the interval \([\hat0,F]\),
\[
\rho((x\vee a_d)\wedge G)
=
\rho(x\vee a_d)+\rho(G)-\rho(F)
=
2+(d-1)-d
=
1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=(x\vee a_d)\wedge G
\]
is a point of \(G\), hence \(y\in A\). Since \(x\) lies on the line \(a_d\vee y\), line-closure gives \(x\in A\). ∎

#### 5.2. A rank-three matching lemma

**Lemma 5.2.** Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let \(w\in\widetilde L(N)\), and let \(\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3,\ell_4\) be lines of \(N\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
w(\ell_i\wedge \ell_j),&\ell_i\neq \ell_j,\\
\infty,&\ell_i=\ell_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two of the \(\ell_i\) coincide, then one matching sum has an \(\infty\)-term and the other two matching sums are equal. So assume the \(\ell_i\) are distinct.

If \(w=\infty^E\), the result is immediate. Otherwise subtract the minimum finite value of \(w\). By Lemma 1.2, the superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank \(3\), every proper chain is contained in a flag
\[
P\le L,
\]
where \(P\) is a point and \(L\) is a line. Thus
\[
w=\alpha\mathbf 1_L+\beta\mathbf 1_P
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb T\) with \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), where \(\mathbf 1_F\) denotes the function that is \(1\) on \(F\) and \(0\) outside, interpreted additively.

First suppose no \(\ell_i\) equals \(L\). Put
\[
q_i=\ell_i\wedge L.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the point \(\ell_i\wedge\ell_j\) lies on \(L\) exactly when \(q_i=q_j\). If \(q_i=q_j\), its contribution is
\[
\gamma(q_i)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&q_i=P,\\
\alpha,&q_i\neq P.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_i\neq q_j\), the contribution is \(0\). The equality pattern of four labels has type
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
The corresponding matching-sum patterns are, up to permutation,
\[
(0,0,0),\quad (\gamma,0,0),\quad (\gamma+\delta,0,0),\quad
(\gamma,\gamma,\gamma),\quad (2\gamma,2\gamma,2\gamma),
\]
with \(\gamma,\delta\ge0\). None has a unique finite minimum.

Now suppose one line, say \(\ell_1\), equals \(L\). Put
\[
q_i=\ell_i\wedge L,\qquad i=2,3,4.
\]
Then
\[
[1i]=\gamma(q_i),
\]
and for \(i,j\in\{2,3,4\}\),
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\gamma(q_i),&q_i=q_j,\\
0,&q_i\neq q_j.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(q_2,q_3,q_4\) are all distinct, then the three matching sums are
\[
\gamma(q_2),\gamma(q_3),\gamma(q_4).
\]
At most one of these is \(\alpha+\beta\), and the others are \(\alpha\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two labels agree, the pattern is
\[
(\gamma,\gamma,\gamma+\delta),
\]
up to permutation. If all three agree, all three sums are equal. ∎

#### 5.3. A local dual Plücker lemma

**Lemma 5.3.** Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two \(H_i\)'s coincide, the claim follows as in Lemma 5.2. Assume they are distinct, and put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(M\) is modular, intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most \(1\), hence
\[
\rho(Z)\ge k-2.
\]
Also \(Z\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and for distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\),
\[
\rho(H_i\wedge H_j)=k.
\]
Thus
\[
\rho(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\rho(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three matching sums are equal.

Suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-2\). Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), choose an atom \(Q_i\) of this interval such that
\[
Q_i\le \bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j,
\qquad
Q_i\not\le H_i.
\]
This is possible because \(\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j\) strictly contains \(Z\), while its meet with \(H_i\) is \(Z\).

The atoms \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4\) are independent over \(Z\): indeed, \(Q_i\) is not contained in the join of the other three, because that join lies inside \(H_i\), whereas \(Q_i\not\le H_i\).

Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose
\[
h_i\in Q_i\setminus Z.
\]
For \(\{i,j,\ell,m\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z\vee Q_\ell\vee Q_m.
\]
Hence
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup h_\ell\cup h_m}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on
\[
A,h_1,h_2,h_3,h_4.
\]

Finally suppose \(\rho(Z)=k-1\). Let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(Y/Z),
\]
a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). Define a weight on the points \(P\) of \(N\) by
\[
w(P)=\bar p(Z\vee P).
\]
This is well-defined by flat constancy. The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\). It has value \(\infty\) on \(Z\), and its value is constant on each point of \(Y/Z\), equal to \(w(P)\). By Lemma 1.2, the superlevel sets of \(D_A^p\) are flats of \(M\); intersecting with \(Y\) and passing to \(Y/Z\) shows that the superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats of \(N\). Therefore
\[
w\in\widetilde L(N).
\]
The hyperplanes \(H_i\) descend to lines \(\ell_i=H_i/Z\) of \(N\), and
\[
[ij]=w(\ell_i\wedge \ell_j).
\]
The claim follows from Lemma 5.2. ∎

#### 5.4. A codimension-one polarity lemma

**Lemma 5.4.** Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1\), and let
\[
N=\operatorname{si}(M/X).
\]
The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde L(N).
\]

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.2, it suffices to show that every superlevel set
\[
\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is finite simple modular, Lemma 5.1 reduces this to showing line-closure.

Let \(P_1,P_2\) be points of \(N\) with
\[
u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda,
\]
and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\qquad
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\),
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
W=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\rho(W)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(W\), and choose
\[
h_i\in H_i\setminus W.
\]
In the contraction \(M/W\), the three elements \(h_1,h_2,h_3\) represent three distinct points on a rank-two line. Hence \(\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) is a circuit in \(M/W\). Equivalently,
\[
A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}
\]
is dependent in \(M\), while every
\[
A\cup\{h_i,h_j\}
\]
with \(i\neq j\) is independent. Therefore any circuit contained in \(A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) contains all three \(h_i\).

Applying Lemma 1.3 to such a circuit and to \(A\), we get that
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup h_1},p_{A\cup h_2},p_{A\cup h_3}\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
p_{A\cup h_i}=\bar p(H_i)=\bar p(P_i^\perp)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), it follows that \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence every superlevel set is line-closed, and therefore a flat of \(N\). ∎

#### 5.5. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient

We verify the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient-incidence relations.

If \(n<2\), there are no Plücker relations to check. Assume \(n\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is dependent in \(M\), then every \(n\)-set appearing in the Plücker relation is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is independent. If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.

Thus assume none of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\rho(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
S\cup\{e,f\}
\]
is independent iff \(H_e\neq H_f\), and in that case
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is dependent, and the corresponding value is \(\infty\). Hence the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly Lemma 5.3.

Now we check quotient-incidence. Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Suppose \(A\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup c\) is dependent. For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By Lemma 5.4, the function
\[
X\vee c\longmapsto \bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in
\[
\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X)).
\]

Suppose the minimum over \(c\in C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\). Then \(c_0\notin X\). In \(M/X\), the point \(X\vee c_0\) lies in the closure of the points \(X\vee c\) for \(c\in C\setminus\{c_0\}\). Hence in \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) there is a circuit containing \(X\vee c_0\) and contained in the set of points \(X\vee c\) with \(c\in C\setminus X\). On this circuit, the above function would have a unique finite minimum at \(X\vee c_0\), contradicting membership in the Bergman fan. Thus the quotient-incidence relations hold.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 6. Involutivity and flat-quotient compatibility

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We show
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\). If \(I\) is dependent in \(M\), then both sides are \(\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is an involution on valuated quotients.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\rho(F)\).

Let \(G\) be a rank-\(r-\rho(F)\) flat of \(M\). Then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
iff an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), iff
\[
F\vee G=E.
\]
Because \(M\) is modular and
\[
\rho(F)+\rho(G)=r,
\]
this is equivalent to
\[
F\wedge G=\hat0.
\]
Thus finite bases of \(p^F\) correspond exactly to lattice complements of \(F\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\), since
\[
(G\vee F)^\perp=G^\perp\wedge F^\perp,
\qquad
(G\wedge F)^\perp=G^\perp\vee F^\perp.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 7. Order reversal

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is a single ground-set element.

We first record the dual-basis identity. For every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

The containment “\(\le\)” is immediate: if \(i\in S\), then
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S)\le \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\}),
\]
so
\[
b_i^*
=
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S)^\perp.
\]

It remains to prove equality. We show that the \(b_i^*\) are independent. Suppose \(j\notin T\) and
\[
b_j^*\le \bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*.
\]
Applying \(\perp\) gives
\[
\left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp
\le
(b_j^*)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_j\}).
\]
But from the containment already proved,
\[
\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T)^\perp,
\]
so
\[
\left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp
\ge
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T).
\]
Since \(j\notin T\), we have \(b_j\in B\setminus T\), hence
\[
b_j\in \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T)
\le
\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_j\}),
\]
contradicting independence of \(B\). Therefore the \(b_i^*\) are independent. Both sides of the displayed identity have rank \(|S|\), so equality holds.

In particular,
\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x
=
\left\{
y\in\widetilde L(M):
\text{for every }M\text{-basis }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

We now prove the key annihilator characterization.

**Proposition 7.1.** Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x .
\]

**Proof.** First assume \(0<k<r\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has at least one finite basis.

For \(|T|=r-k\), the dual-basis identity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in T\}}
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin T\}}
=
\mu_B^*(T).
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Suppose first that
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger),
\qquad
y\in\widetilde L(p).
\]
Then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B).
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\). For every \(A\subseteq B\) with \(|A|=k-1\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining condition of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), we find that \(x|_{B^*}\) is tropically orthogonal to every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu_B\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*).
\]
Thus \(x\) satisfies every \(p^\dagger\)-equation supported inside every basis \(B^*\).

Now let
\[
J\subseteq E,
\qquad
|J|=r-k+1.
\]
We check the \(p^\dagger\)-equation for \(J\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since every \(M\)-basis is of the form \(B^*\) for another \(M\)-basis \(B\), this equation has already been checked.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and every \(J\setminus\{j\}\) is dependent, then every coefficient in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation is \(\infty\). Otherwise \(J\) has rank \(|J|-1\), hence contains a unique circuit \(C_0\). The only finite coefficients can occur for \(j\in C_0\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus\{j\})=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Thus the \(p^\dagger\)-equation for \(J\) is either trivial or reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) on the circuit \(C_0\). Hence
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

The boundary ranks are straightforward.

If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Since \(\infty^E\in\widetilde H_x\) for every \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\), the equivalence is true.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
We must show that
\[
\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x
\]
holds iff \(x=\infty^E\).

If \(x=\infty^E\), this is immediate. Conversely, suppose \(x\neq\infty^E\), and choose \(e\in E\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Let
\[
H=e^\perp,
\]
a hyperplane of \(M\). Choose an \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_1\})=H.
\]
Then \(b_1^*=e\). Choose \(A\gg0\), and define
\[
y_f=
\begin{cases}
A,& f\in H,\\
0,& f\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
The superlevel sets of \(y\) are flats, so \(y\in\widetilde L(M)\). For \(A\) sufficiently large,
\[
y_{b_1}+x_{b_1^*}=x_e
\]
is the unique finite minimum among the numbers
\[
y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Thus \(y\notin\widetilde H_x\). Hence \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) forces \(x=\infty^E\). ∎

It follows immediately that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses inclusions. Indeed, if
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(q),
\]
and \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\), then Proposition 7.1 gives
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by Proposition 7.1,
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Moreover, Proposition 7.1 gives the intrinsic description
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in\widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\}.
\]
Therefore the construction depends only on the tropical linear space \(\widetilde L(p)\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
the map
\[
L(p)\longmapsto L(p^\dagger)
\]
defines an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The construction is compatible with projectivization because adding a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf 1\) to \(x\) or \(y\) does not change the condition defining \(\widetilde H_x\). Rank-zero affine spaces projectivize to the empty space, which is included in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, by the flat-quotient computation above,
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\) of the finite simple modular matroid \(M\).

---

### 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be any finite matroid whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given involution to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\). If \(S\) has rank \(0\), then \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\), and hence \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), has only the rank-zero projective space, and the identity map is the desired involution. Otherwise, the simple case applies to \(S\), giving an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Using the simplification isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
This is an inclusion-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

For a flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). We already showed that
\[
\Delta_*\!\left(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The transported polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Therefore
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) is the required inclusion-reversing involution on the relative Dressian of the finite matroid \(M\), extending the prescribed order-reversing involution on the embedded flat lattice. ∎
